title: A connectome and analysis of the adult Drosophila central brain
abstract: The neural circuits responsible for animal behavior remain largely unknown. We summarize new methods and present the circuitry of a large fraction of the brain of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster. Improved methods include new procedures to prepare, image, align, segment, find synapses in, and proofread such large data sets. We define cell types, refine computational compartments, and provide an exhaustive atlas of cell examples and types, many of them novel. We provide detailed circuits consisting of neurons and their chemical synapses for most of the central brain. We make the data public and simplify access, reducing the effort needed to answer circuit questions, and provide procedures linking the neurons defined by our analysis with genetic reagents. Biologically, we examine distributions of connection strengths, neural motifs on different scales, electrical consequences of compartmentalization, and evidence that maximizing packing density is an important criterion in the evolution of the fly's brain.

ocar.opportunity: Despite the centrality of neural circuits to understanding animal behavior, comprehensive wiring diagrams of even small brains remained elusive due to the enormous scale of imaging and reconstruction required.
ocar.challenge: Mapping an entire adult Drosophila central brain required petabyte-scale electron microscopy data, new alignment and segmentation algorithms, synapse detection across billions of pixels, and extensive proofreading to achieve reliable connectivity.
ocar.action: The authors developed and applied improved procedures for sample preparation, FIB-SEM and transmission EM imaging, image alignment, automated segmentation, synapse detection, and collaborative proofreading to reconstruct the adult fly central brain. They defined cell types, refined compartment boundaries, and built an exhaustive atlas of neurons and their synaptic connections.
ocar.resolution: The resulting connectome covers the large majority of the Drosophila central brain with detailed chemical-synapse connectivity, novel cell types, and an openly accessible dataset. Analyses revealed distributions of connection strengths, neural motifs at multiple scales, electrical implications of compartmentalization, and evidence that packing density is an evolutionary constraint.
ocar.future_work: Functional validation of the identified circuits and extension of the approach to other species or brain regions with larger neuron counts remain key open challenges.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced a nearly complete map of every neuron and connection in the fruit fly brain, like drawing all the wiring in a very small but very complex electrical circuit. The researchers invented new tools to image, align, and trace billions of tiny images automatically, then shared everything publicly so any scientist can look up how any two brain cells are connected.

summaries.beginner: Neurons communicate through connections called synapses, and knowing exactly which neurons connect to which is called a connectome. This paper mapped almost the entire brain of the fruit fly Drosophila using an electron microscope to see individual synapses, then used computers to trace every neuron. The result is a public database letting scientists look up the wiring of fly brain circuits.
summaries.intermediate: Scheffer et al. reconstructed the adult Drosophila central brain connectome using serial-section electron microscopy, automated segmentation, and crowd-sourced proofreading. They identified and classified thousands of neuron types, defined computational compartments, and analyzed graph-theoretic properties of the resulting network including connection-strength distributions and motif frequencies. The dataset is publicly accessible via neuPrint and links neurons to genetic tools.
summaries.advanced: This landmark resource combined FIB-SEM and TEM imaging with flood-filling network segmentation and CATMAID-based proofreading to yield a near-complete adult Drosophila central brain connectome (~25,000 neurons, ~20 million synapses). Key caveats include sectioning artifacts, incomplete coverage of small-diameter neurites, and the single-specimen limitation inherent to EM connectomics. The dataset enables circuit-level hypothesis generation, but functional confirmation and cross-individual variability assessment remain outstanding challenges.

discussion_prompts: How do the authors validate that their automated segmentation and synapse detection are accurate enough for circuit-level conclusions, and what error rates are acceptable?

The paper identifies neural motifs at multiple scales — which motifs are most surprising compared to predictions from simpler nervous systems like C. elegans, and what do they imply about computation?
Given that the connectome represents a single individual fly, how should researchers account for inter-individual variability when drawing functional conclusions?

title: A genetic, genomic, and computational resource for exploring neural circuit function
abstract: The anatomy of many neural circuits is being characterized with increasing resolution, but their molecular properties remain mostly unknown. Here, we characterize gene expression patterns in distinct neural cell types of the Drosophila visual system using genetic lines to access individual cell types, the TAPIN-seq method to measure their transcriptomes, and a probabilistic method to interpret these measurements. We used these tools to build a resource of high-resolution transcriptomes for 100 driver lines covering 67 cell types, available at http://www.opticlobe.com. Combining these transcriptomes with recently reported connectomes helps characterize how information is transmitted and processed across a range of scales, from individual synapses to circuit pathways. We describe examples that include identifying neurotransmitters, including cases of apparent co-release, generating functional hypotheses based on receptor expression, as well as identifying strong commonalities between different cell types.

ocar.opportunity: Despite advances in mapping neural circuit anatomy via connectomics, the molecular identities and gene expression profiles of specific neuron types within those circuits were largely unknown, limiting mechanistic interpretation.
ocar.challenge: Linking circuit-level connectivity with molecular properties requires simultaneous knowledge of cell type identity and gene expression at single-cell resolution, which is technically demanding in complex tissues like the Drosophila visual system.
ocar.action: The authors characterized gene expression patterns in distinct neural cell types of the Drosophila visual system using intersectional genetic lines to access individual cell populations. They combined transcriptomic profiling, genetic tools, and connectomic data to build a multi-modal resource.
ocar.resolution: The study produced an integrated genetic, genomic, and computational resource linking cell type identity, transcriptomic signatures, and circuit anatomy in the Drosophila visual system. This resource enables researchers to probe molecular mechanisms underlying circuit function using existing genetic tools.
ocar.future_work: Extending this multi-modal approach to other Drosophila brain regions and integrating with whole-brain connectome data would further enhance its utility for mechanistic circuit studies.

summaries.beginner: Scientists studying the fly visual system wanted to know not just how neurons are connected, but also what genes each type of neuron turns on. This paper created a combined database linking cell identity, gene expression, and circuit wiring for fly visual neurons.
summaries.intermediate: This paper addresses the disconnect between connectomic anatomy and molecular biology by profiling gene expression in genetically isolated Drosophila visual system neuron types. Using cell-type-specific genetic lines, the authors generated transcriptomic data that can be cross-referenced with connectivity data from existing EM datasets, providing a multi-layered resource for the community.
summaries.advanced: The resource integrates intersectional genetic driver lines with single-cell or population-level transcriptomics to assign molecular identities to neurons already characterized at the circuit level in Drosophila visual system connectomes. Key caveats include the reliance on sparse genetic access (which may not achieve pure cell-type isolation) and the challenge of reconciling transcriptomic clusters with morphologically or connectivity-defined cell types. The computational resource component facilitates cross-referencing with connectomic databases like the hemibrain.

discussion_prompts: How were cell types defined in this study — morphologically, transcriptomically, or by genetic driver — and how do these definitions compare across the three modalities?
What new circuit hypotheses can be generated by combining gene expression profiles with known connectivity from the hemibrain or FAFB connectomes?
How should the community handle cases where transcriptomic clusters and morphology-defined cell types disagree, and what does that tell us about the nature of 'cell type'?

title: Connectomes across development reveal principles of brain maturation

ocar.opportunity: How the brain's wiring changes as an animal develops from birth to adulthood is largely unknown, because capturing the same nervous system at multiple developmental stages with synaptic resolution had never been achieved.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing multiple full connectomes of the same organism type at different developmental ages required generating, aligning, and proofreading several large EM datasets, then establishing correspondence between neurons across time points.
ocar.action: The authors reconstructed the complete connectome of Caenorhabditis elegans at eight larval time points from birth to adulthood using serial-section electron microscopy, then systematically compared wiring patterns, synapse numbers, and circuit motifs across development.
ocar.resolution: The study revealed that the overall connectivity structure is highly conserved across development, but individual synapses are added and removed in a structured fashion. Core circuits stabilize early while modulatory circuits refine over time, providing concrete principles of brain maturation.
ocar.future_work: Whether similar developmental rules apply to organisms with much larger and more plastic brains, such as vertebrates, remains an open and critical question.

plain_language_summary: This paper photographed the same type of worm's entire nervous system at eight different ages — from newborn to adult — to see how brain wiring changes as an animal grows up. Like comparing blueprints of a building at different construction stages, the researchers found that the main highways of the network form early, while finer connections are tuned later.

summaries.beginner: The roundworm C. elegans has only 302 neurons, making it the only animal whose complete nervous system has been repeatedly mapped. This study mapped the worm's entire connectome eight times at different ages, revealing which connections form early in development and which are added or removed later. It gives the first timeline of how a complete nervous system is wired up.
summaries.intermediate: Witvliet et al. generated eight serial-section EM connectomes of C. elegans spanning larval stages L1 through adult, enabling the first developmental time series of a complete nervous system at synaptic resolution. They found that the overall graph structure is highly stereotyped and largely established early, with synaptic weight changes and addition of modulatory connections accounting for most developmental remodeling. Modularity analysis revealed that sensory circuits stabilize before motor output circuits.
summaries.advanced: This work represents the most comprehensive developmental connectome series to date, with eight full C. elegans reconstructions enabling statistical comparison of synapse addition, elimination, and weight changes across time. Key findings include a conserved backbone of strong synaptic connections present from L1 and a progressive increase in recurrent connectivity. Limitations include the invariant cell-number nature of C. elegans, which may limit generalizability, and the challenge of attributing behavioral changes to specific synaptic alterations without causal manipulations.

discussion_prompts: The paper reports that core circuits are established early while modulatory circuits refine over development — how does this map onto known critical periods for learning and plasticity in vertebrates?
How would you design experiments to causally test whether the late-added synaptic connections identified here are necessary for adult-specific behaviors in C. elegans?
What are the principal limitations of using C. elegans as a model for developmental connectomics, given its invariant cell lineage and small neuron count?

title: Collective dynamics of ‘small-world’ networks

ocar.opportunity: Many real-world networks — from power grids to social networks to nervous systems — appeared to fall between the extremes of perfectly regular lattices and purely random graphs, yet no mathematical framework captured this intermediate regime.
ocar.challenge: Characterizing networks required metrics that could simultaneously capture local clustering (neighbors of a node are likely connected) and short global path lengths, properties that prior models could not reproduce together.
ocar.action: Watts and Strogatz introduced the 'small-world' network model by starting with a regular ring lattice and randomly rewiring a small fraction of edges. They measured clustering coefficient and average path length as functions of rewiring probability and compared these to real networks from neuroscience, power engineering, and social science.
ocar.resolution: They demonstrated that a tiny amount of random rewiring dramatically reduces average path length while preserving high local clustering, creating a 'small-world' regime. Real networks including the C. elegans neural network fell into this regime, suggesting a universal organizing principle with implications for efficient signal propagation.
ocar.future_work: Understanding how small-world topology arises from developmental or evolutionary constraints, and its causal role in neural computation, remains an active research area.

plain_language_summary: This famous paper asked: why can you reach almost anyone on Earth through six degrees of separation, even though most people only know their local neighbors? The authors showed that adding just a few long-range shortcuts to an otherwise local network creates a 'small world' — a network that is both tightly clustered and easy to traverse — and found the same pattern in the wiring of C. elegans neurons.

summaries.beginner: Most networks — including the brain — are not random but also not perfectly regular. Watts and Strogatz showed that a small fraction of random long-range connections can dramatically shrink the average distance between any two nodes while keeping local structure intact. The C. elegans nervous system was one of the real-world examples that fit this 'small-world' pattern.
summaries.intermediate: Watts and Strogatz (1998) introduced the small-world network model, showing that interpolating between regular and random graphs via random edge rewiring produces a regime with simultaneously high clustering coefficient C and low characteristic path length L. They validated the model against three empirical networks, including the C. elegans connectome, finding all three exhibit C >> C_random and L ≈ L_random. This work provided the first formal framework for analyzing connectome topology in graph-theoretic terms.
summaries.advanced: The Watts–Strogatz model is foundational for connectome graph analysis; its two summary statistics (clustering coefficient, path length) remain standard benchmarks for comparing neural networks across species. Caveats include the model's assumption of equal degree for all nodes (contrast with scale-free distributions in many biological networks) and the difficulty of separating topological properties from circuit function. In connectomics, small-world metrics have been computed for fly, worm, and mammalian cortical networks, though comparisons are sensitive to thresholding choices and edge-weight definitions.

discussion_prompts: The C. elegans connectome was used as an empirical example of a small-world network in 1998 — how has our understanding changed now that we have whole-brain connectomes for larger organisms?
How sensitive are small-world metrics (clustering coefficient, path length) to the choice of synapse-weight threshold when applied to weighted connectomes like the Drosophila hemibrain?
Does small-world topology in a connectome imply anything specific about the computational properties or robustness of that circuit, or is it purely a descriptive finding?

title: The connectome of an insect brain
abstract: Brains contain networks of interconnected neurons and so knowing the network architecture is essential for understanding brain function. We therefore mapped the synaptic-resolution connectome of an entire insect brain (Drosophila larva) with rich behavior, including learning, value computation, and action selection, comprising 3016 neurons and 548,000 synapses. We characterized neuron types, hubs, feedforward and feedback pathways, as well as cross-hemisphere and brain-nerve cord interactions. We found pervasive multisensory and interhemispheric integration, highly recurrent architecture, abundant feedback from descending neurons, and multiple novel circuit motifs. The brain's most recurrent circuits comprised the input and output neurons of the learning center. Some structural features, including multilayer shortcuts and nested recurrent loops, resembled state-of-the-art deep learning architectures. The identified brain architecture provides a basis for future experimental and theoretical studies of neural circuits.

ocar.opportunity: Understanding how a brain capable of complex behavior — including learning, value computation, and action selection — is wired requires a complete synaptic-resolution connectome of an entire brain, which had not been achieved for any organism with such rich behavioral repertoire.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing 3016 neurons and 548,000 synapses from the Drosophila larva required dense serial-section EM reconstruction and systematic annotation of neuron types, pathways, and inter-hemispheric interactions at a scale previously unachieved for an entire insect brain.
ocar.action: The authors generated a complete synaptic connectome of the Drosophila larva central brain using serial-section EM and manual/semi-automated reconstruction, annotated all neurons into types and functional categories, and performed graph-theoretic analysis of feedforward/feedback pathways, hubs, multisensory integration, and learning circuit architecture.
ocar.resolution: The connectome revealed pervasive multisensory and interhemispheric integration, highly recurrent architecture, abundant feedback from descending neurons, and multiple novel circuit motifs. The most recurrent circuits comprised input and output neurons of the learning center (mushroom body), and structural features including multilayer shortcuts resembled deep learning architectures.
ocar.future_work: Connecting specific circuit motifs to behavioral outputs through genetic manipulation and linking larval connectivity to adult circuit organization remain major open questions.

plain_language_summary: Imagine having a complete instruction manual for how all the neurons in a young fruit fly's brain are wired together. This paper produced exactly that for the Drosophila larva — mapping every one of its 3,016 neurons and half a million connections. The analysis found that the brain's structure shares surprising similarities with modern artificial neural networks, including shortcuts that allow information to flow across many processing layers at once.

summaries.beginner: The Drosophila larva can learn, make decisions, and integrate information from multiple senses despite having only 3,016 neurons. This study mapped every neuron and synaptic connection in its entire brain, creating a complete circuit diagram. The analysis revealed that the fly larva brain is highly interconnected with many feedback loops, especially around its learning center.
summaries.intermediate: Winding et al. reconstructed the complete Drosophila larva connectome (3016 neurons, 548,000 synapses) and performed comprehensive graph analysis including community detection, hub identification, and motif enumeration. Key findings include a highly recurrent architecture with abundant descending feedback, pervasive multisensory convergence, and mushroom body circuits forming the most recurrent subgraph. The structural parallels to deep network architectures offer a potential bridge between biological and artificial neural networks.
summaries.advanced: This first whole-brain insect connectome enables systematic comparison of larval and adult fly circuitry and cross-species connectome analysis. The authors employed Leiden community detection and spectral methods to characterize mesoscale structure, identifying hub neurons and motifs. Caveats include single-animal reconstruction, manual annotation subjectivity for cell-type assignment, and the caveat that larval circuits are reorganized during metamorphosis, limiting direct adult comparisons. The identified structural motifs (multi-layer shortcuts, nested recurrence) invite computational modeling to test their functional implications.

discussion_prompts: The paper finds that the most recurrent circuits cluster around the mushroom body learning center — what experiments would test whether this recurrence is necessary for associative learning in the larva?
The authors note structural parallels between the larval connectome and deep learning architectures — what are the risks of drawing functional analogies from structural similarity alone?
How does the complete larval connectome constrain models of multi-sensory integration and action selection, compared to what could be inferred from partial circuit reconstructions?

title: Neuronal wiring diagram of an adult brain
abstract: Connections between neurons can be mapped by acquiring and analysing electron microscopic brain images. In recent years, this approach has been applied to chunks of brains to reconstruct local connectivity maps that are highly informative<sup>1-6</sup>, but nevertheless inadequate for understanding brain function more globally. Here we present a neuronal wiring diagram of a whole brain containing 5 × 10<sup>7</sup> chemical synapses<sup>7</sup> between 139,255 neurons reconstructed from an adult female Drosophila melanogaster<sup>8,9</sup>. The resource also incorporates annotations of cell classes and types, nerves, hemilineages and predictions of neurotransmitter identities<sup>10-12</sup>. Data products are available for download, programmatic access and interactive browsing and have been made interoperable with other fly data resources. We derive a projectome-a map of projections between regions-from the connectome and report on tracing of synaptic pathways and the analysis of information flow from inputs (sensory and ascending neurons) to outputs (motor, endocrine and descending neurons) across both hemispheres and between the central brain and the optic lobes. Tracing from a subset of photoreceptors to descending motor pathways illustrates how structure can uncover putative circuit mechanisms underlying sensorimotor behaviours. The technologies and open ecosystem reported here set the stage for future large-scale connectome projects in other species.

ocar.opportunity: Local connectome reconstructions of brain sub-volumes provided rich circuit detail but were insufficient for understanding global information flow, sensorimotor integration, and whole-brain computation in any organism.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing 139,255 neurons and 50 million synapses from a complete adult fly brain required new scalable segmentation pipelines, extensive distributed proofreading, and interoperable data infrastructure capable of handling petabyte-scale EM volumes.
ocar.action: The authors generated a complete adult female Drosophila melanogaster whole-brain connectome using FIB-SEM imaging combined with flood-filling network segmentation and community proofreading. They annotated cell classes, types, hemilineages, and predicted neurotransmitter identities for all neurons, then analyzed projectome structure and information flow from sensory inputs to motor outputs.
ocar.resolution: The FlyWire connectome provides a neuronal wiring diagram with 139,255 neurons, 50 million synapses, and comprehensive cell-type annotations, publicly accessible for download and interactive browsing. Projectome analysis and pathway tracing from photoreceptors to descending motor neurons demonstrate how the connectome enables mechanistic hypotheses about sensorimotor circuits.
ocar.future_work: Extending this approach to other Drosophila individuals to assess inter-individual variability, and applying similar methods to other species, are natural next steps.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced the first complete wiring diagram of an entire adult fruit fly brain, mapping over 139,000 neurons and 50 million connections. Think of it as producing a full circuit schematic for a tiny but sophisticated computer — one that processes sight, smell, and touch, then decides how to move. The data are public and the team built tools so any researcher can browse and download the circuit of interest.

summaries.beginner: Neurons in a brain talk to each other through connections called synapses. This paper used an electron microscope to image an entire adult fly brain, then used machine learning to identify every neuron and trace every connection — over 50 million of them. The resulting map (the FlyWire connectome) is freely available, letting scientists look up how sensory information travels all the way to movement commands.
summaries.intermediate: Dorkenwald et al. present the FlyWire whole-brain connectome of adult D. melanogaster, achieving 139,255 reconstructed neurons and ~5×10^7 chemical synapses via flood-filling network segmentation on FIB-SEM data with distributed community proofreading. The dataset includes predicted neurotransmitter identities, hemilineage annotations, and a derived projectome. Pathway tracing from photoreceptors through the visual system to descending motor neurons illustrates the resource's utility for mechanistic circuit analysis.
summaries.advanced: FlyWire represents a qualitative advance over the hemibrain dataset (Scheffer et al. 2020) by covering both hemispheres and connecting central brain to optic lobes. The segmentation pipeline used flood-filling networks with iterative agglomeration; proofreading quality is quantified by false-merge and split rates. Key limitations include single-animal reconstruction, potential segmentation errors in dense neuropil, and the ongoing challenge of matching morphologically defined cell types to genetic lines. The open-ecosystem approach with API access, CAVE backend, and interoperability with other fly resources (neuPrint, VFB) sets a standard for future large-scale connectomics.

discussion_prompts: FlyWire uses community proofreading by many annotators — what quality-control measures were employed, and how do residual errors propagate into circuit-level analyses?
The paper derives a 'projectome' from the connectome — what additional information does this region-to-region connectivity matrix provide compared to individual neuron-level wiring?
How would you use the FlyWire connectome to generate and test a specific hypothesis about a sensorimotor behavior in Drosophila?

title: Autophagy-Independent Functions of the Autophagy Machinery

ocar.opportunity: Autophagy — the cellular self-digestion pathway — was assumed to mediate its physiological roles exclusively through the degradation of cytoplasmic cargo, leaving unexplained a range of cellular effects observed when autophagy flux was absent.
ocar.challenge: Distinguishing between effects caused by the absence of autophagic degradation versus scaffold or signaling functions of autophagy proteins required careful genetic and biochemical dissection in contexts where canonical flux is blocked.
ocar.action: Galluzzi and Green reviewed and synthesized evidence across multiple model systems for autophagy-independent functions of core autophagy machinery proteins (ATG proteins), cataloguing their roles in membrane dynamics, inflammation, cell death, and other processes distinct from cargo degradation.
ocar.resolution: The review established that many ATG proteins participate in cellular processes — including LC3-associated phagocytosis, unconventional secretion, and regulation of apoptosis — through mechanisms entirely independent of autophagic flux, reframing autophagy machinery as multifunctional regulators.
ocar.future_work: Determining which autophagy-independent functions are physiologically dominant in specific disease contexts and whether they can be selectively targeted therapeutically remains an open challenge.

plain_language_summary: Cells have a recycling system called autophagy that breaks down unwanted material. This review revealed that the proteins running this recycling machine do many other jobs as well — including regulating inflammation and cell death — even when no recycling is happening. It is like discovering that garbage trucks also deliver mail and repair roads on their routes.

summaries.beginner: Autophagy is a process where cells break down and recycle their own damaged parts. This review showed that the proteins involved in autophagy also perform many other functions in the cell that have nothing to do with recycling. Understanding these extra functions is important because autophagy proteins are targets for treating cancer and neurodegeneration.
summaries.intermediate: This review by Galluzzi and Green comprehensively catalogues non-canonical roles of ATG proteins outside autophagic flux. These include LC3-associated phagocytosis (LAP), LC3-associated endocytosis (LANDO), secretory autophagy, and direct regulatory interactions with apoptotic and inflammatory signaling cascades. The distinction between flux-dependent and flux-independent functions has important implications for interpreting phenotypes in autophagy knockout studies.
summaries.advanced: The review systematically deconstructs the assumption that ATG protein phenotypes reflect autophagic degradation capacity. Functions covered include non-degradative roles of ATG proteins in immune signaling (e.g., ATG5–ATG12 activating IFN-I responses), regulation of apoptosis by Beclin-1 via Bcl-2 family interactions, and LAP-dependent phagosome maturation. Key experimental caveats include the difficulty of separating flux-dependent and flux-independent phenotypes in genetic models, and the context-dependence of these alternative functions across cell types and tissues. Relevance to neuroscience includes autophagy's role in synaptic pruning and aggregate clearance in neurodegeneration.

discussion_prompts: How can researchers experimentally distinguish between a phenotype caused by loss of autophagic flux versus loss of an autophagy-independent function of an ATG protein?
The review highlights autophagy-independent roles in inflammation — how might these affect interpretation of autophagy studies in neuroinflammatory contexts?
Which autophagy-independent functions described here are most relevant to neurodegeneration research, and what therapeutic implications do they have?

title: The structure of the nervous system of the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans
abstract: The structure and connectivity of the nervous system of the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans has been deduced from reconstructions of electron micrographs of serial sections. The hermaphrodite nervous system has a total complement of 302 neurons, which are arranged in an essentially invariant structure. Neurons with similar morphologies and connectivities have been grouped together into classes; there are 118 such classes. Neurons have simple morphologies with few, if any, branches. Processes from neurons run in defined positions within bundles of parallel processes, synaptic connections being made en passant. Process bundles are arranged longitudinally and circumferentially and are often adjacent to ridges of hypodermis. Neurons are generally highly locally connected, making synaptic connections with many of their neighbours. Muscle cells have arms that run out to process bundles containing motoneuron axons. Here they receive their synaptic input in defined regions along the surface of the bundles, where motoneuron axons reside. Most of the morphologically identifiable synaptic connections in a typical animal are described. These consist of about 5000 chemical synapses, 2000 neuromuscular junctions and 600 gap junctions.

ocar.opportunity: No complete map of any animal nervous system existed at the level of individual neurons and synapses, making it impossible to reason about how neural circuits generate behavior from first principles.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing an entire nervous system required imaging thousands of serial ultrathin sections by transmission electron microscopy, then manually tracing every neuronal process and synapse — a task of unprecedented scale that took over a decade of painstaking work.
ocar.action: White, Southgate, Thomson, and Brenner reconstructed the complete nervous system of C. elegans hermaphrodites from serial-section EM, tracing all 302 neurons and cataloguing approximately 5,000 chemical synapses, 2,000 neuromuscular junctions, and 600 gap junctions.
ocar.resolution: The study produced the first and (for decades) only complete connectome of any organism, revealing an essentially invariant wiring pattern across individuals, a modular organization of neuron classes, and stereotyped synaptic connectivity that provided the foundation for all subsequent C. elegans circuit analysis.
ocar.future_work: Whether the invariant wiring of C. elegans holds for organisms with larger, more plastic nervous systems, and how the connectome maps to the animal's full behavioral repertoire, remain enduring questions.

plain_language_summary: In a project that took over a decade, scientists photographed thin slices of the tiny worm C. elegans under an electron microscope and manually traced every single neuron and connection. With only 302 neurons, it was the first animal to have its complete nervous system wired map made — a landmark achievement that all modern connectomics is built on.

summaries.beginner: The roundworm C. elegans has exactly 302 neurons, making its brain small enough to map completely. This 1986 paper did exactly that using an electron microscope, tracing every neuron and every connection by hand. It was the first complete nervous-system wiring diagram ever created and launched the field of connectomics.
summaries.intermediate: White et al. (1986) established the conceptual and methodological foundation for connectomics by reconstructing the complete C. elegans nervous system from serial TEM sections. The 302-neuron hermaphrodite connectome revealed 118 neuron classes with stereotyped morphology and connectivity, an invariant wiring pattern across individuals, and a modular organization. This work defined the paradigm of using structural connectivity to understand neural circuit function.
summaries.advanced: This foundational work required manual reconstruction of thousands of serial ultrathin sections, a strategy that is computationally automated in modern connectomics. The C. elegans connectome revealed key principles: synaptic connectivity is largely invariant across individuals (unlike vertebrates), neurons make en passant synapses along longitudinal process bundles, and gap junctions constitute a significant fraction of connectivity (~10%). Limitations of the original reconstruction — since revised with additional synapses identified in later re-analysis — illustrate that even 'complete' connectomes require iterative proofreading.

discussion_prompts: The C. elegans connectome was largely invariant across individuals — how does this compare to inter-individual variability observed in more recently reconstructed connectomes, and what does it imply for the relationship between structure and behavior?
How did the manual reconstruction methods used in 1986 compare in accuracy to modern automated segmentation, and what have subsequent re-analyses of the C. elegans connectome corrected?
Given that we now have the complete C. elegans connectome and can genetically manipulate individual neurons, are there still unanswered questions about how its circuits generate behavior?

title: Random Graphs

ocar.opportunity: The mathematical properties of randomly wired networks were poorly characterized, yet random graph theory provides the null models needed to determine whether observed connectivity patterns in real networks — including neural circuits — are non-random and potentially functional.
ocar.challenge: Rigorously deriving the structural properties of random graphs as a function of edge probability required new combinatorial techniques and probabilistic arguments that were not available in earlier graph theory.
ocar.action: Bollobás systematically developed the mathematical theory of random graphs (Erdős–Rényi model and extensions), deriving exact and asymptotic results on properties such as connectivity, component structure, diameter, and subgraph frequencies as functions of edge probability.
ocar.resolution: The book established a comprehensive mathematical framework for random graphs, proving phase-transition phenomena (e.g., the giant component emergence), and providing the baseline null distributions against which real-world network topology — including connectomes — is compared.
ocar.future_work: Extending random graph theory to weighted, directed, and hierarchically structured networks that better match biological connectomes remains an ongoing mathematical challenge.

plain_language_summary: This mathematics textbook laid the groundwork for understanding networks built by randomly connecting nodes. In neuroscience, random graph theory is indispensable as the baseline comparison: when we say a brain network has 'more clustering than expected,' the expected value comes from random graph models. Without this mathematical foundation, we could not distinguish meaningful wiring patterns from statistical noise.

summaries.beginner: A random graph is a network where connections between nodes are made by chance. This book worked out the math for how such networks behave — for example, when does a random network become fully connected? These results give neuroscientists a baseline for comparison: if a real brain network looks different from a random one, something biologically meaningful might explain the difference.
summaries.intermediate: Bollobás's 'Random Graphs' provides the rigorous mathematical foundations for Erdős–Rényi random graphs and their generalizations, including proofs of threshold phenomena for connectivity, Hamiltonian cycles, and subgraph containment. In connectomics, these results underpin statistical tests for non-random features such as overrepresented motifs, high clustering, and community structure, all of which require comparison to appropriately matched random null models.
summaries.advanced: This text is the canonical reference for random graph theory and provides the theoretical machinery for connectome graph analysis. Key results include the sharp threshold for graph connectivity at p = ln(n)/n and the emergence of a giant component at p = 1/n. In connectomics applications, the Erdős–Rényi model is typically insufficient as a null model (it does not preserve degree sequence), motivating configuration model or degree-preserving randomization. Researchers should be aware that graph statistics computed on connectomes (e.g., clustering coefficient, path length) must be compared to null models that control for degree distribution to avoid spurious conclusions.

discussion_prompts: What random graph null model is most appropriate when testing whether a connectome has non-random clustering or motif frequencies, and why does the choice of null model matter?
The Erdős–Rényi model assumes all edges are equally probable — how does this assumption break down for neural connectivity, and what alternative models better capture biological constraints?
Random graph theory predicts a sharp transition from disconnected to connected as edge density increases — is there an analogous transition in connectome development, and what would it look like?

title: Molecular mechanisms of cell death: recommendations of the Nomenclature Committee on Cell Death 2018

ocar.opportunity: The proliferating diversity of cell death modalities discovered in recent decades created terminological confusion that impeded communication across research communities and made it difficult to design and interpret experiments about cellular demise.
ocar.challenge: Defining consistent, mechanistically grounded nomenclature for cell death pathways required integrating evidence from genetics, biochemistry, and cell biology across model systems, while distinguishing regulated from accidental death and separating overlapping pathway activities.
ocar.action: An international Nomenclature Committee on Cell Death reviewed and updated definitions and criteria for all recognized cell death modalities, including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, autophagy-dependent cell death, and others, establishing consensus operational definitions based on molecular mechanisms.
ocar.resolution: The 2018 recommendations established a unified nomenclature with mechanistic criteria distinguishing over a dozen regulated cell death modalities, providing a reference framework that enables reproducible reporting and cross-study comparison in cell biology and disease research.
ocar.future_work: Newly discovered modalities continue to emerge, and determining the in vivo prevalence and pathological relevance of each pathway in human disease requires continued investigation.

plain_language_summary: Cells can die in many different ways — some orderly and programmed, others chaotic and damaging. Scientists discovered so many different cell death pathways that the names and definitions became confusing. This paper brought together an international committee to agree on clear, mechanism-based definitions for all the major ways cells can die, giving researchers a shared vocabulary.

summaries.beginner: Cells in our bodies are constantly dying and being replaced. Scientists have discovered many different ways cells can die — each with different molecular machinery and effects on surrounding tissue. This consensus paper standardized the names and definitions for these pathways so researchers worldwide use the same terminology, which is essential for comparing results across labs.
summaries.intermediate: This Nomenclature Committee recommendation provides operational definitions for regulated cell death (RCD) modalities including intrinsic and extrinsic apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, parthanatos, entotic cell death, NETosis, lysosome-dependent cell death, autophagy-dependent cell death, immunogenic cell death, cellular senescence, and mitotic catastrophe. Each modality is defined by molecular markers and genetic/pharmacological criteria rather than morphology alone. Relevance to neuroscience includes apoptotic and necroptotic pathways in neurodegeneration and the role of pyroptosis in neuroinflammation.
summaries.advanced: The 2018 NCCD recommendations represent the third major update to cell death nomenclature and reflect the field's shift from morphological to mechanistic classification. Key nuances include the distinction between RCD and accidental cell death, the possibility of pathway switching under genetic or pharmacological perturbation, and the recognition that cell death modalities can be immunogenic or tolerogenic with distinct downstream inflammatory consequences. In connectomics-adjacent neuroscience, these distinctions matter for interpreting neuronal loss in disease models, synaptic pruning mechanisms, and the role of microglia-mediated phagocytosis in circuit refinement.

discussion_prompts: How do the mechanistic criteria established in this nomenclature change the interpretation of earlier cell death studies that relied solely on morphological criteria?
Several cell death modalities (e.g., autophagy-dependent cell death, apoptosis) share molecular components — how can researchers cleanly assign a cell death event to one modality?

In the context of neurodegeneration, which regulated cell death pathways are most therapeutically actionable based on current evidence, and what does this nomenclature framework contribute to that question?

title: A Connectome of the Adult Drosophila Central Brain
abstract: Abstract The neural circuits responsible for behavior remain largely unknown. Previous efforts have reconstructed the complete circuits of small animals, with hundreds of neurons, and selected circuits for larger animals. Here we (the FlyEM project at Janelia and collaborators at Google) summarize new methods and present the complete circuitry of a large fraction of the brain of a much more complex animal, the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster . Improved methods include new procedures to prepare, image, align, segment, find synapses, and proofread such large data sets; new methods that define cell types based on connectivity in addition to morphology; and new methods to simplify access to a large and evolving data set. From the resulting data we derive a better definition of computational compartments and their connections; an exhaustive atlas of cell examples and types, many of them novel; detailed circuits for most of the central brain; and exploration of the statistics and structure of different brain compartments, and the brain as a whole. We make the data public, with a web site and resources specifically designed to make it easy to explore, for all levels of expertise from the expert to the merely curious. The public availability of these data, and the simplified means to access it, dramatically reduces the effort needed to answer typical circuit questions, such as the identity of upstream and downstream neural partners, the circuitry of brain regions, and to link the neurons defined by our analysis with genetic reagents that can be used to study their functions. Note: In the next few weeks, we will release a series of papers with more involved discussions. One paper will detail the hemibrain reconstruction with more extensive analysis and interpretation made possible by this dense connectome. Another paper will explore the central complex, a brain region involved in navigation, motor control, and sleep. A final paper will present insights from the mushroom body, a center of multimodal associative learning in the fly brain.

ocar.opportunity: Despite the Drosophila brain's status as a premier model for neural circuit analysis, no complete synaptic wiring diagram of the adult central brain existed, preventing systematic understanding of how the ~100,000-neuron brain is organized at the circuit level.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing the entire adult Drosophila central brain required automated segmentation of a dense, multi-terabyte EM volume and subsequent expert proofreading of hundreds of thousands of neurons — a scale that had never been attempted with the required accuracy.
ocar.action: The FlyEM team (Janelia Research Campus) acquired a new FIB-SEM volume of half the adult Drosophila central brain (the 'hemibrain'), applied automated segmentation using FFNs, and performed systematic proofreading to generate a heavily annotated connectome of ~25,000 neurons with ~10 million synapses.
ocar.resolution: The hemibrain connectome provided the first comprehensive circuit-level description of the adult Drosophila central brain, including the mushroom body, central complex, lateral horn, and optic lobe entry circuits. It revealed new cell types, circuit motifs, and provided a freely accessible resource that has underpinned dozens of subsequent circuit analyses.
ocar.future_work: Future work should complete the male and female Drosophila brains as full hemispheres, improve reconstruction completeness for small-diameter axons, and integrate the structural connectome with trans-synaptic and transcriptomic cell-type annotations.

summaries.beginner: Scientists built the most complete wiring diagram of an adult fruit fly's brain ever created. By imaging half the brain with a special microscope and having computers plus human experts trace ~25,000 nerve cells and their 10 million connection points, they created a free resource that the whole scientific community can use.
summaries.intermediate: The hemibrain connectome uses isotropic FIB-SEM imaging of half the adult Drosophila central brain, automated FFN-based segmentation, and systematic proofreading to produce a ~25,000-neuron, ~10 million-synapse dataset. Freely released with NeuPrint access tools, it has enabled systematic analysis of mushroom body, central complex, and lateral horn circuits, establishing a new standard for EM connectomics.
summaries.advanced: The hemibrain dataset represents the largest isotropic EM connectome at time of publication, using 8 nm voxel FIB-SEM, FFN segmentation, and a proofreading pipeline that achieved >97% synapse recall. The ~25,000 reconstructed neurons cover ~30% of the central brain by volume. Key limitations include incomplete coverage of small processes (<80 nm diameter), single animal/sex (female), and the hemibrain scope (not full brain or VNC). The NeuPrint graph database and API provide powerful query tools but require understanding of the data's incompleteness for valid circuit analysis.

discussion_prompts: What circuit motifs revealed by the hemibrain connectome were unexpected or contradicted previous physiological or genetic models, and how have these findings been validated?
How does the incompleteness of small-process reconstruction in the hemibrain affect the reliability of connectivity analyses, particularly for neurons with thin axons?
How has the open release of the hemibrain dataset with NeuPrint tools changed the pace and scope of Drosophila circuit neuroscience, and what follow-up analyses have been most productive?

title: Fiji: an open-source platform for biological-image analysis

ocar.opportunity: Biological image analysis required diverse, specialized algorithms — from segmentation to registration to measurement — but no single free, open-source platform integrated them with a consistent interface accessible to biologists without programming expertise.
ocar.challenge: Building a unified platform required integrating hundreds of disparate ImageJ plugins, establishing scripting and macro capabilities, ensuring reproducibility across operating systems, and maintaining active community development while keeping the tool accessible to non-programmers.
ocar.action: Schindelin et al. developed Fiji (Fiji Is Just ImageJ), an open-source distribution of ImageJ that bundles an updatable ecosystem of community-contributed plugins with a consistent scripting interface, automated dependency management, and standardized documentation.
ocar.resolution: Fiji became the de facto standard for bioimage analysis, enabling reproducible workflows in electron microscopy, fluorescence imaging, and connectomics through plugins for image registration, segmentation, 3D visualization, and neuron tracing. Its TrakEM2 plugin became central to connectomics reconstruction pipelines.
ocar.future_work: Scaling Fiji workflows to petabyte EM volumes and integrating deep-learning inference natively remain active areas of development.

plain_language_summary: Biological microscopy produces enormous numbers of images, and analyzing them requires many different specialized tools. Fiji is a free software package that bundles hundreds of image-analysis tools together with a point-and-click interface and a scripting system, so biologists can process and measure images without writing complex code from scratch. It became standard in labs worldwide, including those doing connectomics.

summaries.beginner: When scientists take microscope images, they need software to measure, align, and analyze them. Fiji is a free program that bundles many image analysis tools together into one place. It is used in nearly every biology lab, including those reconstructing brain circuits from electron microscopy images.
summaries.intermediate: Fiji is an ImageJ distribution that packages a curated ecosystem of plugins, scripting languages (Jython, BeanShell, ImageJ macro), and an update site framework. For connectomics, key plugins include TrakEM2 for large-scale image data management and neuron annotation, BigDataViewer for petabyte-scale data navigation, and Trainable Weka Segmentation for semi-automated structure segmentation. The paper established a community-driven development model that has since been replicated by many open neuroscience tools.
summaries.advanced: Fiji's architecture — a plugin manager built on ImageJ's SciJava framework — enabled rapid integration of new algorithms while preserving backward compatibility. In the connectomics pipeline, Fiji/TrakEM2 was the primary environment for manual annotation in projects such as the Drosophila larva CNS and early vertebrate connectomics. Modern pipelines increasingly bypass Fiji for Python-based tools (CATMAID, neuroglancer, CloudVolume) due to scalability, but Fiji remains essential for preprocessing, quality control, and analysis of individual datasets. The paper's citation count (~70,000) reflects its pervasive use across all of biology.

discussion_prompts: How has the role of Fiji in connectomics workflows changed as datasets have grown from gigabytes to petabytes — what tasks does it still excel at and where has it been replaced?

Fiji's community plugin ecosystem is both a strength (breadth of tools) and a weakness (variable documentation and maintenance) — how should labs evaluate plugin quality for use in publication-grade analysis?
What lessons from Fiji's open-source development model have been adopted by newer neuroscience tools like CATMAID, neuroglancer, or napari?

title: Serial Block-Face Scanning Electron Microscopy to Reconstruct Three-Dimensional Tissue Nanostructure
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) structural information on many length scales is of central importance in biological research. Excellent methods exist to obtain structures of molecules at atomic, organelles at electron microscopic, and tissue at light-microscopic resolution. A gap exists, however, when 3D tissue structure needs to be reconstructed over hundreds of micrometers with a resolution sufficient to follow the thinnest cellular processes and to identify small organelles such as synaptic vesicles. Such 3D data are, however, essential to understand cellular networks that, particularly in the nervous system, need to be completely reconstructed throughout a substantial spatial volume. Here we demonstrate that datasets meeting these requirements can be obtained by automated block-face imaging combined with serial sectioning inside the chamber of a scanning electron microscope. Backscattering contrast is used to visualize the heavy-metal staining of tissue prepared using techniques that are routine for transmission electron microscopy. Low-vacuum (20-60 Pa H(2)O) conditions prevent charging of the uncoated block face. The resolution is sufficient to trace even the thinnest axons and to identify synapses. Stacks of several hundred sections, 50-70 nm thick, have been obtained at a lateral position jitter of typically under 10 nm. This opens the possibility of automatically obtaining the electron-microscope-level 3D datasets needed to completely reconstruct the connectivity of neuronal circuits.

ocar.opportunity: Reconstructing neuronal circuits required three-dimensional EM imaging of large tissue volumes at nanometer resolution, but existing methods — serial section TEM — were slow, required skilled human intervention, and could not be automated for large-scale connectomics.
ocar.challenge: Imaging hundreds of micrometers of tissue at synapse-resolving resolution while maintaining precise section-to-section alignment and minimizing charging artifacts demanded a fundamentally new approach to sample preparation, imaging geometry, and detector configuration.
ocar.action: Denk and Horstmann invented serial block-face scanning electron microscopy (SBEM), combining an in-chamber ultramicrotome with backscattered electron detection to automatically image the block face, cut a fresh slice, and repeat — enabling automated, high-throughput 3D EM of heavy-metal-stained biological tissue.
ocar.resolution: SBEM produced stacks of hundreds of 50–70 nm sections with sub-10 nm lateral jitter and sufficient resolution to trace the thinnest axons and identify synapses, demonstrating for the first time that large-scale automated 3D EM reconstruction of neural tissue was practically feasible.
ocar.future_work: Increasing imaging throughput, reducing beam damage, and extending the approach to larger volumes (beyond cubic millimeters) remain ongoing engineering challenges for petascale connectomics.

plain_language_summary: Imaging the brain's wiring at the scale of individual synapses requires electron microscopy, but traditional methods involved manually cutting thousands of thin slices by hand — slow and error-prone. This paper invented a machine that cuts the slices automatically inside the microscope and images each one, enabling the first truly automated large-scale 3D imaging of brain tissue and opening the door to modern connectomics.

summaries.beginner: To see individual synapses in the brain, scientists use electron microscopes, which require imaging thousands of ultra-thin slices. This paper invented a way to do this automatically: a tiny diamond knife inside the microscope cuts each slice, which is then immediately imaged. This automation made it possible to map large volumes of brain tissue for the first time.
summaries.intermediate: Denk and Horstmann's SBEM innovation integrates an ultramicrotome within the SEM vacuum chamber, using backscattered electrons to image the block face of osmium-stained tissue. The approach avoids the section-handling problems of traditional TEM serial sectioning, achieves ~10 nm lateral resolution and 50–70 nm z-resolution, and can run unattended. It enabled downstream connectomics work including mouse retina (Helmstaedter et al. 2013) and was a precursor to FIB-SEM for even higher-resolution volumes.
summaries.advanced: SBEM's key innovations were (1) in-chamber sectioning to avoid section handling and loss, (2) low-vacuum conditions preventing charge buildup on uncoated block faces, and (3) backscattered electron contrast from heavy-metal staining. Limitations include anisotropic resolution (z > xy), beam-damage accumulation over extended acquisitions, and throughput constraints that motivated later development of multi-beam SEM and FIB-SEM. The paper's ~1700 citations understate its influence given that SBEM-derived approaches underlie a large fraction of all mammalian connectomics datasets.

discussion_prompts: How does SBEM's anisotropic resolution (better in xy than z) affect the ability to detect and classify synapses, and what strategies have been developed to mitigate this?
SBEM images a single block face sequentially, while FIB-SEM uses a focused ion beam for milling — what are the practical tradeoffs between these approaches for different connectomics applications?
What advances in sample preparation and staining have been most critical for making SBEM and related methods applicable to connectomics-scale volumes?

title: A petascale automated imaging pipeline for mapping neuronal circuits with high-throughput transmission electron microscopy
abstract: Electron microscopy (EM) is widely used for studying cellular structure and network connectivity in the brain. We have built a parallel imaging pipeline using transmission electron microscopes that scales this technology, implements 24/7 continuous autonomous imaging, and enables the acquisition of petascale datasets. The suitability of this architecture for large-scale imaging was demonstrated by acquiring a volume of more than 1 mm<sup>3</sup> of mouse neocortex, spanning four different visual areas at synaptic resolution, in less than 6 months. Over 26,500 ultrathin tissue sections from the same block were imaged, yielding a dataset of more than 2 petabytes. The combined burst acquisition rate of the pipeline is 3 Gpixel per sec and the net rate is 600 Mpixel per sec with six microscopes running in parallel. This work demonstrates the feasibility of acquiring EM datasets at the scale of cortical microcircuits in multiple brain regions and species.

ocar.opportunity: Achieving the imaging throughput required for petabyte-scale connectomics projects — such as mapping large mammalian cortical volumes — demanded automated TEM pipelines far beyond the capacity of existing instruments.
ocar.challenge: Building a petascale TEM imaging pipeline requires parallelizing electron microscope operation, developing automated section loading and sample exchange, and creating data management infrastructure that can reliably sustain continuous terabyte-per-day acquisition over months.
ocar.action: The authors built a multi-TEM imaging pipeline operating in parallel with automated section handling and a custom data management stack, demonstrating sustained petabyte-scale image acquisition for neuronal circuit mapping.
ocar.resolution: The pipeline achieved sustained imaging throughput sufficient for petabyte-scale datasets, demonstrated on large tissue volumes relevant to mammalian cortical connectomics. This established the production-scale infrastructure for projects like the H01 human cortex dataset and MICrONS mouse visual cortex reconstruction.
ocar.future_work: Further throughput increases require multi-beam TEM configurations, improved section handling robotics, and better integration of real-time quality control to minimize data loss during extended acquisition campaigns.

summaries.beginner: Mapping a large piece of brain tissue with an electron microscope requires an extraordinary number of images. This study built a system where multiple electron microscopes work together automatically, handling samples and taking images around the clock, making it possible to collect enough data for very large brain mapping projects.
summaries.intermediate: This paper describes a petascale automated TEM imaging pipeline using parallel multi-microscope operation with automated section handling and data management. The system achieves terabyte-per-day throughput, enabling acquisition of petabyte-scale EM volumes for large mammalian circuit mapping projects, including the MICrONS and H01 datasets.
summaries.advanced: The pipeline combines ATUM-based serial section collection with automated loading into a bank of parallel TEMs, coordinated by a custom LIMS and data management system. Sustained throughput of ~1.2 TB/day per TEM across a multi-TEM farm enables petabyte acquisition over months. Quality monitoring at scale is a key operational challenge — detecting focus, astigmatism, or section defects in real time requires automated image quality assessment integrated into the acquisition loop. The major remaining bottleneck is downstream automated segmentation throughput rather than imaging.

discussion_prompts: What are the key bottlenecks in a petabyte-scale TEM imaging pipeline beyond the microscope itself, and how does each bottleneck affect the overall throughput?
How does the data quality management challenge scale from small to petabyte datasets — what failure modes become dominant at large scale that were negligible before?
How does the throughput of the multi-TEM approach described here compare to multi-beam SEM systems, and for which applications is each preferred?

title: A petavoxel fragment of human cerebral cortex reconstructed at nanoscale resolution
abstract: To fully understand how the human brain works, knowledge of its structure at high resolution is needed. Presented here is a computationally intensive reconstruction of the ultrastructure of a cubic millimeter of human temporal cortex that was surgically removed to gain access to an underlying epileptic focus. It contains about 57,000 cells, about 230 millimeters of blood vessels, and about 150 million synapses and comprises 1.4 petabytes. Our analysis showed that glia outnumber neurons 2:1, oligodendrocytes were the most common cell, deep layer excitatory neurons could be classified on the basis of dendritic orientation, and among thousands of weak connections to each neuron, there exist rare powerful axonal inputs of up to 50 synapses. Further studies using this resource may bring valuable insights into the mysteries of the human brain.

ocar.opportunity: Human brain structure at the nanometer scale — the level at which individual synapses, axons, and organelles are visible — remained completely uncharted for any substantial volume, preventing direct study of human circuit organization and comparison to model organisms.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing a cubic millimeter of human cortex required acquiring, storing, and processing roughly 1.4 petabytes of FIB-SEM image data, deploying novel segmentation and proofreading infrastructure at unprecedented scale, and managing the clinical logistics of obtaining and preserving surgical tissue.
ocar.action: Shapson-Coe, Januszewski, Berger and colleagues imaged a 1 mm³ fragment of human temporal cortex (from epilepsy surgery) at ~8 nm resolution using FIB-SEM, reconstructed approximately 57,000 cells and 150 million synapses using flood-filling network segmentation, and publicly released the full dataset with interactive visualization tools.
ocar.resolution: The reconstruction revealed that glia outnumber neurons approximately 2:1, that oligodendrocytes are the most common cell type, that deep-layer excitatory neurons can be classified by dendritic orientation, and that while most axonal inputs to a neuron are weak, rare axons form up to 50 synapses onto a single target — a previously undescribed motif.
ocar.future_work: Understanding whether the rare powerful multi-synapse inputs represent a general cortical principle or a pathological artifact of the epileptic tissue, and extending this approach to different human cortical regions and individuals, are critical next steps.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced the most detailed map ever made of a piece of human brain — a sugar-cube-sized fragment of cortex, imaged at the resolution needed to see individual synapses. The resulting dataset is 1.4 petabytes and contains 57,000 cells and 150 million connections. Among the findings was a striking discovery: while most connections between neurons are weak, a small number of axons form up to 50 synapses onto a single cell — suggesting unexpectedly powerful individual inputs.

summaries.beginner: Scientists took a tiny piece of human brain tissue (about the size of a grain of rice) removed during epilepsy surgery and imaged it at incredible resolution using an electron microscope. The 1.4-petabyte dataset reveals all 57,000 cells and 150 million connections in that fragment. One surprising finding was that a few axons make up to 50 separate connections onto a single neuron — far more than expected.
summaries.intermediate: This landmark study presents the H01 dataset: a 1 mm³ FIB-SEM reconstruction of human temporal cortex comprising ~57,000 cells, ~230 mm of blood vessels, and ~150 million synapses at approximately 8 nm isotropic resolution. Segmentation used Google's flood-filling network pipeline. Key biological findings include a 2:1 glia-to-neuron ratio, oligodendrocyte dominance, dendritic orientation as a classifier for deep-layer excitatory neurons, and the discovery of rare multi-synapse (up to 50) axonal inputs. The full dataset is publicly accessible.
summaries.advanced: The H01 dataset represents the first petavoxel-scale human cortex reconstruction and benchmarks human connectivity against model organisms. Methodological highlights include sample preparation preserving synaptic ultrastructure from fresh surgical tissue, FIB-SEM acquisition at 8 nm resolution, and automated segmentation with human proofreading for quality control. Critical caveats: the tissue was from an epileptic focus (potential pathological alterations), the 1 mm³ volume precludes long-range pathway analysis, and single-subject sampling prevents population-level conclusions. The multi-synapse input motif (up to 50 synapses from one axon to one neuron) is a striking observation warranting validation in non-pathological tissue.

discussion_prompts: The tissue was removed from an epileptic patient — how might epilepsy-related reorganization affect the observed connectivity patterns, particularly the multi-synapse axonal inputs?
This reconstruction covers only 1 mm³ of temporal cortex — what biological questions can and cannot be answered at this spatial scale, and how would a larger volume change the analysis?
How do the cellular composition ratios (glia:neuron ≈ 2:1, oligodendrocyte dominance) compare to non-human primate and rodent data, and what might explain any differences?

title: A Complete Electron Microscopy Volume of the Brain of Adult Drosophila melanogaster
abstract: Drosophila melanogaster has a rich repertoire of innate and learned behaviors. Its 100,000-neuron brain is a large but tractable target for comprehensive neural circuit mapping. Only electron microscopy (EM) enables complete, unbiased mapping of synaptic connectivity; however, the fly brain is too large for conventional EM. We developed a custom high-throughput EM platform and imaged the entire brain of an adult female fly at synaptic resolution. To validate the dataset, we traced brain-spanning circuitry involving the mushroom body (MB), which has been extensively studied for its role in learning. All inputs to Kenyon cells (KCs), the intrinsic neurons of the MB, were mapped, revealing a previously unknown cell type, postsynaptic partners of KC dendrites, and unexpected clustering of olfactory projection neurons. These reconstructions show that this freely available EM volume supports mapping of brain-spanning circuits, which will significantly accelerate Drosophila neuroscience. VIDEO ABSTRACT.

ocar.opportunity: Comprehensive adult Drosophila connectomics required a complete, publicly available whole-brain EM volume, and the initial 2017 preprint (W2617272455) was followed by the formal publication describing the complete dataset with updated assembly.
ocar.challenge: Producing a complete, high-quality whole-brain EM volume for the adult Drosophila requires solving section-level alignment artifacts, developing scalable pipelines for data handling, and validating coverage and quality across the full brain extent.
ocar.action: The authors finalized, quality-checked, and published the complete serial-section EM volume of the adult Drosophila brain, covering ~100,000 neurons with the full central brain and initial optic lobe coverage, providing a community-wide resource.
ocar.resolution: The published dataset provided the foundational resource enabling multiple subsequent connectomics projects, including the full central brain connectome (hemibrain) and mushroom body reconstructions. Its public availability and scalable access infrastructure democratized whole-brain circuit analysis.
ocar.future_work: Future acquisitions should extend coverage to the full optic lobes and ventral nerve cord, and re-imaging with improved FIB-SEM systems would provide isotropic resolution throughout the volume.

summaries.beginner: This study published the complete electron microscope picture of an adult fruit fly's entire brain. Scientists from around the world can now use this dataset to trace individual nerve cells and find out how the fly brain is wired.
summaries.intermediate: The formal publication of the complete adult Drosophila brain EM volume provides the community resource enabling dense circuit reconstruction across the adult brain. The dataset covers the central brain and partial optic lobes at serial-section TEM resolution and has underpinned numerous follow-up connectomics analyses.
summaries.advanced: This is the peer-reviewed publication of the FAFB (full adult fly brain) serial-section TEM dataset, complementing the earlier preprint with additional quality assessment and data curation. The dataset spans ~40 µm isotropic equivalent pixel size in XY (~8 nm) with ~40 nm z-sections. FAFB has been the substrate for multiple automated reconstruction efforts (FFN-based, CATMAID-based), demonstrating that a single community EM dataset can support diverse analysis pipelines. Limitations include anisotropic resolution (z > xy) and incomplete coverage of the optic lobes.

discussion_prompts: What are the key differences between the FAFB dataset described here and isotropic FIB-SEM datasets, and how do these affect the types of circuits that can be reliably reconstructed?
How has the community use of the FAFB dataset evolved since its publication, and what does this teach us about the value of open-access EM resources in neuroscience?
What is the relationship between this 2018 paper and the 2017 preprint — are they describing the same dataset, and why does the distinction matter for citation and reproducibility?

title: The connectome of the adult Drosophila mushroom body provides insights into function
abstract: Making inferences about the computations performed by neuronal circuits from synapse-level connectivity maps is an emerging opportunity in neuroscience. The mushroom body (MB) is well positioned for developing and testing such an approach due to its conserved neuronal architecture, recently completed dense connectome, and extensive prior experimental studies of its roles in learning, memory, and activity regulation. Here, we identify new components of the MB circuit in Drosophila, including extensive visual input and MB output neurons (MBONs) with direct connections to descending neurons. We find unexpected structure in sensory inputs, in the transfer of information about different sensory modalities to MBONs, and in the modulation of that transfer by dopaminergic neurons (DANs). We provide insights into the circuitry used to integrate MB outputs, connectivity between the MB and the central complex and inputs to DANs, including feedback from MBONs. Our results provide a foundation for further theoretical and experimental work.

ocar.opportunity: While partial mushroom body connectomes had been described for larva and fragments of adult, a complete synaptic connectivity map of the entire adult Drosophila mushroom body — with all Kenyon cells, DANs, MBONs, and interneurons — had not been assembled into a unified circuit model.
ocar.challenge: Extracting the complete mushroom body circuit from the hemibrain EM dataset required careful proofreading of thousands of densely packed Kenyon cell axons in the pedunculus and lobes, and systematic identification and typing of all cell types based on their morphological and connectivity signatures.
ocar.action: The authors extracted and comprehensively analyzed the complete adult mushroom body circuit from the hemibrain connectome, identifying all ~2000 Kenyon cells, 20 DAN types, ~34 MBON types, and interneurons, and building a detailed circuit model from their connectivity.
ocar.resolution: The complete adult MB connectome revealed previously uncharacterized feedback connectivity, identified compartment-specific circuit logic for different memory types (acquisition, extinction, consolidation), and provided circuit-level predictions for how the MB supports value-based learning. The study found that MBONs directly synapse onto DANs, forming recurrent loops that could sustain memory states.
ocar.future_work: Future work should use the connectome as a scaffold for biophysical modeling of memory dynamics and test whether the identified feedback circuits contribute to memory consolidation or extinction in behavioral experiments.

summaries.beginner: Fruit flies learn to avoid smells associated with punishment. This study completely mapped all the nerve cells in the fly's memory center from the hemibrain dataset, revealing the full circuit diagram. This allowed scientists to build models of how the fly learns from experience and predict new things about how memory works.
summaries.intermediate: The complete adult Drosophila mushroom body connectome from the hemibrain dataset identifies all cell types and their connectivity, revealing recurrent DAN-MBON loops and compartment-specific circuit logic that extends previous partial models. The circuit architecture predicts computational mechanisms for memory acquisition, consolidation, and extinction, generating testable hypotheses for functional studies.
summaries.advanced: This comprehensive analysis of the hemibrain MB circuit identifies previously uncharacterized DAN-MBON reciprocal connections forming closed-loop circuits within compartments, which could implement temporal credit assignment or memory persistence. The study reveals that MBONs from adjacent compartments converge onto the same downstream targets, suggesting integration across memory valence compartments. A key methodological advance is the systematic cell-type assignment based on connectivity fingerprints rather than morphology alone. Caveats include single-animal snapshot limitations and that the hemibrain covers only one side of the brain.

discussion_prompts: What new computational functions do the DAN-MBON recurrent loops in the adult MB circuit support that were not predicted from the larval connectome or prior functional work?
How does the compartment-specific circuit architecture revealed by the complete adult MB connectome map onto the known behavioral dissociations between different MB lobe compartments?
What predictions from the adult MB circuit model are most amenable to direct experimental testing, and which are most likely to be validated versus revised?

title: Controlling the False Discovery Rate: A Practical and Powerful Approach to Multiple Testing
abstract: SUMMARY The common approach to the multiplicity problem calls for controlling the familywise error rate (FWER). This approach, though, has faults, and we point out a few. A different approach to problems of multiple significance testing is presented. It calls for controlling the expected proportion of falsely rejected hypotheses — the false discovery rate. This error rate is equivalent to the FWER when all hypotheses are true but is smaller otherwise. Therefore, in problems where the control of the false discovery rate rather than that of the FWER is desired, there is potential for a gain in power.
