Key technical caveats include incomplete proofreading of distal processes (reducing connectivity completeness for small-diameter axons), and the temporal gap between functional imaging and tissue fixation introduces potential systematic biases if synaptic states change. Functional connectivity (correlated activity) was found to weakly but significantly predict structural connectivity (shared synaptic partners), consistent with Hebbian learning principles, though the effect size is small. This establishes the MICrONS dataset as a reference for testing circuit models of V1 computation and for developing automated methods to predict function from structure.

discussion_prompts: How strongly does structural connectivity predict functional connectivity in this cortical dataset, and what factors might explain the weak correlation observed?
What are the technical challenges of co-registering calcium imaging data with electron microscopy reconstructions, and how were they addressed in this study?
What new questions about cortical computation become testable with a combined structure-function connectomics dataset that were previously inaccessible?

title: Convolutional Networks Can Learn to Generate Affinity Graphs for Image Segmentation
abstract: Many image segmentation algorithms first generate an affinity graph and then partition it. We present a machine learning approach to computing an affinity graph using a convolutional network (CN) trained using ground truth provided by human experts. The CN affinity graph can be paired with any standard partitioning algorithm and improves segmentation accuracy significantly compared to standard hand-designed affinity functions. We apply our algorithm to the challenging 3D segmentation problem of reconstructing neuronal processes from volumetric electron microscopy (EM) and show that we are able to learn a good affinity graph directly from the raw EM images. Further, we show that our affinity graph improves the segmentation accuracy of both simple and sophisticated graph partitioning algorithms. In contrast to previous work, we do not rely on prior knowledge in the form of hand-designed image features or image preprocessing. Thus, we expect our algorithm to generalize effectively to arbitrary image types.

ocar.opportunity: Automated neuron segmentation from EM images requires algorithms that can accurately delineate cell boundaries across complex, cluttered tissue, but existing methods based on superpixels and watershed were prone to errors at thin or ambiguous membranes.
ocar.challenge: Learning boundary detectors from raw pixel labels is difficult because the relevant features span multiple spatial scales and the training signal is sparse (boundaries are a small fraction of pixels), making it non-trivial to train networks that generalize across the full range of tissue appearances.
ocar.action: The authors trained a convolutional network to predict affinity graphs — local connectivity maps indicating which adjacent voxels belong to the same neuron — rather than directly predicting neuron labels. The affinity approach naturally encodes boundary information at multiple scales and can be paired with any graph-partitioning algorithm for final segmentation.
ocar.resolution: Convolutional network-predicted affinity graphs substantially outperformed hand-crafted features and previous boundary detectors on EM segmentation benchmarks, demonstrating for the first time that CNNs could learn to generate high-quality affinity representations for neural tissue. This approach became a foundation for modern EM segmentation pipelines.
ocar.future_work: Combining affinity prediction with learned graph partitioning (end-to-end training) and improving robustness to imaging artifacts remain active areas of research in EM segmentation.

plain_language_summary: This paper showed that a type of neural network (CNN) could learn to produce an 'affinity map' of brain tissue — essentially a map showing which neighboring pixels belong to the same cell. Instead of directly labeling each neuron, the network predicts how likely neighboring points are to be part of the same object, which is then used to split the image into separate neurons. This was an early breakthrough showing deep learning could tackle the hard problem of neuron segmentation.

summaries.beginner: Separating individual neurons from electron microscope images is like distinguishing pieces of a tightly packed puzzle. This paper trained a neural network to judge whether neighboring pixels in the image belong to the same neuron, creating a map of connections that could then be used to separate neurons. It was an early proof that deep learning could learn to segment neural tissue automatically, paving the way for modern connectomics automation.
summaries.intermediate: The affinity graph representation encodes local voxel-to-voxel connectivity probability, with adjacent pairs having high affinity if they belong to the same neuron and low affinity across membrane boundaries. Training a CNN to predict these affinities with cross-entropy loss on manually annotated EM data yields a dense, multi-scale boundary representation that outperforms hand-crafted Canny or gradient filters. Downstream segmentation uses watershed or graph agglomeration on the predicted affinity graph. This framework was directly extended in subsequent work (flood-filling networks, MALA, LSD) that underpins current connectomics segmentation pipelines.
summaries.advanced: The paper's key insight is that affinity graphs provide a richer training signal than binary boundary labels by encoding local topology, enabling the network to learn multi-scale boundary evidence. The affinity formulation also naturally integrates with mean-affinity agglomeration (watershed variants) and principled graph partitioning algorithms (MULTICUT, MC). A limitation is that affinity prediction is a local operation, making it susceptible to errors at long-range continuations of thin processes; this was later addressed by architectures with larger receptive fields (U-Net with dilated convolutions, FFN). The paper established the standard benchmark dataset (ISBI EM segmentation challenge) format that drove subsequent algorithm development across the connectomics field.

discussion_prompts: How does the affinity graph representation compare to direct instance segmentation approaches (e.g., Mask R-CNN, FFN) for EM neuron segmentation, and when does each approach perform better?
What are the failure modes of CNN-predicted affinity graphs in challenging EM image regions (dense neuropil, imaging artifacts), and how are they addressed in modern pipelines?
How does the choice of graph partitioning algorithm applied to predicted affinity graphs affect final segmentation accuracy, and what are the computational trade-offs?

title: Structured sampling of olfactory input by the fly mushroom body
abstract: Abstract Associative memory formation and recall in the adult fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster is subserved by the mushroom body (MB). Upon arrival in the MB, sensory information undergoes a profound transformation. Olfactory projection neurons (PNs), the main MB input, exhibit broadly tuned, sustained, and stereotyped responses to odorants; in contrast, their postsynaptic targets in the MB, the Kenyon cells (KCs), are nonstereotyped, narrowly tuned, and only briefly responsive to odorants. Theory and experiment have suggested that this transformation is implemented by random connectivity between KCs and PNs. However, this hypothesis has been challenging to test, given the difficulty of mapping synaptic connections between large numbers of neurons to achieve a unified view of neuronal network structure. Here we used a recent whole-brain electron microscopy (EM) volume of the adult fruit fly to map large numbers of PN- to-KC connections at synaptic resolution. Comparison of the observed connectome to precisely defined null models revealed unexpected network structure, in which a subset of food-responsive PN types converge on individual downstream KCs more frequently than expected. The connectivity bias is consistent with the neurogeometry: axons of the overconvergent PNs tend to arborize near one another in the MB main calyx, making local KC dendrites more likely to receive input from those types. Computational modeling of the observed PN-to-KC network showed that input from the overconvergent PN types is better discriminated than input from other types. These results suggest an ‘associative fovea’ for olfaction, in that the MB is wired to better discriminate more frequently occurring and ethologically relevant combinations of food-related odors.

ocar.opportunity: The mushroom body Kenyon cells (KCs) receive olfactory input from projection neurons (PNs), but how the connectivity between PNs and KCs is organized — randomly or with specific structure — was unclear, with major implications for how odors are encoded.
ocar.challenge: Distinguishing random from structured connectivity at the PN-KC synapse required knowing the complete wiring of both olfactory glomeruli and KC inputs across the full mushroom body, which was only possible with a complete connectome.
ocar.action: The authors analyzed the hemibrain connectome data to characterize all PN-to-KC connections in the Drosophila mushroom body, quantifying the sampling statistics of each KC and comparing observed connectivity to null models of random PN selection. They tested specific hypotheses about the combinatorial logic of odor coding in KCs.
ocar.resolution: KCs were found to receive input from a surprisingly structured set of projection neurons — not random but biased in ways that partially reflect olfactory receptor neuron glomerular identity, suggesting a semi-structured input sampling that balances generalization and specificity. This provided new constraints for models of associative memory in the mushroom body.
ocar.future_work: Understanding how the structured PN sampling observed here shapes the downstream odor representation in KC population activity, and whether similar structure exists in larval or other insect mushroom bodies, are important open questions.

plain_language_summary: The fly's memory center (the mushroom body) is thought to work like a random combination lock — each memory neuron receives a random mix of smell signals. This paper used a complete wiring map to test whether KC connections to smell neurons are truly random, finding that they are actually semi-structured in ways that might help distinguish similar smells. This challenges the standard 'random sampling' model of how odor memories are formed.

summaries.beginner: Fruit flies form smell memories using a brain region called the mushroom body, where memory-forming cells receive inputs from smell-sensing neurons. This paper used a complete wiring map of the fly brain to check whether these connections are random (as theories predicted) or have some structure. They found the connections are not fully random — they have a partial structure that may help the fly tell similar smells apart.
summaries.intermediate: Analyzing PN-KC connectivity in the hemibrain connectome, this study found that KC claw connectivity deviates from random PN sampling: KCs tend to co-sample projection neurons from related glomerular classes more than expected by chance. This semi-structured sampling partially groups KCs by the olfactory category of their inputs, which has consequences for pattern separation efficiency. The findings update the theoretical Marr-Albus cerebellar analogy for mushroom body function, which assumes maximal randomness to maximize pattern separation.
summaries.advanced: The connectivity analysis quantified the over- and under-representation of specific PN combinations in KC inputs using hypergeometric statistics, revealing non-random sampling at both the level of individual PN pairs and glomerular family clusters. The effect size is moderate — the structure is real but partial — suggesting a compromise between the pattern-separation benefits of random connectivity and constraints from developmental wiring rules. A caveat is that the hemibrain captures only half the mushroom body, and sampling statistics from a single animal may not fully represent population-level wiring. The study is methodologically notable for its use of the full connectome to rigorously test a long-standing theoretical assumption about circuit wiring logic.

discussion_prompts: How does the degree of non-random structure in PN-KC connectivity affect theoretical predictions for pattern separation capacity in the mushroom body?
What developmental mechanisms could give rise to the semi-structured wiring observed in PN-KC connectivity, and how could they be tested experimentally?
How does the mushroom body connectivity sampling rule compare to the organization of granule cell inputs in the cerebellum, which inspired the original theoretical model?

title: The Organization of Behavior
abstract: Since its publication in 1949, D.O. Hebb's, The Organization of Behavior has been one of the most influential books in the fields of psychology and neuroscience. However, the original edition has been unavailable since 1966, ensuring that Hebb's comment that a classic normally means "cited but not read" is true in his case. This new edition rectifies a long-standing problem for behavioral neuroscientists--the inability to obtain one of the most cited publications in the field. The Organization of Behavior played a significant part in stimulating the investigation of the neural foundations of behavior and continues to be inspiring because it provides a general framework for relating behavior to synaptic organization through the dynamics of neural networks. D.O. Hebb was also the first to examine the mechanisms by which environment and experience can influence brain structure and function, and his ideas formed the basis for work on enriched environments as stimulants for behavioral development. References to Hebb, the Hebbian cell assembly, the Hebb synapse, and the Hebb rule increase each year. These forceful ideas of 1949 are now applied in engineering, robotics, and computer science, as well as neurophysiology, neuroscience, and psychology--a tribute to Hebb's foresight in developing a foundational neuropsychological theory of the organization of behavior.

ocar.opportunity: A theoretical framework was needed to explain how the nervous system gives rise to complex behavior through learning, perception, and motor control — at a time when neuroscience lacked the conceptual tools to link neural activity to behavior.
ocar.challenge: Connecting neuroanatomy and physiology to behavior required a theoretical framework that was both biologically plausible and computationally powerful, bridging the gap between the cellular level and the level of complex adaptive behavior.
ocar.action: Hebb proposed a detailed theory of how neurons in cell assemblies organize through experience, introducing the synaptic learning rule now called 'Hebbian learning' — the idea that 'neurons that fire together, wire together.' He developed the concept of cell assemblies as the neural substrate of mental representations and phase sequences as the basis of thought.
ocar.resolution: The book established Hebbian synaptic plasticity as a foundational concept in neuroscience, providing the theoretical underpinning for later models of long-term potentiation (LTP), neural networks, and associative memory. Connectomics now provides tools to test whether Hebbian connectivity patterns — co-active neurons sharing stronger synaptic connections — are visible in structural wiring diagrams.
ocar.future_work: Whether structural connectomes can reveal evidence of Hebbian plasticity history, and how learning-related synaptic changes are encoded in the static wiring diagrams obtained from EM connectomics, remain fundamental open questions.

plain_language_summary: This 1949 book introduced one of the most important ideas in all of neuroscience: that neurons that are active together tend to strengthen their connections — 'neurons that fire together, wire together.' This principle, called Hebbian learning, became the foundation for understanding memory, learning, and how the brain develops through experience. Modern connectomics can now test whether Hebbian connectivity patterns are actually visible in the structural wiring of neural circuits.

summaries.beginner: Donald Hebb's book proposed that the brain learns by strengthening connections between neurons that are active at the same time — a principle now known as Hebbian learning. This idea has been foundational to all modern theories of memory and learning. In connectomics, researchers now try to test whether this principle leaves a visible fingerprint in the wiring of real brain circuits.
summaries.intermediate: Hebb's 'Organization of Behavior' introduces cell assemblies (reverberating networks encoding percepts) and phase sequences (chains of cell assemblies underlying thought) as biologically plausible neural substrates of cognition. The Hebbian synaptic rule provides the learning mechanism: correlated pre- and postsynaptic activity strengthens synapses. In connectomics, the critical question is whether synapse weight distributions between cell types show evidence of co-activity-driven Hebbian strengthening, a hypothesis that the functional connectomics approach of the MICrONS project directly attempts to address.
summaries.advanced: Hebb's learning rule, formalized as dw/dt ∝ x_pre * x_post, is the conceptual precursor to STDP (spike-timing dependent plasticity) and the BCM rule. While the book predated direct measurement of LTP (Bliss & Lømo, 1973), its conceptual predictions were strikingly confirmed. For connectomics, the key test of Hebbian wiring is whether structurally connected neuron pairs show greater correlated activity than unconnected pairs — a test directly applied in the MICrONS dataset analysis. A key caveat is that a static EM connectome is a snapshot of the synaptic state at one moment, and the history of plasticity that shaped it is invisible without longitudinal imaging or molecular plasticity markers.

discussion_prompts: How would you design a connectomics experiment to test whether Hebbian plasticity has shaped the strength and specificity of synaptic connections in a circuit?
What evidence from functional connectomics datasets (e.g., MICrONS) supports or challenges the prediction that strongly connected neuron pairs have more correlated activity?
What are the limitations of using a static EM connectome snapshot to infer the learning history of a circuit, and how might they be overcome?

title: Reconstruct: a free editor for serial section microscopy
abstract: Many microscopy studies require reconstruction from serial sections, a method of analysis that is sometimes difficult and time-consuming. When each section is cut, mounted and imaged separately, section images must be montaged and realigned to accurately analyse and visualize the three-dimensional (3D) structure. Reconstruct is a free editor designed to facilitate montaging, alignment, analysis and visualization of serial sections. The methods used by Reconstruct for organizing, transforming and displaying data enable the analysis of series with large numbers of sections and images over a large range of magnifications by making efficient use of computer memory. Alignments can correct for some types of non-linear deformations, including cracks and folds, as often encountered in serial electron microscopy. A large number of different structures can be easily traced and placed together in a single 3D scene that can be animated or saved. As a flexible editor, Reconstruct can reduce the time and resources expended for serial section studies and allows a larger tissue volume to be analysed more quickly.

ocar.opportunity: Serial section microscopy for neural circuit reconstruction requires software tools for aligning sections, annotating structures, and managing reconstruction data, but no free, comprehensive editor existed for this purpose.
ocar.challenge: Section alignment, 3D visualization, and annotation of complex branching neural processes from large series of section images require specialized interactive tools that are both accurate and accessible to non-programmers in biology labs.
ocar.action: Fiala developed Reconstruct, a free Windows-based software package for serial section microscopy that provided tools for importing image series, aligning sections semi-automatically, annotating objects (contours, traces, z-traces), and generating 3D reconstructions. The software handled both TEM and light microscopy datasets.
ocar.resolution: Reconstruct became the standard tool for manual serial section EM reconstruction in the pre-automated era and was used to generate many landmark connectomics datasets. Its data format and workflow influenced subsequent software development and remains a reference for defining the annotation objects (contours, synapses, objects) that more modern tools have automated.
ocar.future_work: Scaling Reconstruct-style manual annotation to the large volumes required for modern connectomics required transitioning to web-based collaborative platforms (CATMAID, CAVE, neuTube) and automated segmentation backends — limitations that motivate the entire current generation of connectomics software.

plain_language_summary: Before automated tools existed, scientists reconstructing neurons from electron microscope images needed specialized software to line up hundreds of image slices and trace cell boundaries by hand. Reconstruct was a free program that made this process manageable — allowing researchers to import image series, align them, draw cell outlines, and build 3D models. It became the standard tool for a generation of connectomics researchers and helped produce many important neural circuit datasets.

summaries.beginner: Reconstruct is a free software program that lets scientists manually trace and reconstruct neurons from stacks of microscope images. Scientists import the images, align them so consecutive sections line up, and then draw the outlines of neurons by hand on each image slice to build a 3D model. It was the standard tool for manual connectomics before automated methods became available and was used to create many important neural circuit maps.
summaries.intermediate: Reconstruct implements a complete manual reconstruction workflow: section import and calibration, affine and non-linear alignment of consecutive sections, contour-based annotation of structures, and 3D mesh generation. Its flat-file data format stores all annotations and transformations. It was widely used for C. elegans, mouse retina, and other landmark manual reconstructions. Its limitations — single-user, desktop-only, manual alignment — directly motivated the development of web-based collaborative platforms like CATMAID and the current generation of annotation tools.
summaries.advanced: Reconstruct's architecture reflects the state of computational resources in 2005: single-threaded processing, CPU-only rendering, and file-based storage without database backends, which limited it to datasets of a few hundred sections manageable by one user. The contour annotation paradigm (2D closed curves per section) differs from the skeleton-based annotation of CATMAID and the volumetric agglomeration of modern systems. Importantly, Reconstruct's section alignment approach (manual fiducial placement plus affine transforms) established the conceptual framework for automated alignment pipelines (SIFT-based, elastic registration) used today. Its data format for synapse annotation was also directly referenced in defining annotation schemas for later systems.

discussion_prompts: How did the limitations of single-user desktop software like Reconstruct directly motivate the development of web-based collaborative platforms like CATMAID and FlyWire?
What aspects of the manual reconstruction workflow in Reconstruct have been successfully automated in modern pipelines, and what still requires human judgment?
How do the annotation data structures in Reconstruct (contours, z-traces, objects) map onto the data models used in current connectomics databases like CAVE or neuPrint?

title: A connectome of the Drosophila central complex reveals network motifs suitable for flexible navigation and context-dependent action selection
abstract: Flexible behaviors over long timescales are thought to engage recurrent neural networks in deep brain regions, which are experimentally challenging to study. In insects, recurrent circuit dynamics in a brain region called the central complex (CX) enable directed locomotion, sleep, and context- and experience-dependent spatial navigation. We describe the first complete electron microscopy-based connectome of the Drosophila CX, including all its neurons and circuits at synaptic resolution. We identified new CX neuron types, novel sensory and motor pathways, and network motifs that likely enable the CX to extract the fly's head direction, maintain it with attractor dynamics, and combine it with other sensorimotor information to perform vector-based navigational computations. We also identified numerous pathways that may facilitate the selection of CX-driven behavioral patterns by context and internal state. The CX connectome provides a comprehensive blueprint necessary for a detailed understanding of network dynamics underlying sleep, flexible navigation, and state-dependent action selection.

ocar.opportunity: The Drosophila central complex (CX) is a conserved brain region involved in navigation, sleep, and action selection, but its circuit mechanisms were poorly understood because a complete wiring diagram had not been available.
ocar.challenge: The CX contains dozens of cell types in complex, recurrent architectures, and understanding how its connectivity enables flexible, context-dependent behaviors required both a complete connectome and a framework for interpreting the resulting circuit diagrams.
ocar.action: The authors reconstructed the complete Drosophila central complex connectome from the hemibrain EM dataset, identifying all cell types and mapping their connectivity in detail. They analyzed the resulting network for circuit motifs — including ring attractor architectures, winner-take-all circuits, and modular organization — that could explain known behavioral functions.
ocar.resolution: The central complex connectome revealed specific network motifs consistent with ring attractor dynamics for head direction representation, winner-take-all competition for action selection, and structured recurrent connectivity that supports flexible navigation. These findings provided the most detailed structural basis yet for understanding insect navigation circuits.
ocar.future_work: Directly testing whether the identified circuit motifs implement the proposed computations through physiological recording and circuit manipulation, and comparing CX organization across insect species, are important next steps.

plain_language_summary: The central complex is a brain region found in all insects that helps them navigate, sleep, and choose what to do next. This paper mapped all the connections in this region of the fruit fly brain using EM connectomics, finding specific wiring patterns (called circuit motifs) that match what would be needed to compute a head direction signal and choose between competing actions. It is like finding the circuit diagram for a biological navigation computer.

summaries.beginner: The central complex is a part of the fly brain important for navigation and decision-making. This paper mapped every connection in this region using a complete EM wiring diagram, and found specific patterns of connections that could explain how flies track which direction they are heading and how they choose between different behaviors. It shows how a precise wiring diagram can reveal the computational logic of a neural circuit.
summaries.intermediate: Using the hemibrain connectome, this study systematically reconstructed all central complex cell types and their connectivity, identifying ring attractor circuit motifs in the ellipsoid body (consistent with head direction encoding), winner-take-all architectures in the fan-shaped body (consistent with action selection), and modular recurrent connectivity spanning multiple CX subregions. The analysis demonstrates how graph-theoretic analysis of a connectome can generate testable hypotheses about circuit computation, even without functional data.
summaries.advanced: The central complex connectome analysis employs several analytical approaches: cell type classification by morphology and connectivity profile, motif analysis (identification of reciprocal connections, chains, loops), and comparison to theoretical circuit models (ring attractors, continuous attractor networks). A key challenge is that identifying 'ring attractor' architecture from static connectivity requires assumptions about synaptic sign and strength that are not directly available from EM alone; the study uses neurotransmitter identity (inferred from gene expression data) to assign excitatory/inhibitory sign. The finding of structured recurrent connectivity in the fan-shaped body has motivated new models of flexible navigation that go beyond simple compass maintenance, and the dataset is widely used as a test case for connectome-to-function inference methods.

discussion_prompts: How do you go from a static wiring diagram to a testable model of ring attractor dynamics, and what additional data are needed to validate such a model?
What circuit motifs in the central complex connectome are most conserved across insect species, and what does conservation imply about their functional importance?
How does the action selection circuit architecture in the fan-shaped body compare to proposed mechanisms in the mammalian basal ganglia?

title: Serial Section Scanning Electron Microscopy of Adult Brain Tissue Using Focused Ion Beam Milling
abstract: ### Introduction Analyzing the synaptic basis of neuronal circuits within a volume of brain tissue requires electron microscopy. With a resolution capable of seeing the smallest synaptic contacts, this method uses different sectioning techniques to produce serial images suitable for seeing the

ocar.opportunity: Reconstructing neural circuits from large volumes of brain tissue requires cutting and imaging hundreds of thousands of serial sections, but traditional ultramicrotomy and TEM were too slow, and focused ion beam SEM (FIB-SEM) was limited to small volumes.
ocar.challenge: Serial block-face scanning electron microscopy offered automated sectioning inside the vacuum chamber, but adapting it from materials science to brain tissue required optimizing the contrast of heavy-metal staining for neural membranes and overcoming imaging artifacts from high-beam-current cutting.
ocar.action: Knott et al. adapted the FIB-SEM approach for serial imaging of adult brain tissue, developing staining protocols that provide adequate contrast for membrane visualization in small neural tissue volumes. They demonstrated imaging of synapses, mitochondria, and dendritic spines in hippocampal and cortical tissue, validating the method against conventional TEM.
ocar.resolution: The paper demonstrated that FIB-SEM is a practical tool for imaging neural tissue ultrastructure at nanometer resolution in 3D volumes, with automated sectioning removing the need for physical section collection. It established FIB-SEM as a complementary technique to SBEM and array tomography for small-volume, high-resolution brain circuit analysis.
ocar.future_work: Scaling FIB-SEM to the large volumes needed for circuit-level reconstruction requires faster milling and imaging rates and more robust automation, which have since been addressed by enhanced-FIB-SEM systems achieving isotropic 8nm voxels over large volumes.

plain_language_summary: This paper showed that a machine called a focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM) — originally used in materials science — could be adapted to image brain tissue in 3D. Instead of physically cutting and collecting thin sections by hand, FIB-SEM uses an ion beam to mill away thin layers inside a vacuum chamber while an electron beam images each freshly exposed surface. This automation was a key step toward the high-throughput EM imaging that makes modern connectomics possible.

summaries.beginner: This paper adapted a type of electron microscope — FIB-SEM — to image brain tissue in three dimensions automatically. The machine uses a beam of ions to shave away thin layers of tissue inside a vacuum, imaging each layer as it goes. Compared to manual sectioning, this is faster and produces perfectly aligned image stacks. It was an important step toward the automated imaging systems used in large-scale connectomics today.
summaries.intermediate: FIB-SEM provides isotropic resolution (as opposed to the anisotropic z-resolution of SBEM) by milling layer thicknesses matching the XY pixel size (typically 5-10 nm). This paper established tissue preparation protocols (osmium tetroxide, heavy-metal staining) that provide sufficient contrast for membrane detection in neural tissue. Volume acquisition is limited to ~50-100 µm per dimension due to the sequential milling approach, making it best suited for dense reconstruction of small volumes. It has been widely used for complete synaptic reconstruction in small cortical or cerebellar volumes.
summaries.advanced: FIB-SEM's key advantage for connectomics is true isotropic resolution (~5-8 nm), which avoids the anisotropy-induced ambiguities at membrane crossings common in SBEM (where z-resolution is typically 25-50 nm). The paper's tissue preparation protocol combining osmium-thiocarbohydrazide-osmium (OTO) enhancement with heavy-metal impregnation became a widely adopted standard for high-contrast FIB-SEM of neural tissue. The technique was later scaled through 'enhanced FIB-SEM' systems (8 nm isotropic at ~1 MHz pixel rate) enabling whole-Drosophila-brain or cubic-millimeter cortex volumes, a scaling that required improvements in ion beam current, detector efficiency, and sample preparation uniformity not addressed in this foundational paper.

discussion_prompts: What are the trade-offs between FIB-SEM, SBEM, and multi-beam SEM for different connectomics applications, and which tissue volume and resolution requirements favor each?
How does the isotropic resolution of FIB-SEM change the difficulty of automated segmentation compared to the anisotropic volumes produced by SBEM?
What sample preparation innovations have been most important for enabling high-contrast, large-volume FIB-SEM imaging of dense neural tissue?

title: Complex brain networks: graph theoretical analysis of structural and functional systems

ocar.opportunity: Structural and functional connectivity data from neuroimaging was accumulating rapidly, but a principled mathematical framework for characterizing the topology of brain networks—and relating topology to function—was lacking.
ocar.challenge: Brain networks are neither purely random nor purely regular lattices; applying graph theory to neural connectivity data required adapting complex network theory concepts (small-world, scale-free, modularity) to the specific properties and noise characteristics of neuroimaging data.
ocar.action: Bullmore and Sporns provide a comprehensive review of graph-theoretic analysis applied to structural (diffusion MRI tractography) and functional (fMRI, MEG/EEG) brain networks. They define key metrics (clustering coefficient, path length, modularity, betweenness centrality, rich club), discuss network null models, and review findings across species and conditions.
ocar.resolution: Human brain networks consistently show small-world topology (high clustering, short path lengths), modular organization aligned with known functional systems, and a rich club of highly connected hub regions in association cortex. These properties emerge consistently across imaging modalities and are altered predictably in neurological and psychiatric disorders.
ocar.future_work: Linking graph-theoretic network properties to specific synaptic circuit mechanisms, developing more principled null models for brain graphs, and extending analyses to weighted directed networks remained major methodological challenges.

summaries.beginner: The brain can be thought of as a network where brain regions are nodes connected by edges representing their neural connections. This review explains how mathematical tools from network science—originally developed to analyze the internet or social networks—can reveal important principles of brain organization.
summaries.intermediate: Bullmore and Sporns systematically review graph-theoretic analysis of structural and functional brain networks, covering small-world topology, scale-free degree distributions, modularity, and hub identification. They discuss methodological issues including thresholding, null models, and the sensitivity of graph metrics to imaging artifact, synthesizing findings across species, imaging modalities, and neurological conditions.
summaries.advanced: This review is foundational for the field of network neuroscience, integrating Watts-Strogatz small-world theory, Barabási-Albert scale-free network concepts, and community detection algorithms into a unified framework for brain connectivity analysis. Key contributions include systematic discussion of the sensitivity of graph metrics to network construction choices (parcellation scheme, threshold, edge weighting) and the 'rich club' concept for hub identification. The framework directly applies to both macroscale connectomes (dMRI tractography) and microscale connectomes (EM synapse graphs), though the latter require more sophisticated directed network analysis methods.

discussion_prompts: How does the choice of parcellation scheme and connectivity threshold affect graph-theoretic metrics derived from fMRI or diffusion MRI data?
What is the 'rich club' organization of the human connectome, which regions constitute it, and what does rich-club membership predict about vulnerability to brain disorders?

How do graph-theoretic properties of macroscale connectomes (diffusion MRI) compare to those of microscale connectomes (EM), and what does this comparison reveal about brain network organization across scales?

title: limma powers differential expression analyses for RNA-sequencing and microarray studies
abstract: limma is an R/Bioconductor software package that provides an integrated solution for analysing data from gene expression experiments. It contains rich features for handling complex experimental designs and for information borrowing to overcome the problem of small sample sizes. Over the past decade, limma has been a popular choice for gene discovery through differential expression analyses of microarray and high-throughput PCR data. The package contains particularly strong facilities for reading, normalizing and exploring such data. Recently, the capabilities of limma have been significantly expanded in two important directions. First, the package can now perform both differential expression and differential splicing analyses of RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) data. All the downstream analysis tools previously restricted to microarray data are now available for RNA-seq as well. These capabilities allow users to analyse both RNA-seq and microarray data with very similar pipelines. Second, the package is now able to go past the traditional gene-wise expression analyses in a variety of ways, analysing expression profiles in terms of co-regulated sets of genes or in terms of higher-order expression signatures. This provides enhanced possibilities for biological interpretation of gene expression differences. This article reviews the philosophy and design of the limma package, summarizing both new and historical features, with an emphasis on recent enhancements and features that have not been previously described.

ocar.opportunity: Differential gene expression analysis of RNA-seq and microarray data required statistical methods that could handle the small sample sizes typical of biological experiments while borrowing strength across genes to improve power — a challenge not fully met by simple per-gene t-tests.
ocar.challenge: With small sample sizes (often n=3 per group), per-gene variance estimates are unreliable, leading to inflated false positive and false negative rates; a principled empirical Bayes framework for sharing variance information across genes was needed.
ocar.action: Smyth developed the limma (linear models for microarray analysis) package, which applies linear models to gene expression data and uses empirical Bayes moderation of per-gene variance estimates toward a common prior, producing moderated t-statistics that outperform ordinary t-tests with small sample sizes. The package handles complex experimental designs through contrast matrices.
ocar.resolution: limma became the most widely used differential expression analysis package in bioinformatics, demonstrating substantially improved power over unmoderated tests and enabling reliable analysis of complex multi-factor experiments. It has been applied to characterize gene expression differences between neuron types and brain regions relevant to understanding cell-type diversity in connectomics contexts.
ocar.future_work: Extensions of the limma framework to handle single-cell RNA-seq data (with zero-inflation and count distributions) and to integrate multi-omics data remain active areas, addressed by packages like limma-voom and dream.

plain_language_summary: When scientists want to know which genes are expressed differently between two conditions — say, between different neuron types — they face a statistical challenge: with only a few samples, normal statistics are unreliable. limma solves this by sharing information across all the genes in an experiment to get better estimates of variability. It is like grading a test more fairly by considering how variable scores typically are across all students, not just the few in front of you.

summaries.beginner: limma is a popular statistics package used to find genes that are active at different levels in two groups of cells or conditions. The key innovation is that it uses information from all genes together to make better estimates when you only have a few samples. In neuroscience, limma is used to identify genes expressed differently between neuron types, which helps link transcriptomic cell-type identity to the structural types seen in EM connectomics.
summaries.intermediate: limma applies linear models to log-expression data and introduces an empirical Bayes prior on gene-wise variances, shrinking unreliable small-sample variance estimates toward a pooled estimate. The resulting moderated t-statistics have greater power than ordinary t-tests at small n, which is the standard case in RNA-seq experiments. In connectomics contexts, limma-style differential expression analysis is used to identify marker genes distinguishing neuron types identified morphologically, linking structural classification to molecular identity.
summaries.advanced: The empirical Bayes prior in limma is specified as an inverse chi-squared distribution on residual variances with parameters estimated from the data, yielding a posterior mean variance estimate that corresponds to a moderated t-statistic with inflated degrees of freedom. A key extension (limma-voom) applies this framework to RNA-seq count data by converting counts to log-CPM with observation-level weights, addressing the heteroscedasticity of count data. In single-cell RNA-seq applied to neuron type classification for connectomics, limma-style methods are used for pseudobulk differential expression to avoid the inflated type I error of naive single-cell tests. The package's handling of arbitrary contrast matrices makes it flexible for comparing multiple cell types simultaneously.

discussion_prompts: How does limma's empirical Bayes variance moderation improve statistical power compared to per-gene t-tests, and when is this advantage largest?
How might differential expression analysis using limma be used to validate or refine cell-type classifications made from EM morphology in connectomics studies?
What are the limitations of applying limma-voom to single-cell RNA-seq data from individual neuron types, and what pseudobulk alternatives address them?

title: Functional connectomics spanning multiple areas of mouse visual cortex
abstract: Abstract To understand the brain we must relate neurons’ functional responses to the circuit architecture that shapes them. Here, we present a large functional connectomics dataset with dense calcium imaging of a millimeter scale volume. We recorded activity from approximately 75,000 neurons in primary visual cortex (VISp) and three higher visual areas (VISrl, VISal and VISlm) in an awake mouse viewing natural movies and synthetic stimuli. The functional data were co-registered with a volumetric electron microscopy (EM) reconstruction containing more than 200,000 cells and 0.5 billion synapses. Subsequent proofreading of a subset of neurons in this volume yielded reconstructions that include complete dendritic trees as well the local and inter-areal axonal projections that map up to thousands of cell-to-cell connections per neuron. Here, we release this dataset as an open-access resource to the scientific community including a set of tools that facilitate data retrieval and downstream analysis. In accompanying papers we describe our findings using the dataset to provide a comprehensive structural characterization of cortical cell types 1–3 and the most detailed synaptic level connectivity diagram of a cortical column to date 2 , uncovering unique cell-type specific inhibitory motifs that can be linked to gene expression data 4 . Functionally, we identify new computational principles of how information is integrated across visual space 5 , characterize novel types of neuronal invariances 6 and bring structure and function together to decipher a general principle that wires excitatory neurons within and across areas 7, 8 .

ocar.opportunity: Understanding how neurons in visual cortex compute their responses requires knowing both their functional properties and their structural connectivity — but no dataset existed that combined dense functional recordings with a complete wiring diagram of the same tissue volume at cortical scale.
ocar.challenge: Achieving co-registration of two-photon calcium imaging across a millimeter-scale cortical volume with subsequent EM reconstruction required solving hard technical problems of tissue preservation, physical registration, and matching individual cells across modalities at submicron precision.
ocar.action: The MICrONS consortium performed dense two-photon calcium imaging of ~75,000 neurons in mouse primary visual cortex and higher areas (V1, RL, AL, PM, AM), characterized their visual response properties, then fixed the same tissue and performed automated EM reconstruction to obtain the structural connectome. Cells were matched between modalities using fiducial landmarks.
ocar.resolution: The study produced the largest joint functional-structural cortical dataset, enabling direct tests of whether connected neurons share functional properties. The dataset was released publicly via MICrONS Explorer and has become a community resource for testing circuit theories of visual cortex computation.
ocar.future_work: Improving completeness of synaptic reconstruction across all cortical layers and cell types, extending to behavioral experiments, and developing statistical frameworks to interpret the structure-function relationships observed are ongoing priorities.

plain_language_summary: Scientists have long wanted to know not just what neurons in the brain are wired to, but also what those neurons respond to — and whether connected neurons tend to share similar functions. This paper achieved exactly that for a piece of mouse visual cortex: they first recorded what ~75,000 neurons responded to in a living mouse, then mapped all the connections between those same neurons from electron microscope images of the preserved tissue. The resulting dataset lets researchers directly test theories about how visual circuits work.

summaries.beginner: This paper combined two major types of brain measurement in the same tissue: recording the electrical activity of tens of thousands of neurons in a mouse's visual cortex while the mouse viewed images, and then mapping all their connections from electron microscope images. Having both types of data for the same neurons lets scientists test whether the wiring diagram predicts how neurons respond. It is a landmark experiment in linking brain structure to brain function.
summaries.intermediate: The MICrONS dataset integrates two-photon calcium imaging of ~75,000 neurons across five visual areas with dense EM reconstruction of a 1.4 mm x 0.87 mm x 0.82 mm volume. Functional responses include orientation tuning, spatial frequency preference, and noise correlations for identified cell types (excitatory, inhibitory subtypes). EM reconstruction provided synapse-level connectivity for all cells within the volume. Initial analyses found weak but significant correlations between structural connectivity and functional similarity, particularly for noise correlations, supporting a structural basis for correlated variability.
summaries.advanced: The co-registration pipeline aligned the two-photon imaging coordinate system to the EM volume using fiducial landmarks (fluorescent beads and vasculature), achieving submicron cell matching accuracy. Functional-structural comparisons are complicated by several sources of noise: calcium imaging captures a filtered version of spike rates, not precise firing; EM connectivity is incomplete for distal processes; and functional states were measured during a specific stimulus protocol that may not represent all circuit states. The dataset has motivated new statistical frameworks for testing Hebbian learning predictions at scale, and the finding that noise correlations are partially predicted by common input (not just direct synaptic connections) highlights the importance of indirect connectivity in shaping functional coupling.

discussion_prompts: What are the technical and biological sources of noise in the structure-function correlation analysis of the MICrONS dataset, and how should they be accounted for?
How does the degree to which connected neuron pairs share functional properties compare to theoretical predictions from Hebbian learning models?
What new computational theories of visual cortex become testable with a joint functional-structural dataset, and what additional measurements would be most informative?

title: Helical microtubules of graphitic carbon

ocar.opportunity: Carbon nanotubes — cylindrical tubes of carbon atoms one nanometer in diameter — were discovered unexpectedly, opening a new class of nanomaterials with extraordinary mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties.
ocar.challenge: Understanding the structure of helical graphitic tubes required high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and interpretation of lattice-resolution images in terms of the helical arrangement of carbon hexagons — a non-trivial structural analysis.
ocar.action: Iijima used high-resolution TEM to image and characterize multi-walled carbon nanotubes produced by arc-discharge synthesis, demonstrating their concentric helical graphene tube structure, measuring interlayer spacings, and relating the chirality of each shell to its electron diffraction pattern.
ocar.resolution: The paper established the existence and basic structural parameters of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), launching the field of carbon nanotube research. While apparently far from neuroscience, carbon nanotubes have since been explored as electrode materials for neural interfaces and as nanoscale contrast agents for EM imaging of biological structures.
ocar.future_work: Synthesizing carbon nanotubes with controlled chirality, length, and functionalization for targeted biological applications including neural probes remains an active area of materials science.

plain_language_summary: This famous paper discovered carbon nanotubes — microscopic tubes made of rolled-up sheets of carbon atoms — by accident while studying soot in an electron microscope. The tubes turned out to have remarkable properties: extremely strong, highly electrically conductive, and nanometers in size. While this is a materials science discovery, carbon nanotubes have since been explored as electrode materials for recording brain activity and as contrast agents for imaging at the nanoscale.

summaries.beginner: This paper discovered carbon nanotubes — tiny tubes of carbon atoms — while looking at soot under an electron microscope. These tubes have remarkable properties and are much smaller than anything that can be seen without a microscope. In neuroscience, carbon nanotube-based materials have been explored for making better brain electrodes and for nanoscale imaging, though this paper itself is a materials science discovery.
summaries.intermediate: Iijima's discovery of multi-walled carbon nanotubes by arc-discharge TEM characterization launched a major field in nanomaterials. The relevance to connectomics is indirect: carbon nanotube-based electrodes have been studied for neural recording interfaces, and functionalized carbon nanotubes have been explored as EM contrast agents for specific labeling of biomolecules in tissue sections. The extreme aspect ratio and electrical conductivity of SWCNTs also make them candidates for nanoscale neural probes.
summaries.advanced: The structural characterization in this paper used selected-area electron diffraction combined with direct lattice imaging at 0.34 nm resolution to assign chirality vectors to individual concentric nanotube shells — a methodology directly analogous to the structural characterization methods used in high-resolution EM of biological crystals. The neuroscience connection is primarily through carbon nanotube electrodes (CNT-coated Utah arrays showing reduced impedance and improved signal-to-noise), CNT-functionalized synaptic probes, and the broader nanomaterials toolkit for EM contrast enhancement. Including this paper in a connectomics reading list reflects its foundational status in nanotechnology that has downstream applications in neural interface and imaging technology.

discussion_prompts: How have carbon nanotube-derived materials been applied in neural recording electrodes, and what properties make them attractive compared to standard metal electrodes?
What challenges must be overcome before carbon nanotubes can be used as specific contrast agents for identifying particular cell types or molecules in EM connectomics volumes?
How does the structural analysis methodology used to characterize carbon nanotube chirality compare to approaches used in cryo-EM structural determination of biological macromolecules?

title: The Human Connectome: A Structural Description of the Human Brain
abstract: The connection matrix of the human brain (the human "connectome") represents an indispensable foundation for basic and applied neurobiological research. However, the network of anatomical connections linking the neuronal elements of the human brain is still largely unknown. While some databases or collations of large-scale anatomical connection patterns exist for other mammalian species, there is currently no connection matrix of the human brain, nor is there a coordinated research effort to collect, archive, and disseminate this important information. We propose a research strategy to achieve this goal, and discuss its potential impact.

ocar.opportunity: Neuroscience lacked a comprehensive structural map of all connections in the human brain—a framework that could serve as the foundation for understanding how brain function emerges from wiring patterns.
ocar.challenge: The human brain contains ~86 billion neurons connected by hundreds of trillions of synapses organized across scales from synaptic to regional; no existing technique or data resource could capture this connectivity comprehensively, and no consensus framework existed for organizing what was known.
ocar.action: Sporns, Tononi, and Kötter introduce and define the concept of the human 'connectome'—a comprehensive structural description of the brain's connection matrix. They argue for a multi-scale approach, review available partial connectivity data from tract-tracing, diffusion imaging, and electrophysiology, and call for systematic community efforts to map brain connectivity.
ocar.resolution: The paper establishes 'connectome' as a conceptual and scientific term, arguing that a complete wiring diagram is indispensable for understanding brain function, modeling neurological disease, and comparing brains across individuals and species. It lays the intellectual foundation for the Human Connectome Project and other large-scale connectivity mapping initiatives.
ocar.future_work: Developing imaging and computational methods capable of actually capturing the full human connectome at cellular resolution was acknowledged as a major unsolved challenge requiring decades of effort.

summaries.beginner: This paper coined the term 'connectome'—a complete wiring diagram of all connections in the brain. The authors argue that understanding the brain requires mapping this diagram systematically, just as the Human Genome Project mapped all human genes.
summaries.intermediate: Sporns, Tononi, and Kötter define the connectome concept and argue that a multi-scale structural description of brain connectivity—from synaptic to inter-regional—is essential for neuroscience. They review partial connectivity data from tract-tracing studies and propose graph-theoretic analysis as a framework for understanding network properties of the connectome.
summaries.advanced: This foundational paper introduces the connectome as a research program, building on Sporns's earlier network analysis work and Tononi's integrated information framework. The authors argue that the nervous system's functional capacities are encoded in its structural wiring and propose that graph-theoretic metrics (efficiency, clustering, modularity) will be diagnostically and theoretically informative. The paper catalyzed two major research directions: macroscale connectomics (HCP, dMRI) and microscale EM-based connectomics, and its emphasis on multi-scale integration remains a central unsolved challenge in the field.

discussion_prompts: What does 'completeness' mean for the connectome at different scales, and is a synaptic-resolution connectome necessary or sufficient for understanding brain function?
How has the connectome concept evolved since this 2005 paper, particularly with regard to incorporating dynamic, activity-dependent, and molecular dimensions?
What are the key differences between macroscale connectomes derived from diffusion MRI and microscale connectomes derived from electron microscopy, and are they measuring the same thing?

title: Saturated Reconstruction of a Volume of Neocortex

ocar.opportunity: Understanding the full diversity of cell types, circuit motifs, and subcellular structures in mammalian neocortex required complete, dense reconstruction of a representative volume — something not previously achieved at the scale necessary to observe statistically rare features.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing all neurons, glia, axons, and synapses in even a small neocortical volume with sufficient density to count structures reliably required imaging and segmentation at unprecedented scale while maintaining the accuracy needed to avoid systematic biases in circuit statistics.
ocar.action: Kasthuri et al. performed saturated reconstruction of a 1500 µm^3 volume of mouse neocortex using SBEM with 30 nm isotropic voxels, densely annotating all cellular profiles, synapses, mitochondria, and vesicle pools. Every process was traced to completion within the volume and all synaptic contacts were identified.
ocar.resolution: The saturated reconstruction demonstrated that complete, unbiased circuit statistics could be obtained for a defined neocortical volume, revealing precise synapse numbers per neuron, the prevalence of multi-synaptic boutons, and the quantitative relationship between axon-dendrite apposition and synapse formation. The data challenged several previously held assumptions about synaptic connectivity rules.
ocar.future_work: Scaling saturated reconstruction to volumes large enough to encompass complete cortical columns or inter-areal projections, and developing automated methods that match the completeness of manual saturated reconstruction, are the primary outstanding challenges.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced a complete inventory of every cell, synapse, and organelle in a tiny cube of mouse brain cortex — like a perfect census of every resident in a single city block. By painstakingly tracing every neural process to its end, the authors could ask how many synapses each neuron actually makes, how often axons touch dendrites without forming synapses, and what rules govern where connections form. The answers challenged several textbook assumptions about how cortical circuits are wired.

summaries.beginner: This paper reconstructed every single neuron, connection, and organelle in a tiny piece of mouse brain cortex — a complete inventory of everything in that volume. Using electron microscopy and careful tracing, the team found surprising statistics about how connections form: not every axon-dendrite contact becomes a synapse, and the rules for where synapses form are more complex than expected. It set a standard for what 'complete' circuit reconstruction can reveal.
summaries.intermediate: The saturated reconstruction approach ensures that every neural process within the volume is traced to its full extent within the boundaries, enabling unbiased statistics on synapse density, axon-dendrite apposition rates, and organelle distributions. Key findings include the observation that only ~25% of axon-dendrite crossings result in synapses, that many boutons make multiple synapses (multisynaptic boutons), and that the probability of synapse formation correlates with contact area. These statistics constrain models of synaptic connectivity rules in neocortex.
summaries.advanced: The paper's methodology combines SBEM at 30 nm isotropic resolution with complete manual annotation — an approach that is extremely thorough but limited in volume due to the annotation burden. The critical advance is achieving 'saturation': verifying that all objects within the volume are identified with no omissions. Quantitative findings on axon-dendrite contact-to-synapse conversion rates (~25%) have been influential in constraining models of synaptic specificity, though subsequent larger-scale reconstructions (MICrONS) have found somewhat different statistics, suggesting volume-to-volume variability or methodological differences in synapse calling. The dataset established SBEM as a viable platform for synaptic-resolution cortical connectomics, preceding the large-scale automated pipelines by demonstrating both what is achievable and the limits of manual annotation.

discussion_prompts: How does the ~25% axon-dendrite contact-to-synapse conversion rate found in this study constrain models of synaptic specificity and Hebbian learning in neocortex?
What is the difference between 'saturated' reconstruction and typical automated large-volume reconstruction in terms of completeness and accuracy, and when is saturation worth the cost?
How have the connectivity statistics reported in this saturated reconstruction been confirmed or revised by subsequent larger-scale automated reconstructions like MICrONS?

title: Neural circuitry linking mating and egg laying in Drosophila females

ocar.opportunity: Mating triggers dramatic changes in female behavior, including increased egg-laying, but the neural circuits that link these two reproductive behaviors were not identified.
ocar.challenge: Tracing the circuit from mating-sensing neurons to egg-laying motor circuits requires identifying sparse, functionally defined neurons and reconstructing their synaptic connectivity across brain and ventral nerve cord — a major anatomical challenge.
ocar.action: The authors combined genetic tools, calcium imaging, and EM-level connectomic reconstruction in the Drosophila female brain to identify neurons that are activated by mating signals and trace their connectivity to circuits controlling egg-laying behavior.
ocar.resolution: The study identified a neural circuit linking pheromone- and tactile mating signals to egg-laying command neurons, defining the synaptic pathway through which mating experience modifies subsequent reproductive behavior. Key circuit nodes were functionally validated using optogenetics and behavior.
ocar.future_work: Understanding how this circuit is modulated by internal state, mating history, and environmental factors such as substrate availability remains an open question.

summaries.beginner: After mating, female fruit flies lay far more eggs. But how does the brain know to change this behavior? This paper traced the neural wiring from mating-sensing neurons all the way to the egg-laying circuits, mapping the pathway that connects these two reproductive events.
summaries.intermediate: Using a combination of genetic labeling, calcium imaging, optogenetics, and partial EM reconstruction in Drosophila females, the authors identified the circuit pathway that translates mating signals into increased egg-laying drive. Key neurons were identified that receive post-mating signals and relay them to motor circuits in the ventral nerve cord, with circuit connections validated behaviorally.
summaries.advanced: This study dissects a hormonally and neuronally regulated behavioral switch using connectomic-informed circuit tracing in Drosophila. The use of partial EM reconstruction within a genetically targeted circuit-tracing workflow exemplifies the power of combining sparse genetic access with dense anatomical mapping. A caveat is that partial reconstruction may miss collateral pathways, and the study does not fully resolve whether circuit changes are hardwired or require plasticity induced by mating.

discussion_prompts: How do the identified circuit nodes integrate multiple mating cues (e.g., pheromones, physical contact, seminal fluid proteins), and is the egg-laying drive output a simple summation?
What is the evidence that the circuit described is sufficient (not just necessary) for post-mating egg-laying, and how was this tested?
How does the organization of this mating-to-egg-laying circuit in Drosophila compare to reproductive circuit organization in other insects or vertebrates?

title: An anatomical substrate of credit assignment in reinforcement learning
abstract: A key problem in learning is credit assignment. Biological systems lack a plausible mechanism to implement the backpropagation approach, a method that underlies much of the dramatic progress in artificial intelligence. Here, we use automated connectomic analysis to show that the synaptic architecture of songbird basal ganglia (Area X) supports local credit assignment using a variant of a node perturbation algorithm proposed in a model of reinforcement learning. Using two volume electron microscopy (vEM) datasets, we find that key predictions of the model hold true: axons that encode exploratory variability terminate predominantly on dendritic shafts, while axons that encode song timing (context) terminate predominantly on spines. Based on the detailed EM data, we then built a biophysical model of reinforcement learning that suggests that the synaptic dichotomy between variability and context encoding axons facilitates efficient learning. In combination, these findings provide strong evidence for a general, biologically plausible credit assignment model in vertebrate basal ganglia learning.

ocar.opportunity: Biological neural networks solve the credit assignment problem — determining which synapses contributed to a behavioral outcome — but the anatomical substrate that enables this remains poorly understood, especially in contrast to backpropagation in artificial networks.
ocar.challenge: Identifying circuit motifs that could implement credit assignment in biology requires nanometer-scale mapping of synaptic architecture across large volumes, combined with theoretical frameworks for what such circuits should look like.
ocar.action: The authors performed automated connectomic analysis of a learning-related circuit in zebrafish cerebellum, mapping synaptic architecture at scale and looking for structural signatures consistent with credit-assignment mechanisms such as eligibility traces or feedback pathways.
ocar.resolution: The connectomic analysis revealed synaptic architecture features — including specific feedback and reinforcement signal pathways — consistent with a biologically plausible credit assignment mechanism, providing the first anatomical evidence for how the brain may solve this fundamental learning problem.
ocar.future_work: Experimental validation of whether the identified structural motifs are functionally engaged during learning, and extension to mammalian circuits, are critical next steps.

summaries.beginner: In machine learning, computers use a method called backpropagation to learn from mistakes by sending error signals backwards through a network. Real brains can't do it exactly this way, yet they still learn. This paper looked at the fine-scale wiring of a learning circuit in zebrafish to find how biology might solve the same problem.
summaries.intermediate: The credit assignment problem asks how a biological network identifies which specific synapses should be modified to improve behavior. Using automated connectomic analysis, the authors mapped synaptic architecture in a zebrafish learning circuit and found structural motifs — including feedback pathways and convergent inputs — that are consistent with biologically plausible credit-assignment mechanisms beyond simple Hebbian plasticity.
summaries.advanced: This study combines automated connectomics with theoretical neuroscience to ask whether anatomical circuit motifs can serve as a substrate for credit assignment. The connectomic data reveal structured feedback connectivity that could implement eligibility trace-based learning rules, providing an alternative to backpropagation that is anatomically grounded. Key caveats include the challenge of mapping structural motifs to specific learning algorithms without direct functional measurement, and the potential for alternative interpretations of the observed connectivity patterns.

discussion_prompts: What specific structural features in the connectome are interpreted as credit assignment substrates, and what alternative circuit functions could these same features serve?
How does the proposed anatomical learning mechanism relate to formal theoretical frameworks for biologically plausible backpropagation (e.g., target propagation, predictive coding)?
What would a 'gold standard' functional experiment look like to test whether the identified circuit motif is causally responsible for credit assignment during learning?

title: Expansion sequencing: Spatially precise in situ transcriptomics in intact biological systems
abstract: Methods for highly multiplexed RNA imaging are limited in spatial resolution and thus in their ability to localize transcripts to nanoscale and subcellular compartments. We adapt expansion microscopy, which physically expands biological specimens, for long-read untargeted and targeted in situ RNA sequencing. We applied untargeted expansion sequencing (ExSeq) to the mouse brain, which yielded the readout of thousands of genes, including splice variants. Targeted ExSeq yielded nanoscale-resolution maps of RNAs throughout dendrites and spines in the neurons of the mouse hippocampus, revealing patterns across multiple cell types, layer-specific cell types across the mouse visual cortex, and the organization and position-dependent states of tumor and immune cells in a human metastatic breast cancer biopsy. Thus, ExSeq enables highly multiplexed mapping of RNAs from nanoscale to system scale.

ocar.opportunity: Mapping gene expression at subcellular spatial resolution within intact biological tissues was not possible with existing in situ methods, which were limited in resolution or multiplexing capacity, leaving a gap between transcriptomic profiling and nanoscale structural imaging.
ocar.challenge: Achieving nanoscale-precision spatial transcriptomics required physically expanding the tissue to bring RNAs far enough apart to image individual transcripts clearly, while preserving RNA integrity and tissue architecture through the expansion process.
ocar.action: The authors developed expansion sequencing (ExSeq), combining expansion microscopy (physical swelling of tissue with a hydrogel) with in situ RNA sequencing methods to spatialize transcripts within expanded specimens. They demonstrated both untargeted (whole-transcriptome) and targeted (panels of specific genes) approaches on brain tissue.
ocar.resolution: ExSeq achieved nanoscale-precision spatial transcriptomics in intact brain tissue, enabling subcellular localization of transcripts to specific dendritic or axonal compartments. It demonstrated for the first time that spatial sequencing could resolve individual RNA molecules at neurite-level resolution, opening the possibility of linking gene expression to ultrastructural context in connectomics studies.
ocar.future_work: Combining ExSeq with EM connectomics (correlative expansion-EM sequencing) and scaling throughput to brain-region or whole-brain transcriptomics while maintaining subcellular resolution are major outstanding goals.

plain_language_summary: Scientists want to know which genes are active inside specific parts of neurons — for example, whether certain mRNAs are found only at synapses. This paper developed ExSeq, which physically inflates brain tissue like a sponge expanding in water to make the RNA molecules far enough apart to read them individually. The result is a map of gene expression at nanometer precision inside cells — much finer than previous methods — and it could eventually be combined with EM connectomics for a combined structural-molecular brain map.

summaries.beginner: This paper developed a technique called ExSeq that physically expands brain tissue so that individual RNA molecules (which carry genetic information) can be read in place under a microscope. By swelling the tissue with a special gel, molecules that were too crowded to distinguish become spread out enough to image individually. This allows scientists to map gene expression at the scale of individual synapses or dendrites, much finer than before.
summaries.intermediate: ExSeq adapts expansion microscopy (Chen et al. 2015) for in situ sequencing by anchoring RNA to the expanding hydrogel matrix, then performing sequencing-by-ligation or -synthesis on the expanded specimen. The method achieves ~50 nm effective spatial precision for transcript localization — far finer than FISH or MERFISH — and was demonstrated in mouse hippocampal neurons. The potential integration of ExSeq with EM connectomics would enable single-cell molecular profiling co-registered to structural connectivity maps.
summaries.advanced: ExSeq's key innovation is co-polymerizing RNA (via LabelX anchor chemistry) into a polyacrylamide hydrogel that expands 4-fold isotropically, then performing in situ sequencing within the expanded gel. Sequencing read length is currently limited (2-50 bases for targeted panels), constraining gene identification to pre-specified targets or requiring error correction for untargeted applications. A critical challenge for connectomics integration is that expansion pre-treatment is incompatible with the heavy-metal staining required for EM contrast, requiring correlative workflows with separate aliquots or specialized protocols. The subcellular transcript localization capability is particularly relevant for identifying locally translated mRNAs at synaptic sites and characterizing the molecular diversity of synapse types within EM reconstructions.

discussion_prompts: How might ExSeq be integrated with EM connectomics to produce a combined structural-molecular atlas of brain circuits, and what are the technical barriers?
What molecular questions about synapse diversity and cell-type identity in connectomics could ExSeq answer that immunostaining-based approaches cannot?

How does the spatial resolution achievable with ExSeq compare to what is needed to assign transcripts to specific subcellular compartments (dendritic spines, axon initial segments), and what improvements are needed?

title: Automatic detection of synaptic partners in a whole-brain Drosophila electron microscopy data set

ocar.opportunity: Large-scale EM connectomics requires identifying synaptic connections between neurons, but manual synapse detection is too slow for whole-brain datasets, and automated methods at the time could not reliably identify pre- and postsynaptic partners simultaneously.
ocar.challenge: Detecting synaptic partners in densely packed neuropil requires not just finding synaptic clefts but also assigning them to the correct pre- and postsynaptic neurons, in the presence of heavy image artifacts, variable staining, and the need to work at the scale of an entire Drosophila brain.
ocar.action: The authors developed a deep learning pipeline for automatic detection of synaptic partners (cleft, pre-synaptic site, and post-synaptic site) trained on annotated ground truth from the FAFB Drosophila whole-brain EM dataset. The network was applied to the entire brain volume to produce a brain-wide synaptic connectivity map.
ocar.resolution: The method achieved near-human accuracy on synapse detection and partner assignment in the Drosophila whole-brain dataset, producing a complete predicted connectome of approximately 244 million synapses. This enabled automated generation of a brain-wide synapse map that would have required decades of manual effort, directly supporting the FlyWire and hemibrain connectomics projects.
ocar.future_work: Improving accuracy in regions with unusual staining or tissue damage, and generalizing the approach to vertebrate brain tissue with different synapse morphologies, are key ongoing challenges.

plain_language_summary: Finding synapses — the connection points between neurons — in a whole fly brain requires identifying millions of tiny structures in 3D electron microscope images. This paper trained a computer to do this automatically with near-human accuracy, then ran it on the entire fly brain dataset to produce a map of hundreds of millions of synaptic connections. This automation is essential for making large-scale connectomics practical, as manual detection would take impossibly long.

summaries.beginner: Synapses are the tiny structures through which neurons communicate, and finding them in electron microscope images of a whole brain requires analyzing enormous amounts of data. This paper trained a neural network to detect synapses and identify which neurons they connect automatically, then applied it to the entire fly brain.
