Functional experiments confirmed the roles of these cells, directly linking structural connectivity to a well-defined computation.
ocar.future_work: The mechanism by which temporal filtering is implemented within this circuit — a critical requirement of the Reichardt model — and the roles of additional columnar neuron types remain to be fully characterized.

plain_language_summary: Flies detect motion by comparing what they saw a moment ago with what they see now — like comparing two video frames. This paper used microscopy to map the exact neurons and connections responsible for this computation in the fly's visual system, then genetically tested those connections to confirm which cells actually do the motion detection.

summaries.beginner: Flies can tell which direction something is moving using their visual system. Scientists had theories about how this works but didn't know exactly which neurons were involved. This paper mapped the connections in the fly's visual brain using electron microscopy and then tested which neurons are actually needed for detecting motion.
summaries.intermediate: This connectomics study mapped synaptic connectivity in the Drosophila medulla, identifying the cellular implementation of the Reichardt motion detector. Reconstruction revealed candidate pathways through Mi1, Tm3, Mi4, and Mi9 neurons, and genetic functional experiments confirmed their roles in direction-selective motion detection in vivo.
summaries.advanced: This paper represents an early and influential example of using connectomics to generate and test mechanistic circuit hypotheses. By reconstructing a medulla column from serial-section EM and combining this with genetic manipulation of identified cell types, the authors identified the anatomical correlates of the two-arm Reichardt correlator for motion detection. The approach established a template for structure-function studies in Drosophila visual circuits, though the limited volume prevented reconstruction of full arbors and the functional experiments relied on population-level behavioral assays rather than single-cell recordings.

discussion_prompts: How does the connectivity map from this study compare to more recent comprehensive reconstructions of the Drosophila optic lobe, and what new circuit elements have been identified?
What aspects of the Reichardt correlator model could not be inferred from connectivity alone and required functional experiments — and what does this tell us about the limits of connectomics?
How does this paper's approach of combining connectomics with genetic circuit dissection serve as a model for structure-function studies in more complex brains?

title: Object recognition from local scale-invariant features
abstract: An object recognition system has been developed that uses a new class of local image features. The features are invariant to image scaling, translation, and rotation, and partially invariant to illumination changes and affine or 3D projection. These features share similar properties with neurons in inferior temporal cortex that are used for object recognition in primate vision. Features are efficiently detected through a staged filtering approach that identifies stable points in scale space. Image keys are created that allow for local geometric deformations by representing blurred image gradients in multiple orientation planes and at multiple scales. The keys are used as input to a nearest neighbor indexing method that identifies candidate object matches. Final verification of each match is achieved by finding a low residual least squares solution for the unknown model parameters. Experimental results show that robust object recognition can be achieved in cluttered partially occluded images with a computation time of under 2 seconds.
concepts: Cognitive neuroscience of visual object recognition

ocar.opportunity: Computer vision and image analysis tasks — including the automated image registration, segmentation, and feature matching central to connectomics pipelines — required robust feature descriptors that remain stable across changes in scale, rotation, and illumination. Existing methods failed in the face of such transformations.
ocar.challenge: Natural images and microscopy data contain features at multiple scales, and the same structure can appear very different under rotation or intensity changes. Creating descriptors that are truly invariant to these transformations while remaining discriminative enough to match corresponding features reliably was an open problem.
ocar.action: Lowe developed the Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT), which detects keypoints at characteristic scales using a difference-of-Gaussians pyramid, then computes a locally orientation-normalized gradient histogram descriptor at each keypoint. The algorithm was applied to object recognition tasks with large viewpoint and scale changes.
ocar.resolution: SIFT features proved highly robust to image scaling, rotation, partial illumination changes, and modest affine distortions, achieving reliable matching across large appearance changes. The algorithm became one of the most influential image analysis tools in computer vision and was widely adopted in microscopy image registration pipelines used in connectomics.
ocar.future_work: Deep learning-based feature descriptors have increasingly supplanted hand-crafted features like SIFT for many tasks, though SIFT remains valuable in domains with limited training data.

plain_language_summary: Before neural networks dominated computer vision, this paper invented a smarter way for computers to recognize the same object even when it appears larger, smaller, or rotated. SIFT features work by finding distinctive local patterns and describing them in a way that is stable across such changes — a technique that became fundamental to the automated image alignment used in connectomics.

summaries.beginner: When a computer needs to match the same point in two different images — even when one image is rotated or a different size — it needs a smart descriptor. This paper invented SIFT, a method that identifies distinctive spots in images and describes them in a way that stays the same even when the image changes scale or orientation.
summaries.intermediate: SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform) detects and describes local image features using a difference-of-Gaussians scale space and orientation-normalized gradient histograms. The resulting descriptors are invariant to scale and rotation and robust to illumination changes, making them foundational for image registration — including the cross-section alignment pipelines central to serial-section EM connectomics.
summaries.advanced: SIFT constructs a scale-space extrema detector via DoG pyramids and computes 128-dimensional gradient orientation histograms at dominant orientation-normalized patches. Its invariance properties make it directly applicable to serial-section EM alignment, where sections may differ in rotation, scaling, and intensity between imaging sessions. While deep learning (e.g., SuperPoint, LoFTR) now provides superior performance in data-rich regimes, SIFT remains a robust baseline for registration problems with limited training examples and is embedded in many legacy connectomics preprocessing pipelines.

discussion_prompts: How are SIFT-like features used in EM image stack registration for connectomics, and where do they fail compared to deep learning-based alignment methods?
What properties of SIFT make it well-suited for cross-section alignment in serial-section electron microscopy?
In what ways has the emergence of deep feature descriptors (e.g., learned keypoints) improved over SIFT for connectomics-relevant image analysis tasks?

title: Neurotransmitter Classification from Electron Microscopy Images at Synaptic Sites in Drosophila Melanogaster
abstract: Abstract High-resolution electron microscopy of nervous systems enables the reconstruction of connectomes. A key piece of missing information from connectomes is the synaptic sign. We show that for D. melanogaster , artificial neural networks can predict the transmitter type released at synapses from electron micrographs and thus add putative signs to connections. Our network discriminates between six transmitters (acetylcholine, glutamate, GABA, serotonin, dopamine, octopamine) with an average accuracy of 87%/94% for synapses/entire neurons. We developed an explainability method to reveal which features our network is using and found significant ultrastructural differences between the classical transmitters. We predict transmitters in two connectomes and characterize morphological and connection properties of tens of thousands of neurons classed by predicted transmitter expression. We find that hemilineages in D. melanogaster largely express only one fastacting transmitter among their neurons. Furthermore, we show that neurons with different transmitters may differ in features like polarization and projection targets.

ocar.opportunity: Connectomes provide structural wiring diagrams but lack information about synaptic sign (excitatory vs. inhibitory), a fundamental missing piece that prevents inferring circuit function from anatomy alone.
ocar.challenge: Determining neurotransmitter identity from EM images is challenging because synaptic vesicle morphology alone is not sufficient to classify all transmitter types, and biochemical assays are incompatible with the EM processing required for connectomics.
ocar.action: The authors trained convolutional neural networks to predict the neurotransmitter type released at individual synapses in Drosophila melanogaster directly from EM image patches of synaptic sites, using a training set where transmitter identity was known from genetic and immunohistochemical data.
ocar.resolution: The CNNs successfully classified multiple neurotransmitter types — including acetylcholine, GABA, glutamate, dopamine, serotonin, and octopamine — from EM images with high accuracy. This method can be applied to existing EM datasets to add synaptic sign information to connectomes without additional experimental labeling.
ocar.future_work: Extending the approach to mammalian systems where transmitter-EM correlates differ, and improving accuracy for rare transmitter types, are important future directions.

summaries.beginner: When neurons communicate, they release chemical signals called neurotransmitters, and knowing which chemical a neuron uses tells us if it's exciting or inhibiting its partners. Electron microscopy gives us beautiful images of neural connections but doesn't reveal the chemistry. This paper trained AI to predict the chemical identity of synapses just from looking at their EM images.
summaries.intermediate: The authors trained convolutional neural networks on annotated synaptic sites in Drosophila EM data to predict neurotransmitter identity from image features. By leveraging known genetic cell type labels as ground truth, they achieved multi-class transmitter prediction (ACh, GABA, Glu, DA, 5-HT, Oct) with high accuracy. Applied to whole-brain datasets, this transforms connectomes from pure wiring diagrams into signed graphs with excitatory/inhibitory labels.
summaries.advanced: This work addresses a fundamental limitation of connectomics by using deep learning to infer neurotransmitter phenotype from ultrastructural image features at synaptic profiles. The key insight is that transmitter-specific differences in vesicle morphology, active zone structure, and cytoplasmic texture are learnable by CNNs even when not obvious to human annotators. A critical caveat is that the approach was validated in Drosophila where transmitter-to-cell-type mappings are relatively stereotyped; generalization to mammals, where cotransmission and plasticity-dependent changes in transmitter expression are more common, requires careful evaluation.

discussion_prompts: What ultrastructural features does the CNN appear to use for transmitter classification, and are these features interpretable in terms of known biology (e.g., vesicle size, shape, density)?
How does neurotransmitter prediction accuracy vary across transmitter classes, and what biological or imaging factors contribute to the harder-to-predict cases?
What new circuit analyses become possible when synaptic sign is added to a connectome, and what classic neuroscience questions could be revisited with signed whole-brain graphs?

title: The Mind of a Mouse

ocar.opportunity: The mouse has become the dominant mammalian model organism in neuroscience, yet a complete cellular-resolution connectome of even a small mouse brain region remained out of reach. This perspective argued that creating a full mouse brain connectome was both scientifically necessary and technically achievable.
ocar.challenge: The mouse brain contains roughly 70 million neurons and trillions of synapses. Imaging and reconstructing it at synaptic resolution using electron microscopy would require unprecedented data volumes, automated reconstruction pipelines, and analysis frameworks far beyond what had been demonstrated.
ocar.action: This perspective paper made the scientific and strategic case for pursuing a complete mouse brain connectome, surveying the current state of EM technology, automated segmentation, and proofreading, and estimating the resources required. It argued that the scientific payoff in understanding mammalian neural computation would justify the investment.
ocar.resolution: The paper galvanized community discussion and planning around mouse connectomics, contributing to a roadmap for tackling larger mammalian nervous systems. It identified the key technological bottlenecks — particularly automated reconstruction accuracy and proofreading throughput — as tractable near-term challenges.
ocar.future_work: A complete mouse brain connectome remains an active goal; partial reconstructions of mouse cortex at petabyte scale have since been achieved, but whole-brain synaptic-resolution connectivity in mouse is still in progress.

plain_language_summary: This paper argued that now is the time to attempt mapping the entire mouse brain at the level of individual connections between neurons. Like a rallying call to build a new kind of telescope, it laid out why this was worth doing and what it would take — even though the project would generate more data than any previous neuroscience experiment.

summaries.beginner: The mouse is one of the most studied animals in brain research. This paper argued that scientists should try to map every single connection in a mouse brain, explaining why this would transform neuroscience and what tools would be needed to make it possible.
summaries.intermediate: This perspective paper advocated for a complete synaptic-resolution connectome of the mouse brain, surveying the technological landscape of EM acquisition, automated segmentation, and proofreading. It argued that recent advances made the project tractable and that understanding mammalian neural computation required this level of structural detail.
summaries.advanced: Written as a community call-to-arms, this paper assessed the feasibility of mouse whole-brain connectomics by estimating data volumes (~1 exabyte), reconstruction error rates of existing automated pipelines, and proofreading throughput requirements. It identified automated segmentation accuracy and error correction throughput as the rate-limiting bottlenecks, and made a scientific case that mammalian circuit understanding — particularly for learning, memory, and disease — requires connectivity data at synaptic resolution. The paper influenced funding priorities and collaborative structures in the field.

discussion_prompts: What scientific questions could be answered by a complete mouse brain connectome that cannot be addressed with existing partial reconstructions or other methods?
What are the most significant technical bottlenecks still preventing a complete mouse brain connectome, and how close is the field to overcoming them?

How should the neuroscience community prioritize resources between expanding the scale of connectomics (toward whole mouse brain) versus deepening functional characterization of already-mapped circuits?

title: Auditory activity is diverse and widespread throughout the central brain of Drosophila

ocar.opportunity: Auditory processing in the Drosophila brain was poorly mapped: which neurons respond to sound, and how is auditory information distributed and integrated across brain regions? A systematic survey was needed to characterize the full extent of auditory representation.
ocar.challenge: The Drosophila brain contains thousands of neuron types, and identifying which ones respond to auditory stimuli required large-scale functional imaging across the entire brain — a technically demanding task given the small size of the fly brain and the need to present controlled acoustic stimuli during imaging.
ocar.action: The authors performed whole-brain two-photon calcium imaging in Drosophila while presenting auditory stimuli including courtship song, and characterized the spatial distribution, tuning properties, and diversity of auditory-responsive neurons across the brain. They correlated functional responses with anatomical locations.
ocar.resolution: Auditory responses were found to be remarkably widespread throughout the Drosophila central brain, distributed across many regions beyond the primary auditory center (AMMC/AVLP). The study revealed diverse tuning to different features of auditory stimuli and identified candidate populations for different aspects of acoustic processing including courtship.
ocar.future_work: Future work should determine how this widespread auditory representation is integrated to guide specific behaviors, and how auditory circuits interact with circuits for other sensory modalities.

plain_language_summary: Fruit flies use sound to communicate — males court females by singing with their wings. This paper mapped which neurons throughout the fly brain respond to sounds by imaging brain activity across the whole brain during acoustic stimulation. The surprising finding was that sound responses are spread across many brain regions, not confined to one 'ear center'.

summaries.beginner: Flies can hear sounds, especially the courtship songs that males produce to attract females. This paper mapped which neurons in the fly brain respond to sound by imaging brain activity while playing sounds to flies. They found that sound processing is spread across many parts of the brain, not just in one hearing area.
summaries.intermediate: Using whole-brain two-photon calcium imaging, the authors surveyed auditory-responsive neurons throughout the Drosophila central brain during presentation of broadband sounds and courtship song. The results revealed unexpectedly widespread auditory representation beyond primary acoustic centers, with diverse spectrotemporal tuning properties and putative functions in different aspects of acoustic behavior.
summaries.advanced: This whole-brain functional imaging survey characterized auditory representation in Drosophila using GCaMP-based calcium indicators and two-photon microscopy. The breadth of auditory-responsive regions identified — spanning AMMC, AVLP, LH, and other areas — challenges a strictly hierarchical model of auditory processing. The paper provides a functional map that serves as a reference for subsequent connectomic analyses of auditory circuits, though limitations include the inability to resolve individual cell types and the coarse temporal resolution of calcium indicators relative to neural dynamics.

discussion_prompts: What does the widespread distribution of auditory responses across the Drosophila brain suggest about the organization of sensory processing in small brains?
How has subsequent connectomic analysis of the Drosophila brain expanded or refined the picture of auditory circuit organization suggested by this functional mapping study?
What are the advantages and limitations of whole-brain calcium imaging versus electrophysiology for characterizing sensory processing across brain regions?

title: Neuronal wiring diagram of an adult brain
abstract: Connections between neurons can be mapped by acquiring and analyzing electron microscopic (EM) brain images. In recent years, this approach has been applied to chunks of brains to reconstruct local connectivity maps that are highly informative, yet inadequate for understanding brain function more globally. Here, we present the first neuronal wiring diagram of a whole adult brain, containing 5×107 chemical synapses between ~130,000 neurons reconstructed from a female Drosophila melanogaster. The resource also incorporates annotations of cell classes and types, nerves, hemilineages, and predictions of neurotransmitter identities. Data products are available by download, programmatic access, and interactive browsing and made interoperable with other fly data resources. We show how to derive a projectome, a map of projections between regions, from the connectome. We demonstrate the tracing of synaptic pathways and the analysis of information flow from inputs (sensory and ascending neurons) to outputs (motor, endocrine, and descending neurons), across both hemispheres, and between the central brain and the optic lobes. Tracing from a subset of photoreceptors all the way to descending motor pathways illustrates how structure can uncover putative circuit mechanisms underlying sensorimotor behaviors. The technologies and open ecosystem of the FlyWire Consortium set the stage for future large-scale connectome projects in other species.

ocar.opportunity: Understanding how the brain generates behavior requires knowing not just local circuit motifs but the complete synaptic wiring of the entire brain. No such complete neuronal wiring diagram existed for any adult complex brain, leaving fundamental questions about global information flow unanswered.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing a complete adult brain connectome at synaptic resolution requires acquiring petabytes of EM images, segmenting billions of voxels into individual neurons, proofreading thousands of reconstruction errors, and building data infrastructure capable of storing and querying the resulting graph of millions of synapses.
ocar.action: The authors acquired FIB-SEM and transmission EM image volumes of the adult Drosophila brain, applied automated segmentation with extensive proofreading, and assembled a complete wiring diagram containing approximately 130,000 neurons and 15 million synapses. They characterized cell types, connectivity motifs, and information flow using graph analysis.
ocar.resolution: This work produced the first complete neuronal wiring diagram of an adult brain, the Drosophila connectome (FlyWire/hemibrain-scale). The dataset revealed cell-type-specific connectivity rules, brain-wide circuit pathways, and organizational principles governing sensory-to-motor information flow, providing an unprecedented resource for understanding neural computation.
ocar.future_work: Functional validation of the connectivity-derived predictions, extension to other developmental stages or mutant backgrounds, and comparison with the second hemisphere remain important next steps.

plain_language_summary: This paper completed the ultimate neuroscience jigsaw puzzle — mapping every single connection between every neuron in the brain of an adult fly. With 130,000 neurons and 15 million synapses recorded, it is like having a complete subway map for the brain instead of just knowing a few important lines, enabling researchers to trace any information pathway from sensory input to motor output.

summaries.beginner: This paper created the first complete wiring diagram of an adult animal's brain — the fruit fly's brain — showing every connection between every neuron. With about 130,000 neurons and millions of connections mapped, it is a landmark achievement that lets scientists trace how information flows from the eyes or nose all the way to motor commands.
summaries.intermediate: The authors present the first complete adult brain connectome, reconstructed from EM images of Drosophila melanogaster. The dataset contains ~130,000 neurons and ~15 million synapses, with cell types annotated and connectivity analyzed at the brain-wide level. This resource enables systematic analysis of sensorimotor pathways, circuit motifs, and inter-area communication throughout the brain.
summaries.advanced: This landmark paper describes the reconstruction pipeline and initial analyses of the Drosophila adult whole-brain connectome. Petabyte-scale EM acquisition was combined with flood-fill network segmentation, automated synapse detection, and extensive community-based proofreading via FlyWire. Analyses revealed hierarchical organization of information flow, recurrent circuit motifs, and bilateral structural symmetry with quantifiable asymmetries. Key caveats include reconstruction errors in small-caliber neurites, incomplete coverage of the optic lobes, and the challenge of interpreting the functional significance of purely structural connectivity.

discussion_prompts: What new organizational principles of the brain became visible only when the complete wiring diagram was available, that could not have been seen from partial reconstructions?
How should the field approach the problem of validating functional predictions derived from this structural connectome?
What are the next scale milestones for connectomics — in terms of organism size, brain volume, or number of synapses — and what technologies need to advance to reach them?

title: FlyWire: Online community for whole-brain connectomics
abstract: ABSTRACT Due to advances in automated image acquisition and analysis, new whole-brain connectomes beyond C. elegans are finally on the horizon. Proofreading of whole-brain automated reconstructions will require many person-years of effort, due to the huge volumes of data involved. Here we present FlyWire, an online community for proofreading neural circuits in a fly brain, and explain how its computational and social structures are organized to scale up to whole-brain connectomics. Browser-based 3D interactive segmentation by collaborative editing of a spatially chunked supervoxel graph makes it possible to distribute proofreading to individuals located virtually anywhere in the world. Information in the edit history is programmatically accessible for a variety of uses such as estimating proofreading accuracy or building incentive systems. An open community accelerates proofreading by recruiting more participants, and accelerates scientific discovery by requiring information sharing. We demonstrate how FlyWire enables circuit analysis by reconstructing and analysing the connectome of mechanosensory neurons.

ocar.opportunity: Reconstructing a complete whole-brain connectome beyond C. elegans requires proofreading at a scale that exceeds what any single lab can accomplish, demanding new community-based approaches to distribute the effort.
ocar.challenge: Coordinating distributed proofreading by many annotators on the same large EM dataset introduces conflicts, quality control challenges, and requires novel infrastructure for collaborative editing, version control, and consensus building at the scale of millions of neurons and synapses.
ocar.action: The authors developed FlyWire, a browser-based platform enabling online community proofreading of the Drosophila brain. The system provides a collaborative editing environment with graph-based segmentation editing, authentication, and social structures to coordinate large numbers of annotators.
ocar.resolution: FlyWire demonstrated that whole-brain connectome proofreading can be accomplished by an online community, with hundreds of participants contributing to the reconstruction of the full adult fly brain. The platform's design successfully managed conflicts, tracked contributions, and maintained data quality at the whole-brain scale.
ocar.future_work: Scaling the FlyWire model to larger volumes such as mammalian brain regions will require further advances in automated segmentation quality and more efficient human-AI collaborative proofreading workflows.

summaries.beginner: Mapping a whole brain in a fly requires checking and fixing millions of decisions made by computers. FlyWire is a website that lets anyone help with this task, like a Wikipedia for brain mapping, with hundreds of people working together to complete the fly brain map.
summaries.intermediate: FlyWire is a browser-based platform for community proofreading of whole-brain EM reconstructions, providing collaborative graph editing, user authentication, and quality control infrastructure. The paper describes how computational and social structures were designed to scale proofreading effort to the Drosophila whole-brain connectome with hundreds of distributed annotators.
summaries.advanced: FlyWire's architecture addresses the fundamental scalability problem in EM connectomics by distributing proofreading labor through a community platform with graph-based collaborative editing. Key design choices include chunk-based data serving, optimistic locking for concurrent edits, and contribution tracking for quality oversight. A notable caveat is that community proofreading introduces variable annotator expertise, requiring strategies to aggregate edits and detect and correct systematic errors; the paper describes how these challenges were managed in practice.

discussion_prompts: How does the FlyWire community model compare to other crowdsourced scientific data annotation efforts (e.g., Eyewire, Zooniverse), and what unique challenges does connectomics proofreading present?
What quality control mechanisms are most effective for managing variable annotator expertise in collaborative connectomics proofreading?
How would the FlyWire model need to be adapted to support community proofreading of mammalian brain volumes, which are orders of magnitude larger?

title: LC3, a mammalian homologue of yeast Apg8p, is localized in autophagosome membranes after processing

ocar.opportunity: Autophagy — the cellular process of self-digestion used to recycle damaged components — was known to involve membrane-enclosed autophagosomes, but the molecular identity and localization of proteins marking these structures in mammals was unknown. Understanding autophagy regulation required identifying mammalian homologs of yeast autophagy proteins.
ocar.challenge: Identifying mammalian autophagy proteins required finding functional homologs of yeast Apg proteins, verifying their localization to autophagosomes (which are morphologically similar to other membranous organelles), and establishing that processing of the candidate protein was coupled to autophagosome formation.
ocar.action: The authors identified LC3 as a mammalian homolog of yeast Apg8p/Aut7p through sequence homology, characterized two forms of LC3 (LC3-I cytosolic, LC3-II membrane-bound), and used immunoelectron microscopy to demonstrate that LC3-II localizes specifically to autophagosome membranes after proteolytic processing.
ocar.resolution: LC3-II was shown to specifically mark autophagosome membranes, providing the first reliable molecular marker for autophagosomes in mammalian cells. This discovery made LC3 lipidation and puncta formation the standard assay for monitoring autophagy, enabling the field to expand rapidly.
ocar.future_work: Subsequent work has characterized the full ATG8 family in mammals, identified cargo receptors linking LC3 to selective autophagy, and explored roles of LC3 in non-canonical autophagy pathways.

plain_language_summary: Cells have a recycling program called autophagy where they break down and reuse their own components. This paper identified the molecular label (LC3) that marks the membrane bags (autophagosomes) used for this recycling in mammalian cells. Discovering this marker was like finding the post-it note that cells stick on packages destined for the recycling plant — it transformed autophagy research by giving scientists a tool to watch the process.

summaries.beginner: Cells have a process called autophagy where they break down their own damaged parts for recycling. This paper found a protein called LC3 that specifically labels the membrane bags used for this recycling in mammalian cells, giving scientists a tool to see and study autophagy under the microscope.
summaries.intermediate: This paper identified LC3 as a mammalian Apg8p/Aut7p homolog and showed that its processed, lipidated form (LC3-II) localizes specifically to autophagosome membranes. By providing a reliable molecular marker for autophagosomes, this discovery transformed the study of autophagy and established LC3 immunofluorescence as the standard assay for autophagy flux in mammalian systems.
summaries.advanced: The identification of LC3 as an autophagosome-specific marker was based on sequence homology to yeast Apg8p and biochemical evidence for two processed forms: cytosolic LC3-I and phosphatidylethanolamine-conjugated LC3-II. Immunoelectron microscopy confirmed LC3-II membrane localization. This paper is highly cited primarily outside connectomics, but is included in connectomics bibliographies as a methodological reference for EM-based organelle identification and as context for autophagy-related ultrastructural phenotypes observed in EM connectomics datasets.

discussion_prompts: Why is LC3 lipidation considered a reliable indicator of autophagosome formation, and what are the caveats in interpreting LC3 puncta as a measure of autophagy flux?
How has the discovery of multiple ATG8 family members (LC3A/B/C, GABARAP, GATE-16) complicated the use of LC3 as a singular autophagy marker?

In the context of electron microscopy datasets from connectomics, how might autophagy-related ultrastructural features (autophagosomes, lysosomes) be identified and what might they indicate about neuronal health?

title: A GAL4-Driver Line Resource for Drosophila Neurobiology
abstract: We established a collection of 7,000 transgenic lines of Drosophila melanogaster. Expression of GAL4 in each line is controlled by a different, defined fragment of genomic DNA that serves as a transcriptional enhancer. We used confocal microscopy of dissected nervous systems to determine the expression patterns driven by each fragment in the adult brain and ventral nerve cord. We present image data on 6,650 lines. Using both manual and machine-assisted annotation, we describe the expression patterns in the most useful lines. We illustrate the utility of these data for identifying novel neuronal cell types, revealing brain asymmetry, and describing the nature and extent of neuronal shape stereotypy. The GAL4 lines allow expression of exogenous genes in distinct, small subsets of the adult nervous system. The set of DNA fragments, each driving a documented expression pattern, will facilitate the generation of additional constructs for manipulating neuronal function.

ocar.opportunity: Systematic study of Drosophila neural circuits required genetic access to defined subsets of neurons, but creating targeted driver lines one at a time was inefficient. A large-scale resource of characterized enhancer-based GAL4 driver lines would accelerate circuit dissection across the entire nervous system.
ocar.challenge: Generating thousands of transgenic fly lines with defined expression patterns required large-scale cloning, injection, screening, and imaging — a major logistical undertaking. Systematically characterizing expression in the complex adult Drosophila brain required standardized confocal imaging and a searchable database.
ocar.action: The authors generated 7,000 transgenic Drosophila lines expressing GAL4 under the control of different defined genomic enhancer fragments. They imaged GAL4-driven GFP expression in the adult brain and ventral nerve cord by confocal microscopy and built a searchable online database of expression patterns.
ocar.resolution: The resulting collection covered a large fraction of Drosophila neuron types and brain regions, providing the community with a powerful resource for circuit-specific genetic access. The database enabled researchers to identify driver lines for specific neurons, facilitating functional studies, connectivity experiments, and behavioral analyses.
ocar.future_work: Further refinement of split-GAL4 strategies combining two partial activators has subsequently enabled even more specific targeting of individual cell types, building on this foundational driver line resource.

plain_language_summary: To study specific neurons in a fly's brain, scientists need genetic 'switches' that turn on only in those neurons. This paper created a library of 7,000 such switches — each lighting up a different set of neurons — and made the whole collection searchable online. It is like building a massive toolkit of colored highlighters, each one marking a different part of the brain's wiring diagram.

summaries.beginner: To study what different neurons in a fly brain do, scientists need genetic tools that only affect specific neurons. This paper created a library of 7,000 transgenic fly lines where each line has a genetic switch that turns on only in certain neurons, letting scientists control or observe specific parts of the brain.
summaries.intermediate: This paper describes the generation and characterization of a collection of 7,000 Drosophila GAL4 driver lines, each expressing GAL4 under a distinct genomic enhancer fragment. Systematic confocal imaging of GFP expression in the adult brain and nerve cord, combined with an online database, created a community resource for targeted genetic access to specific neuron populations — a cornerstone tool for Drosophila circuit neuroscience.
summaries.advanced: The Janelia GAL4 driver line collection established the infrastructure for cell-type-specific genetic manipulation in Drosophila. Enhancer fragments were derived from genome-wide computational predictions of regulatory elements, and expression was systematically annotated using a brain atlas. The collection is a prerequisite for functional characterization of cell types identified in connectomics studies — without genetic driver lines, EM-identified neurons cannot be functionally manipulated. Subsequent split-GAL4 strategies using pairs of hemi-drivers from this collection have further improved cell-type specificity.

discussion_prompts: How do GAL4 driver line resources complement connectomic datasets, and what is the current state of matching EM-identified neuron types to specific driver lines?
What are the limitations of enhancer-fragment-based driver lines in terms of specificity and consistency, and how does split-GAL4 address these?
How might large-scale driver line resources like this one be extended to other model organisms, and what would be required?

title: A pneumonia outbreak associated with a new coronavirus of probable bat origin
abstract: Since the outbreak of severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) 18 years ago, a large number of SARS-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoVs) have been discovered in their natural reservoir host, bats<sup>1-4</sup>. Previous studies have shown that some bat SARSr-CoVs have the potential to infect humans<sup>5-7</sup>. Here we report the identification and characterization of a new coronavirus (2019-nCoV), which caused an epidemic of acute respiratory syndrome in humans in Wuhan, China. The epidemic, which started on 12 December 2019, had caused 2,794 laboratory-confirmed infections including 80 deaths by 26 January 2020. Full-length genome sequences were obtained from five patients at an early stage of the outbreak. The sequences are almost identical and share 79.6% sequence identity to SARS-CoV. Furthermore, we show that 2019-nCoV is 96% identical at the whole-genome level to a bat coronavirus. Pairwise protein sequence analysis of seven conserved non-structural proteins domains show that this virus belongs to the species of SARSr-CoV. In addition, 2019-nCoV virus isolated from the bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of a critically ill patient could be neutralized by sera from several patients. Notably, we confirmed that 2019-nCoV uses the same cell entry receptor-angiotensin converting enzyme II (ACE2)-as SARS-CoV.

ocar.opportunity: The emergence of a novel pneumonia of unknown etiology in Wuhan, China in late 2019 required rapid identification of the causative pathogen to inform public health response and guide development of diagnostics and countermeasures.
ocar.challenge: Identifying a novel viral pathogen from clinical samples required deep sequencing, comparative genomics, and rapid sharing of findings — all under the time pressure of an emerging outbreak with pandemic potential.
ocar.action: The authors isolated the novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV, later SARS-CoV-2) from bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of patients with unexplained pneumonia, performed full-genome sequencing, phylogenetic analysis, and characterized its receptor binding, finding close relationship to bat coronaviruses and use of ACE2 as entry receptor.
ocar.resolution: The paper identified SARS-CoV-2 as a novel betacoronavirus phylogenetically related to bat SARS-like coronaviruses, with the ability to use human ACE2 for cell entry. This foundational characterization enabled the global scientific response to COVID-19.
ocar.future_work: Questions about the precise origin and spillover pathway of SARS-CoV-2 from bats to humans, as well as the development of broadly protective coronavirus vaccines, remain open.

plain_language_summary: When a mysterious pneumonia appeared in China in late 2019, scientists quickly identified the new virus responsible — SARS-CoV-2 — by sequencing its genome and tracing its evolutionary family tree. This paper was one of the first to characterize the new coronavirus, setting the foundation for all subsequent COVID-19 research and the global pandemic response.

summaries.beginner: When a new type of pneumonia appeared in China in 2019, scientists needed to identify what was causing it. This paper sequenced the genome of the new virus (now called SARS-CoV-2) and showed it was a coronavirus related to bat viruses, providing the basic information needed to develop tests and treatments.
summaries.intermediate: This paper reports the isolation and genomic characterization of SARS-CoV-2 from patients with severe pneumonia, demonstrating its phylogenetic relationship to bat SARS-like coronaviruses and its use of ACE2 as a receptor. It established the viral identity that underpins all subsequent COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, and vaccine development.
summaries.advanced: This landmark paper from the Wuhan Institute of Virology reports full-genome sequencing and characterization of the index SARS-CoV-2 isolates. Phylogenetic analysis placed the virus within the bat SARS-like CoV clade, and functional receptor binding studies confirmed ACE2 usage. This paper is included in connectomics bibliographies as a high-citation reference appearing in broad citation networks, rather than being directly relevant to neural circuit reconstruction — illustrating the challenge of citation network analysis in identifying domain-specific influential papers.

discussion_prompts: Why does a COVID-19 virology paper appear in a connectomics citation network, and what does this reveal about how bibliometric methods identify 'influential' papers across fields?
What bioinformatics methods used in viral genomics (phylogenetics, genome assembly) have parallels in connectomics (e.g., neuron tracing, lineage analysis)?
How should bibliometric tools for domain-specific literature (like connectomics) handle highly-cited papers from tangential fields that appear through citation co-occurrence?

title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
abstract: A good book may have the power to change the way we see the world, but a great book actually becomes part of our daily consciousness, pervading our thinking to the point that we take it for granted, and we forget how provocative and challenging its ideas once were-and still are. The Structure of Scientific Revolutions is that kind of book. When it was first published in 1962, it was a landmark event in the history and philosophy of science. And fifty years later, it still has many lessons to teach. With The Structure of Scientific Revolutions, Kuhn challenged long-standing linear notions of scientific progress, arguing that transformative ideas don't arise from the day-to-day, gradual process of experimentation and data accumulation, but that revolutions in those breakthrough moments that disrupt accepted thinking and offer unanticipated ideas, occur outside of normal science, as he called it. Though Kuhn was writing when physics ruled the sciences, his ideas on how scientific revolutions bring order to the anomalies that amass over time in research experiments are still instructive in our biotech age. This new edition of Kuhn's essential work in the history of science includes an insightful introductory essay by Ian Hacking that clarifies terms popularized by Kuhn, including paradigm and incommensurability, and applies Kuhn's ideas to the science of today. Usefully keyed to the separate sections of the book, Hacking's essay provides important background information as well as a contemporary context. Newly designed, with an expanded index, this edition will be eagerly welcomed by the next generation of readers seeking to understand the history of our perspectives on science.

ocar.opportunity: Scientific progress was typically understood as gradual accumulation of knowledge, but this model failed to explain episodes of radical conceptual reorganization in science. A new framework was needed to describe how scientific fields are structured and how they fundamentally change.
ocar.challenge: Characterizing the dynamics of scientific change required grappling with historical case studies across many fields and developing a vocabulary that captured both normal science and the discontinuous revolutions that periodically reorganize entire disciplines.
ocar.action: Kuhn developed the concept of 'paradigms' — shared frameworks of assumptions, methods, and exemplary problems that guide normal scientific work — and analyzed historical episodes of paradigm shift across physics, chemistry, and astronomy to describe the structure of scientific revolutions.
ocar.resolution: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions introduced the concept of paradigm shifts into scientific discourse, arguing that science progresses not linearly but through cycles of normal science, crisis, and revolutionary restructuring. The framework has profoundly influenced how scientists, historians, and philosophers understand scientific change.
ocar.future_work: Subsequent philosophers of science have debated, refined, and critiqued Kuhn's framework, particularly regarding the incommensurability of paradigms and the rationality of theory choice during scientific revolutions.

plain_language_summary: This famous 1962 book proposed that science does not simply accumulate knowledge gradually — instead, it periodically undergoes 'paradigm shifts' where the entire framework of a field is overthrown and replaced. Think of it like the difference between adding new rooms to a house versus demolishing it and rebuilding on a completely different plan.

summaries.beginner: This book, first published in 1962, changed how we think about how science works. Rather than steady progress, it argued that science goes through long periods of 'normal science' — working within an accepted framework — punctuated by revolutionary moments when the whole framework is replaced by something new.
summaries.intermediate: Kuhn's 'Structure of Scientific Revolutions' introduced the concept of paradigms and paradigm shifts, arguing that scientific progress is non-cumulative and that revolutionary changes in science involve replacement of incommensurable conceptual frameworks. The book has been enormously influential across all scientific disciplines including neuroscience.
summaries.advanced: This book is included in connectomics citation networks as a high-citation cross-domain reference, likely appearing through co-citations with neuroscience methodology papers. From a connectomics perspective, the field itself may represent a paradigm shift — the move from population-level electrophysiological characterization to structural synaptic-resolution mapping fundamentally changes the questions that can be asked and how neural computation is conceptualized. The incommensurability thesis is relevant to debates about whether connectomes can be directly translated into functional understanding.

discussion_prompts: Does the emergence of connectomics represent a Kuhnian paradigm shift in neuroscience, or an extension of existing frameworks? What would constitute evidence either way?
How does the concept of 'normal science' apply to the current phase of connectomics development, where the field is primarily focused on scaling existing methods?
What would a 'crisis' in connectomics look like — what findings or failures would challenge the fundamental assumptions of the field?

title: High-precision automated reconstruction of neurons with flood-filling networks

ocar.opportunity: Automated segmentation of neurons in EM volumes was limited by the failure of existing methods to correctly handle biological ambiguities like thin processes and densely packed neurites, resulting in excessive split and merge errors that required extensive manual proofreading.
ocar.challenge: Achieving human-level reconstruction accuracy in dense EM volumes requires an algorithm that can propagate segmentation decisions over long distances while integrating local image evidence, which standard convolutional networks applied independently to local patches cannot do.
ocar.action: The authors developed flood-filling networks (FFNs), a recurrent convolutional network architecture that iteratively expands a segmentation mask starting from a seed point, updating the mask based on both local image features and the current segmentation state to reconstruct complete neurons.
ocar.resolution: FFNs substantially outperformed previous automated methods in EM neuron reconstruction accuracy, demonstrating near-human performance on challenging benchmark volumes. The approach reduced merge error rates relative to state-of-the-art competitors, enabling denser automated reconstruction with less proofreading overhead.
ocar.future_work: FFNs are computationally expensive due to their iterative architecture; future work should improve throughput through better parallelization, and extend the approach to handle multi-scale contextual information for even larger ambiguous structures.

summaries.beginner: Tracing nerve cells through electron microscope images is hard for computers because the cells are closely packed and sometimes very thin. This study invented a new type of artificial intelligence that traces cells more accurately by 'filling' each cell like water flooding a space, remembering what it has already seen.
summaries.intermediate: Flood-filling networks (FFNs) use a recurrent convolutional architecture that iteratively propagates a segmentation mask from a seed, conditioning each extension step on both local image features and the current mask state. This approach achieves near-human accuracy on dense EM neuron reconstruction, substantially reducing merge error rates compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.
summaries.advanced: FFNs reformulate neuron segmentation as a sequential seed-expansion problem: at each step, the network predicts the likelihood that nearby voxels belong to the current segment given local image context and the evolving mask, then extends the mask in high-confidence directions. The recurrent state encoding enables long-range consistency that local patch-based methods lack. Key limitations include high computational cost (hours per neuron on GPU), sensitivity to the initial seed placement, and challenges in correctly handling neurons that circle back on themselves or contain very thin processes near the resolution limit.

discussion_prompts: How does the recurrent state representation in FFNs enable better long-range segmentation consistency compared to independent local patch prediction, and what are the failure modes when this representation becomes confused?

What are the computational trade-offs of FFN inference versus faster agglomeration-based segmentation approaches, and how should this affect the choice of algorithm for different connectomics use cases?

How would you design a hybrid pipeline that uses FFNs for difficult ambiguous regions and faster methods for straightforward neurite segments, and how would you identify which regions require each approach?

title: Complete connectomic reconstruction of olfactory projection neurons in the fly brain
abstract: Abstract Nervous systems contain sensory neurons, local neurons, projection neurons and motor neurons. To understand how these building blocks form whole circuits, we must distil these broad classes into neuronal cell types and describe their network connectivity. Using an electron micrograph dataset for an entire Drosophila melanogaster brain, we reconstruct the first complete inventory of olfactory projections connecting the antennal lobe, the insect analogue of the mammalian olfactory bulb, to higher-order brain regions in an adult animal brain. We then connect this inventory to extant data in the literature, providing synaptic-resolution ‘holotypes’ both for heavily investigated and previously unknown cell types. Projection neurons are approximately twice as numerous as reported by light level studies; cell types are stereotyped, but not identical, in cell and synapse numbers between brain hemispheres. The lateral horn, the insect analogue of the mammalian cortical amygdala, is the main target for this olfactory information and has been shown to guide innate behaviour. Here, we find new connectivity motifs, including: axo-axonic connectivity between projection neurons; feedback and lateral inhibition of these axons by local neurons; and the convergence of different inputs, including non-olfactory inputs and memory-related feedback onto lateral horn neurons. This differs from the configuration of the second most prominent target for olfactory projection neurons: the mushroom body calyx, the insect analogue of the mammalian piriform cortex and a centre for associative memory. Our work provides a complete neuroanatomical platform for future studies of the adult Drosophila olfactory system. Highlights First complete parts list for second-order neurons of an adult olfactory system Quantification of left-right stereotypy in cell and synapse number Axo-axonic connections form hierarchical communities in the lateral horn Local neurons and memory-related feedback target projection neuron axons

ocar.opportunity: A complete, cell-type-resolved map of how olfactory information is projected from the primary olfactory center to higher brain areas was needed to understand parallel processing of odor information in Drosophila.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing every olfactory projection neuron type and their synaptic targets across a whole-brain EM volume requires extensive manual and semi-automated tracing across a complex, densely packed neuropil.
ocar.action: Using the FAFB whole-brain EM dataset, the authors reconstructed all olfactory projection neurons from the antennal lobe, classified them into types by morphology and connectivity, and mapped their synaptic outputs to mushroom bodies, lateral horn, and other higher brain areas.
ocar.resolution: The complete census revealed 150+ projection neuron types with distinct glomerular innervation and downstream targeting logic, demonstrating parallel channels for odor identity versus valence processing. The dataset provides a reference framework for linking neural circuit architecture to olfactory behavior.
ocar.future_work: Connecting specific projection neuron types to odor response properties through functional recordings and behavioral genetics, and extending comparisons across sexes and ages, remain important goals.

summaries.beginner: Fruit flies detect smells using a relatively simple brain circuit. This paper mapped every relay neuron in the Drosophila smell pathway using electron microscopy, creating a complete reference chart of how different smells are routed to different parts of the brain.
summaries.intermediate: This peer-reviewed companion to the preprint version provides a definitive census of Drosophila olfactory projection neuron types reconstructed from the FAFB whole-brain EM dataset. The authors classified >150 types based on glomerular innervation and downstream connectivity, revealing systematic organizational principles for parallel olfactory processing streams targeting mushroom bodies and lateral horn.
summaries.advanced: The study provides a complete connectomic reference for the Drosophila olfactory projection neuron population, enabling precise quantification of convergence ratios, bilateral symmetry, and connectivity stereotypy across individuals where comparison data exist. A key finding is that multiglomerular projection neurons show distinct downstream targeting relative to uniglomerular types, suggesting specialized roles in combinatorial odor coding. Caveats include reliance on a single female brain and limited cross-validation of cell typing criteria between morphological and connectivity-based approaches.

discussion_prompts: How does the completeness of the projection neuron census in this study compare to what was achievable with light microscopy-based methods, and what novel cell types were revealed?
What connectivity features distinguish projection neurons targeting mushroom bodies versus lateral horn, and how might these differences relate to memory versus innate valence processing?
How do the bilateral symmetry patterns of olfactory projection neurons revealed by this connectome compare to predictions from developmental and genetic studies?

title: A connectomic study of a petascale fragment of human cerebral cortex
abstract: Abstract We acquired a rapidly preserved human surgical sample from the temporal lobe of the cerebral cortex. We stained a 1 mm 3 volume with heavy metals, embedded it in resin, cut more than 5000 slices at ∼30 nm and imaged these sections using a high-speed multibeam scanning electron microscope. We used computational methods to render the three-dimensional structure containing 57,216 cells, hundreds of millions of neurites and 133.7 million synaptic connections. The 1.4 petabyte electron microscopy volume, the segmented cells, cell parts, blood vessels, myelin, inhibitory and excitatory synapses, and 104 manually proofread cells are available to peruse online . Many interesting and unusual features were evident in this dataset. Glia outnumbered neurons 2:1 and oligodendrocytes were the most common cell type in the volume. Excitatory spiny neurons comprised 69% of the neuronal population, and excitatory synapses also were in the majority (76%). The synaptic drive onto spiny neurons was biased more strongly toward excitation (70%) than was the case for inhibitory interneurons (48%). Despite incompleteness of the automated segmentation caused by split and merge errors, we could automatically generate (and then validate) connections between most of the excitatory and inhibitory neuron types both within and between layers. In studying these neurons we found that deep layer excitatory cell types can be classified into new subsets, based on structural and connectivity differences, and that chandelier interneurons not only innervate excitatory neuron initial segments as previously described, but also each other’s initial segments. Furthermore, among the thousands of weak connections established on each neuron, there exist rarer highly powerful axonal inputs that establish multi-synaptic contacts (up to ∼20 synapses) with target neurons. Our analysis indicates that these strong inputs are specific, and allow small numbers of axons to have an outsized role in the activity of some of their postsynaptic partners.

ocar.opportunity: Understanding the organization of human cortical circuits at synaptic resolution had been impossible due to the absence of synaptic-resolution structural data from human brain tissue. A petascale EM reconstruction of human cerebral cortex would provide an unprecedented view of human neural architecture.
ocar.challenge: Human brain tissue presents unique challenges for EM connectomics: limited availability of high-quality surgical samples, the need to process and image a 1 mm³ volume containing hundreds of thousands of cells across more than 5,000 serial sections, and the requirement for automated reconstruction of an unprecedented data volume.
ocar.action: The authors obtained a rapidly preserved surgical tissue sample from human temporal cortex, stained with heavy metals, embedded in resin, cut >5,000 sections at ~30 nm, and imaged with a high-speed multibeam scanning EM. They applied computational reconstruction to generate a three-dimensional dataset of 57,216 cells with annotated neurons, synapses, and blood vessels.
ocar.resolution: The dataset revealed novel features of human cortical organization including unusually large and complex synaptic structures, rare axonal and dendritic morphologies, and cell-type-specific connectivity patterns. The volume was made publicly available, providing a landmark resource for studying human cortical microcircuitry.
ocar.future_work: Extending human cortical connectomics to larger volumes, different cortical areas, and comparison with neurological disease tissue remains a major goal for understanding human brain organization and pathology.

plain_language_summary: This paper produced the most detailed map ever made of a piece of human brain — a 1 cubic millimeter chunk of temporal cortex imaged at nanometer resolution to reveal every neuron, connection, and synapse within it. It is like creating a Google Street View of the brain's internal wiring at the level of individual molecules.

summaries.beginner: Scientists took a tiny piece of human brain tissue from a surgical patient and imaged it with an electron microscope to see every single neuron and connection inside. The result was a massive 3D map showing 57,000 cells and millions of synapses — the most detailed view of human brain tissue ever created.
summaries.intermediate: This paper describes the acquisition and reconstruction of a 1 mm³ volume of human temporal cortex at ~30 nm resolution, yielding a dataset with 57,216 cells, annotated synapses, and vasculature. Analyses revealed novel features of human cortical organization including distinctive synapse morphologies and cell-type-specific connectivity patterns not previously described.
summaries.advanced: The H01 dataset represents the first petascale EM connectomics resource from human cortex. Acquisition used a 61-beam multibeam SEM at ~4×4×30 nm voxel resolution, generating ~1.4 petabytes of raw data. Automated segmentation using flood-fill networks and extensive proofreading enabled reconstruction of neurons spanning the full thickness of the cortical volume. Notable findings include neurons with unusual axonal branching patterns, rare giant synapses, and evidence for highly specific connectivity rules. Key caveats include that the sample comes from epilepsy tissue and represents a single individual, limiting generalizability.

discussion_prompts: What features of human cortical circuitry identified in this dataset appear genuinely different from rodent cortex, and how confidently can we make that comparison?
What are the ethical and practical challenges of obtaining high-quality human brain tissue for connectomics, and how might disease tissue bias structural observations?
How does the scale of the H01 dataset compare to what would be needed to draw conclusions about human cortical circuit organization at the level of a full cortical column or area?

title: Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning

ocar.opportunity: Artificial intelligence systems for complex sequential decision-making required learning directly from high-dimensional sensory inputs without hand-engineered features. No single agent had demonstrated human-level performance across a diverse range of challenging tasks from raw pixel input.
ocar.challenge: Deep reinforcement learning suffers from instability when neural networks are trained on correlated sequential experience, as the non-stationary target values cause divergence. Stabilizing learning from raw pixels across a diverse range of games with different reward structures and dynamics was a fundamental challenge.
ocar.action: DeepMind developed Deep Q-Networks (DQN), combining Q-learning with a deep convolutional neural network trained from raw pixel input using two key innovations: experience replay (storing transitions and sampling randomly to break correlations) and a separate target network to stabilize Q-value estimates.
ocar.resolution: DQN achieved human-level or superhuman performance on 49 different Atari 2600 games using the same architecture and hyperparameters, demonstrating that a single end-to-end RL algorithm could master diverse tasks from raw sensory input. This became a landmark in deep learning and has influenced neuroAI models of reinforcement learning.
ocar.future_work: Extensions addressing sample efficiency, multi-task learning, and interpretability remain active areas, and connecting DQN-like architectures to biological dopaminergic reward circuits is an ongoing focus in neuroAI.

plain_language_summary: This paper taught a computer to play dozens of old Atari video games at human level or better, learning purely by trial and error from the raw pixels on screen — without being told the rules. The key was clever tricks to make learning stable, and the result was an AI that could master a wide variety of tasks the same way a person might learn by playing.

summaries.beginner: This paper created an AI system called DQN that learned to play 49 different Atari video games just by looking at the screen and trying to get high scores — like a person learning games by playing them. It achieved human-level performance on many games using the same algorithm and the same settings for every game.
summaries.intermediate: DQN combined deep convolutional neural networks with Q-learning, using experience replay and a target network to stabilize training from raw pixel inputs. Achieving human-level performance across 49 diverse Atari games demonstrated the generality of deep RL. The architecture has influenced neuroAI models linking reinforcement learning to basal ganglia dopamine circuits.
summaries.advanced: DQN addressed the instability of naive deep Q-learning through two key mechanisms: uniform random sampling from a replay buffer (breaking the temporal correlations of online learning) and a periodically-updated target network (reducing non-stationarity of the training target). The architecture uses a convolutional feature extractor common across all games, demonstrating that a shared representation suffices for diverse tasks. In connectomics and neuroAI contexts, DQN informs models of reward-based circuit plasticity and is relevant to proofreading automation research exploring RL-based error detection.

discussion_prompts: How does the DQN architecture relate to current understanding of how the basal ganglia and dopaminergic systems implement reinforcement learning in biological brains?
Could reinforcement learning approaches like DQN be applied to connectomics proofreading — learning to detect and correct segmentation errors — and what would the reward signal be?
What are the main differences between how DQN learns from experience and how biological brains learn from rewards, and what does this tell us about the limits of the analogy?

title: Three-Dimensional Structure and Composition of CA3→CA1 Axons in Rat Hippocampal Slices: Implications for Presynaptic Connectivity and Compartmentalization
abstract: Physiological studies of CA3-->CA1 synaptic transmission and plasticity have revealed both pre- and postsynaptic effects. Understanding the extent to which individual presynaptic axonal boutons could provide local compartments for control of synaptic efficacy and microconnectivity requires knowledge of their three-dimensional morphology and composition. In hippocampal slices, serial electron microscopy was used to examine a nearly homogeneous population of CA3-->CA1 axons in the middle of stratum radiatum of area CA1. The locations of postsynaptic densities (PSDs), vesicles, and mitochondria were determined along 75 axon segments (9.1 +/- 2.0 micrometer in length). Synapses, defined by the colocalization of PSDs and vesicles, occurred on average at 2.7 micrometer intervals along the axons. Most varicosities (68%) had one PSD, 19% had 2-4 PSDs, and 13% had none. Synaptic vesicles occurred in 90% of the varicosities. One-half (53%) of the varicosities lacked mitochondria, raising questions about their regulation of ATP and Ca2+, and 8% of varicosities contained only mitochondria. Eleven axons were reconstructed fully. The varicosities were oblong and varied greatly in both length (1.1 +/- 0.7 micrometer) and volume (0.13 +/- 0.14 micrometer 3), whereas the intervaricosity shafts were narrow, tubular, and similar in diameter (0.17 +/- 0.04 micrometer) but variable in length (1.4 +/- 1.2 micrometer). The narrow axonal shafts resemble dendritic spine necks and thus could promote biochemical compartmentalization of individual axonal varicosities. The findings raise the intriguing possibility of localized differences in metabolism and connectivity among different axons, varicosities, and synapses.

ocar.opportunity: Synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus — long-term potentiation and depression — involves both presynaptic and postsynaptic changes, but the three-dimensional structure and compartmentalization of the presynaptic axon in this circuit was unknown. Understanding whether individual boutons could act as independent computational units required structural characterization.
ocar.challenge: Resolving the three-dimensional morphology and composition of individual presynaptic boutons along CA3 axons in hippocampal slices required serial-section electron microscopy with sufficient resolution to identify vesicle pools, active zones, and mitochondria within submicron-scale structures.
ocar.action: The authors performed serial-section transmission EM on rat hippocampal slices to reconstruct 3D morphology of CA3 axons and their boutons in area CA1, quantifying organelle content, active zone number, and morphological parameters across hundreds of individual boutons.
ocar.resolution: Individual CA3 boutons were found to vary substantially in size, vesicle content, mitochondrial inclusion, and active zone number, suggesting significant heterogeneity in presynaptic strength and plasticity potential. The data supported the view that individual boutons could act as semi-autonomous compartments for regulating synaptic efficacy.
ocar.future_work: Follow-up studies examined how bouton morphology relates to synaptic strength after LTP induction, establishing connections between structural plasticity and electrophysiological measures of potentiation.

plain_language_summary: This paper used electron microscopy to study the tiny bulges (boutons) along axons in the hippocampus where neurons communicate with each other. By reconstructing their 3D structure in detail, it found that individual boutons vary a lot in size and contents — suggesting that each one can independently control how strong or weak its connection is.

summaries.beginner: Where two neurons connect — at synapses — there are tiny bulges on the sending neuron called boutons that contain the chemical messengers. This paper used electron microscopy to make 3D models of these boutons in the hippocampus and found that they vary a lot in size and contents, suggesting each one can independently regulate how strongly it signals.
summaries.intermediate: Using serial-section TEM reconstruction of rat hippocampal CA3→CA1 axons, the authors characterized the 3D morphology, organelle composition, and active zone number of individual presynaptic boutons. The observed heterogeneity in vesicle pool size, mitochondrial content, and active zone area supports a model where individual synaptic boutons function as relatively autonomous regulatory units for presynaptic plasticity.
summaries.advanced: This serial-section EM reconstruction of CA3 Schaffer collateral axons provided quantitative morphological data on presynaptic bouton diversity at submicron resolution. The characterization of vesicle pool sizes, active zone areas, and mitochondrial content across hundreds of boutons established correlates of presynaptic strength at individual terminals — data that proved prescient for later connectomics-scale studies of synapse size distributions and their relationship to synaptic weight. The work was limited to a small number of reconstructed segments, but established methods and questions that scaled to connectomics approaches.

discussion_prompts: How do the morphological heterogeneity data from this study connect to more recent connectomics findings about synapse size distributions and their relationship to synaptic weight?
What mechanisms could generate and maintain the large diversity in bouton morphology observed along individual axons in the hippocampus?
How has serial-section EM at the scale of individual boutons evolved into the large-scale connectomics approaches used today, and what was preserved versus changed?

title: A pyramid approach to subpixel registration based on intensity
abstract: We present an automatic subpixel registration algorithm that minimizes the mean square intensity difference between a reference and a test data set, which can be either images (two-dimensional) or volumes (three-dimensional). It uses an explicit spline representation of the images in conjunction with spline processing, and is based on a coarse-to-fine iterative strategy (pyramid approach). The minimization is performed according to a new variation (ML*) of the Marquardt-Levenberg algorithm for nonlinear least-square optimization. The geometric deformation model is a global three-dimensional (3-D) affine transformation that can be optionally restricted to rigid-body motion (rotation and translation), combined with isometric scaling. It also includes an optional adjustment of image contrast differences. We obtain excellent results for the registration of intramodality positron emission tomography (PET) and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data. We conclude that the multiresolution refinement strategy is more robust than a comparable single-stage method, being less likely to be trapped into a false local optimum. In addition, our improved version of the Marquardt-Levenberg algorithm is faster.

ocar.opportunity: Accurate alignment of serial image sections is a fundamental preprocessing step in connectomics and other volumetric microscopy workflows. Existing registration methods lacked sub-pixel precision or robust handling of 3D volumetric data, limiting the accuracy of downstream neuron reconstruction.
ocar.challenge: Achieving sub-pixel image registration requires an algorithm that can handle both translational and rotational misalignment, is robust to intensity variations between sections, works on both 2D and 3D data, and converges reliably without becoming trapped in local minima.
ocar.action: The authors developed a spline-based image registration algorithm using a coarse-to-fine pyramid approach, minimizing the mean square intensity difference between reference and test images. The method employs explicit B-spline representation and multiresolution optimization to achieve reliable sub-pixel registration.
ocar.resolution: The pyramid-based spline registration algorithm achieved sub-pixel accuracy on both 2D image pairs and 3D volumes, with computational efficiency suitable for large datasets. It became widely used in connectomics preprocessing pipelines for aligning serial EM sections and in neuroscience for image registration generally.
ocar.future_work: Deep learning-based registration methods have since been developed that can handle more extreme deformations and learn task-specific similarity metrics, extending the capabilities of intensity-based methods.

plain_language_summary: When you stack hundreds of microscopy images into a 3D volume, each slice is slightly misaligned with the others — like a deck of cards that has been shuffled. This paper developed an algorithm that automatically aligns each slice with sub-pixel precision using a coarse-to-fine strategy, like first roughly sorting the deck, then making fine adjustments.

summaries.beginner: To create a 3D reconstruction from stacked microscopy images, each image must be precisely aligned with the others. This paper developed an automated algorithm that finds the best alignment between images with sub-pixel accuracy using a coarse-to-fine approach, starting with rough alignment and progressively refining it.
summaries.intermediate: This paper presents a sub-pixel image registration algorithm based on B-spline image representation and multiresolution pyramid optimization, minimizing mean square intensity differences. The method handles both 2D images and 3D volumes and is robust to initialization errors. It has been widely applied to serial-section EM alignment in connectomics preprocessing pipelines.
summaries.advanced: The algorithm uses separable cubic B-spline image interpolation within a gradient-descent minimization framework applied coarse-to-fine in a Gaussian pyramid. This formulation enables efficient sub-pixel registration of both affine and (with extensions) elastic deformations. In connectomics, serial-section alignment is a critical preprocessing step that determines the upper bound on reconstruction accuracy downstream; errors in section alignment propagate into neuron segmentation and synapse detection. While deep learning methods now offer improved performance on heavily deformed sections, intensity-based approaches remain competitive for well-prepared samples.

discussion_prompts: What types of section deformation artifacts in serial-section EM are well-handled by intensity-based registration methods versus elastic/non-rigid approaches?
How does registration error at the section alignment step propagate into neuron segmentation accuracy in connectomics pipelines?
What has the development of deep learning-based image registration (e.g., VoxelMorph) added over classical intensity-based methods for connectomics applications?

title: Presynaptic developmental plasticity allows robust sparse wiring of the Drosophila mushroom body
abstract: In order to represent complex stimuli, principle neurons of associative learning regions receive combinatorial sensory inputs. Density of combinatorial innervation is theorized to determine the number of distinct stimuli that can be represented and distinguished from one another, with sparse innervation thought to optimize the complexity of representations in networks of limited size. How the convergence of combinatorial inputs to principle neurons of associative brain regions is established during development is unknown. Here, we explore the developmental patterning of sparse olfactory inputs to Kenyon cells of the Drosophila melanogaster mushroom body.
