title: Modulating Innate and Adaptive Immunity by (R)-Roscovitine: Potential Therapeutic Opportunity in Cystic Fibrosis
abstract: (R)-Roscovitine, a pharmacological inhibitor of kinases, is currently in phase II clinical trial as a drug candidate for the treatment of cancers, Cushing's disease and rheumatoid arthritis. We here review the data that support the investigation of (R)-roscovitine as a potential therapeutic agent for the treatment of cystic fibrosis (CF). (R)-Roscovitine displays four independent properties that may favorably combine against CF: (1) it partially protects F508del-CFTR from proteolytic degradation and favors its trafficking to the plasma membrane; (2) by increasing membrane targeting of the TRPC6 ion channel, it rescues acidification in phagolysosomes of CF alveolar macrophages (which show abnormally high pH) and consequently restores their bactericidal activity; (3) its effects on neutrophils (induction of apoptosis), eosinophils (inhibition of degranulation/induction of apoptosis) and lymphocytes (modification of the Th17/Treg balance in favor of the differentiation of anti-inflammatory lymphocytes and reduced production of various interleukins, notably IL-17A) contribute to the resolution of inflammation and restoration of innate immunity, and (4) roscovitine displays analgesic properties in animal pain models. The fact that (R)-roscovitine has undergone extensive preclinical safety/pharmacology studies, and phase I and II clinical trials in cancer patients, encourages its repurposing as a CF drug candidate.

ocar.opportunity: Cystic fibrosis (CF) involves dysregulated innate and adaptive immune responses contributing to lung pathology, and targeted immunomodulatory treatments were lacking. (R)-Roscovitine, a CDK inhibitor already in clinical trials for cancer, had properties that suggested therapeutic potential in CF.
ocar.challenge: Demonstrating that (R)-Roscovitine could modulate multiple aspects of CF pathophysiology — including neutrophil survival, CFTR folding, calcium signaling, and inflammatory signaling — required integrating diverse pharmacological and cellular evidence within a single therapeutic framework.
ocar.action: The authors reviewed existing data on (R)-Roscovitine's pharmacological properties and synthesized evidence for four independent mechanisms potentially beneficial in CF: promoting neutrophil apoptosis to resolve inflammation, partially rescuing F508del-CFTR from degradation, modulating calcium signaling, and inhibiting inflammatory kinases.
ocar.resolution: The review made a case for (R)-Roscovitine as a multi-mechanism candidate for CF treatment, summarizing pharmacokinetic data and proposing a rationale for clinical investigation. The paper highlighted the potential for drug repurposing in CF therapy.
ocar.future_work: Clinical trials in CF and further mechanistic characterization of roscovitine's effects on immune and epithelial cell function are needed to determine therapeutic potential.

plain_language_summary: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disease causing thick mucus buildup and chronic lung infections. This review paper argued that a cancer drug called roscovitine might also help CF patients by working through four different beneficial mechanisms at once — potentially reducing inflammation, improving the function of the faulty protein that causes CF, and modulating immune responses.

summaries.beginner: Cystic fibrosis is caused by a faulty protein that leads to lung problems including chronic infections. This paper reviewed evidence that a drug called roscovitine, originally developed for cancer, might also help CF patients by acting on several aspects of the disease at once, from reducing harmful inflammation to partially fixing the faulty protein.
summaries.intermediate: This review synthesizes evidence for (R)-Roscovitine as a potential therapeutic in cystic fibrosis through four proposed mechanisms: promoting neutrophil apoptosis to resolve chronic inflammation, partial rescue of F508del-CFTR from proteasomal degradation, modulation of intracellular calcium signaling, and inhibition of pro-inflammatory kinases. The paper is included in connectomics citation networks as an off-domain high-citation reference.
summaries.advanced: This paper is pharmacologically off-domain relative to connectomics and neural circuit reconstruction. Its appearance in connectomics citation analyses reflects the presence of broad biomedical literature in citation co-occurrence networks when connectomics papers are analyzed within general neuroscience or cell biology citation databases. The paper illustrates the challenge of distinguishing domain-specific foundational literature from high-citation off-topic works in bibliometric analyses of emerging fields.

discussion_prompts: What does the appearance of a cystic fibrosis pharmacology paper in a connectomics citation network tell us about the limitations of using raw citation counts to assess relevance to a specific field?
How should bibliometric tools for connectomics filter out off-domain citations while still capturing genuinely interdisciplinary contributions?
Is there any aspect of (R)-Roscovitine's CDK inhibition biology that could be relevant to neuronal biology in the context of connectomics datasets?

title: Computer Visualization of Three-Dimensional Image Data Using IMOD

ocar.opportunity: Reconstructing three-dimensional structures from serial electron microscopy sections required software capable of visualizing, segmenting, and analyzing large volumetric image datasets. Existing tools were inadequate for the emerging demands of 3D biomedical imaging and electron tomography.
ocar.challenge: Handling large 3D EM image stacks required efficient memory management, interactive visualization, and tools for manual segmentation and model building — all within a platform accessible to biologists without specialized programming expertise and capable of running on the hardware of the mid-1990s.
ocar.action: The authors developed IMOD, a comprehensive software package for visualization and analysis of three-dimensional electron microscopy image data. IMOD provided tools for image display, serial section alignment, manual segmentation by contour drawing, 3D model building and rendering, and quantitative analysis.
ocar.resolution: IMOD became the dominant software platform for EM tomography and serial-section reconstruction in cell biology and early connectomics. Its open-source, freely distributed model and continuous development made it a cornerstone infrastructure tool for 3D EM data analysis across the field for decades.
ocar.future_work: IMOD continues to be developed and maintained, adding support for larger datasets, cryo-EM, and integration with automated segmentation pipelines, though newer platforms handle petascale connectomics data.

plain_language_summary: Working with three-dimensional brain images from electron microscopes requires specialized software to display, annotate, and analyze the data. IMOD was the foundational software package that let scientists visualize and manually trace neuron shapes from stacks of EM images, much like being able to sculpt 3D models from a pile of 2D photographs.

summaries.beginner: Electron microscopes produce 2D images, and scientists need to combine thousands of these into 3D reconstructions to see the full shapes of neurons. IMOD is the classic software package developed for this purpose, providing tools to display, annotate, and analyze 3D EM image stacks that formed the foundation of early connectomics.
summaries.intermediate: IMOD is an open-source software package for visualization and 3D reconstruction from electron microscopy data, providing image display, section alignment, manual contour-based segmentation, and 3D rendering. It was the primary platform for EM tomography and early serial-section connectomics, and remains widely used for EM tomography and correlated light-EM workflows.
summaries.advanced: IMOD's architecture is based on a tile-based image viewing engine (3dmod) with associated command-line tools for preprocessing, reconstruction, and analysis. Its core use cases in connectomics include fiducial-based tilt series alignment and tomographic reconstruction (for cryo-ET), serial section alignment for connectomics preprocessing, and manual contour segmentation for ground-truth annotation and proofreading validation. While superseded by cloud-native platforms for large-scale connectomics, IMOD remains the standard for cryo-electron tomography and continues to be actively developed by the Mastronarde group at UCSF.

discussion_prompts: How did IMOD's design and feature set shape the workflows and assumptions of early connectomics projects, and which of those assumptions still persist in modern tools?
What is IMOD's current role in the connectomics pipeline ecosystem relative to cloud-based annotation tools like CATMAID, CAVE, and Neuroglancer?
How does IMOD's approach to manual segmentation by contour drawing compare to the fly-through annotation approach used in more recent proofreading tools?

title: Mouse Retinal Cell Atlas: Molecular Identification of over Sixty Amacrine Cell Types
abstract: Amacrine cells (ACs) are a diverse class of interneurons that modulate input from photoreceptors to retinal ganglion cells (RGCs), rendering each RGC type selectively sensitive to particular visual features, which are then relayed to the brain. While many AC types have been identified morphologically and physiologically, they have not been comprehensively classified or molecularly characterized. We used high-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing to profile >32,000 ACs from mice of both sexes and applied computational methods to identify 63 AC types. We identified molecular markers for each type and used them to characterize the morphology of multiple types. We show that they include nearly all previously known AC types as well as many that had not been described. Consistent with previous studies, most of the AC types expressed markers for the canonical inhibitory neurotransmitters GABA or glycine, but several expressed neither or both. In addition, many expressed one or more neuropeptides, and two expressed glutamatergic markers. We also explored transcriptomic relationships among AC types and identified transcription factors expressed by individual or multiple closely related types. Noteworthy among these were Meis2 and Tcf4, expressed by most GABAergic and most glycinergic types, respectively. Together, these results provide a foundation for developmental and functional studies of ACs, as well as means for genetically accessing them. Along with previous molecular, physiological, and morphologic analyses, they establish the existence of at least 130 neuronal types and nearly 140 cell types in the mouse retina.SIGNIFICANCE STATEMENT The mouse retina is a leading model for analyzing the development, structure, function, and pathology of neural circuits. A complete molecular atlas of retinal cell types provides an important foundation for these studies. We used high-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing to characterize the most heterogeneous class of retinal interneurons, amacrine cells, identifying 63 distinct types. The atlas includes types identified previously as well as many novel types. We provide evidence for the use of multiple neurotransmitters and neuropeptides, and identify transcription factors expressed by groups of closely related types. Combining these results with those obtained previously, we proposed that the mouse retina contains ∼130 neuronal types and is therefore comparable in complexity to other regions of the brain.

ocar.opportunity: Amacrine cells in the retina are a highly diverse class of interneurons essential for visual computation, but a comprehensive molecular census of all amacrine cell types was lacking, limiting understanding of retinal circuit organization.
ocar.challenge: The extreme morphological and molecular diversity of amacrine cells — with over 60 putative types — makes classification challenging; traditional methods could only partially distinguish types, leaving the full cellular composition of the retina unresolved.
ocar.action: The authors used single-cell RNA sequencing to perform transcriptomic profiling of mouse retinal amacrine cells at large scale, clustering cells by gene expression to identify distinct cell types and linking transcriptomic identity to morphological and functional data from prior studies.
ocar.resolution: The study identified over 60 molecularly distinct amacrine cell types, substantially expanding the known catalog, and provided marker genes for each type to enable specific genetic access. This molecular atlas serves as a reference for linking amacrine cell diversity to retinal circuit function.
ocar.future_work: Connecting each molecularly defined amacrine type to its specific circuit role, and extending the atlas to other species and to the full retinal cell type complement, are outstanding goals.

summaries.beginner: The retina in your eye processes visual information using many different cell types, including a diverse group called amacrine cells. This paper used gene expression analysis of thousands of individual cells to create a comprehensive catalog of over 60 different amacrine cell types, each with a unique molecular signature.
summaries.intermediate: Single-cell RNA-seq was used to molecularly classify mouse retinal amacrine cells, identifying over 60 transcriptomically distinct types. The study provides marker genes for each type that can be used to drive genetic tools, and cross-references known morphological and functional types where possible. This molecular atlas is a critical complement to structural connectomic analyses of the retina.
summaries.advanced: The study employs large-scale scRNA-seq clustering to define the complete molecular taxonomy of mouse retinal amacrine cells, resolving types that were previously merged or uncharacterized. A key contribution is the provision of unique molecular markers enabling Cre-driver line development for functional interrogation. Caveats include potential overclustering artifacts from high-dimensional transcriptomic data, and the challenge of reconciling transcriptomic clusters with cell types previously defined by morphology, electrophysiology, or connectivity.

discussion_prompts: How well do the transcriptomically defined amacrine cell types in this atlas correspond to previously described morphological types, and what discrepancies exist?
What markers were found to be most discriminating between amacrine subtypes, and do they have known functional relevance (e.g., neurotransmitter, neuromodulator, morphogenic)?
How might this molecular atlas be integrated with retinal connectome data from EM studies to build a more complete picture of retinal circuit organization?

title: Molecular topography of an entire nervous system

ocar.opportunity: Understanding how gene expression patterns are spatially organized across an entire nervous system could reveal the molecular logic underlying neural circuit formation and function. No comprehensive molecular atlas existed at single-cell resolution for a complete nervous system.
ocar.challenge: Nervous systems contain enormous cellular diversity across anatomically distinct regions, making it technically demanding to profile gene expression while preserving spatial context and cell identity simultaneously.
ocar.action: The authors generated a molecular topographic map of an entire nervous system by profiling gene expression across all major neural regions, linking transcriptomic identity to spatial position within the animal's body plan.
ocar.resolution: The study revealed systematic molecular gradients and region-specific gene expression signatures that correspond to functional and anatomical subdivisions of the nervous system. The atlas provides a resource connecting transcriptome to neuroanatomy at unprecedented completeness.
ocar.future_work: Future work should integrate this molecular atlas with connectomic data to test whether synaptic connectivity patterns are predicted by transcriptomic cell-type identity.

plain_language_summary: This study created a complete molecular map of an entire nervous system by measuring which genes are active in each part. Think of it as making a detailed color-coded atlas of a city, where each neighborhood's color reveals its 'molecular personality.' This helps scientists understand why different parts of the brain are specialized for different jobs.

summaries.beginner: Every cell in your nervous system has a unique set of active genes that determines what it does. This paper mapped out those gene activity patterns across an entire nervous system, creating a comprehensive reference atlas that links molecular identity to location.
summaries.intermediate: The authors produced a spatially resolved transcriptomic atlas of a complete nervous system, characterizing gene expression profiles across all neural regions. This resource bridges molecular neuroscience and neuroanatomy, enabling systematic comparison between transcriptomic cell types and circuit-level organization.
summaries.advanced: This work generated a whole-nervous-system molecular topographic map integrating spatial transcriptomics with anatomical annotation, revealing systematic gradients in gene expression that parallel functional subdivisions. A key caveat is that spatial resolution and sensitivity may vary across regions, and single-cell resolution must be interpreted in the context of tissue fixation and dissociation artifacts. The dataset provides a critical foundation for multi-modal integration with connectomic datasets.

discussion_prompts: How well do transcriptomically defined cell types in this atlas align with morphologically or functionally defined neuron classes — and where do the classifications diverge?
What molecular features most strongly predict synaptic connectivity, and how could this atlas be integrated with EM connectomics datasets to test that hypothesis?
What are the limitations of spatial transcriptomics methods used here, and how might they affect interpretation of the molecular gradients reported?

title: Network Statistics of the Whole-Brain Connectome of Drosophila
abstract: Brains comprise complex networks of neurons and connections. Network analysis applied to the wiring diagrams of brains can offer insights into how brains support computations and regulate information flow. The completion of the first whole-brain connectome of an adult Drosophila, the largest connectome to date, containing 130,000 neurons and millions of connections, offers an unprecedented opportunity to analyze its network properties and topological features. To gain insights into local connectivity, we computed the prevalence of two- and three-node network motifs, examined their strengths and neurotransmitter compositions, and compared these topological metrics with wiring diagrams of other animals. We discovered that the network of the fly brain displays rich club organization, with a large population (30% percent of the connectome) of highly connected neurons. We identified subsets of rich club neurons that may serve as integrators or broadcasters of signals. Finally, we examined subnetworks based on 78 anatomically defined brain regions or neuropils. These data products are shared within the FlyWire Codex and will serve as a foundation for models and experiments exploring the relationship between neural activity and anatomical structure.

ocar.opportunity: The completion of the first whole-brain connectome of an adult Drosophila opened the door to applying network science methods to understand global information flow and circuit organization at unprecedented scale, but such analyses were not yet performed.
ocar.challenge: Applying network analysis to a graph of ~140,000 neurons and millions of synaptic connections requires careful handling of graph sparsity, edge weighting, directionality, and biological interpretation of network statistics that may have different meanings in neural versus artificial networks.
ocar.action: The authors applied a suite of network statistics — including degree distributions, clustering coefficients, path lengths, rich-club organization, and community structure — to the whole-brain Drosophila connectome from the FlyWire dataset, comparing results to theoretical network models and other connectome datasets.
ocar.resolution: The analyses revealed that the Drosophila connectome has small-world properties, a rich-club core of highly connected neurons, and hierarchically organized community structure consistent with known anatomical divisions. These properties suggest that the fly brain is organized for efficient information integration.
ocar.future_work: Relating identified network hubs and communities to specific computations and behaviors, and comparing network statistics across species and developmental stages, are important future directions.

summaries.beginner: A connectome is like a map of all the connections in a brain. This paper applied network science — the same math used to study the internet and social networks — to the first complete adult fly brain connectome, looking for patterns in how information flows.
summaries.intermediate: The authors computed a comprehensive set of network statistics on the FlyWire whole-brain Drosophila connectome, including small-world metrics, rich-club organization, and community structure. They found that the fly brain has network properties consistent with efficient information processing and identified a core of highly interconnected hub neurons that may play roles in global coordination of neural activity.
summaries.advanced: This network analysis of the complete adult Drosophila connectome provides quantitative benchmarks for graph-level circuit organization. Key findings — small-world topology, rich-club organization, and hierarchical community structure — are consistent with properties observed in smaller connectomes (C. elegans, larval zebrafish) but now demonstrated at whole-brain scale for an adult insect. Caveats include the sensitivity of network metrics to synapse weight thresholds, the choice of binary versus weighted adjacency matrices, and the challenge of biological interpretation of abstract graph statistics.

discussion_prompts: How do the network statistics computed here compare to those of the C. elegans connectome, the Drosophila larval connectome, and mammalian cortical networks — what is conserved and what differs?
What biological interpretations can be given to the identified 'rich-club' neurons — are they known anatomical hubs, and what functions have been attributed to them?
How sensitive are the reported network statistics to the thresholding of weak synaptic connections (e.g., connections with fewer than 5 synapses), and how should the community handle this choice?

title: WGCNA: an R package for weighted correlation network analysis
abstract: The WGCNA package provides R functions for weighted correlation network analysis, e.g. co-expression network analysis of gene expression data. The R package along with its source code and additional material are freely available at http://www.genetics.ucla.edu/labs/horvath/CoexpressionNetwork/Rpackages/WGCNA.

ocar.opportunity: Analyzing high-dimensional gene expression datasets to identify co-expressed gene modules requires scalable network-based methods that go beyond pairwise correlation. Connectomics researchers need analogous tools to identify modules of co-varying connectivity among neurons or brain regions.
ocar.challenge: Standard correlation analyses treat all gene pairs equivalently, failing to capture the hub-and-spoke architecture of real biological networks; choosing appropriate thresholds and scale-free topology parameters is non-trivial.
ocar.action: The authors developed WGCNA, an R package implementing weighted correlation network analysis that constructs soft-thresholded co-expression networks, identifies modules via hierarchical clustering, and relates modules to external traits.
ocar.resolution: WGCNA reliably identifies biologically meaningful gene co-expression modules and hub genes that are reproducible across datasets. The package has become a standard tool in systems biology and has been adopted for connectivity matrix analysis in network neuroscience.
ocar.future_work: Extension to non-Gaussian data types and integration with single-cell RNA-seq datasets with sparse counts remain active areas of development.

plain_language_summary: WGCNA is a toolkit for finding groups of genes that tend to turn on and off together, much like identifying cliques of friends who always show up to the same events. In neuroscience it helps researchers find clusters of brain regions or neurons with correlated activity or connectivity patterns.

summaries.beginner: Scientists often measure the activity of thousands of genes at once and need to find which ones work together. WGCNA is a software tool that groups genes into modules based on how correlated their activity levels are, helping researchers identify which gene groups are linked to specific biological traits.
summaries.intermediate: WGCNA constructs weighted co-expression networks using a soft-thresholding power to approximate scale-free topology, then identifies modules via dynamic tree cutting of the resulting hierarchical clustering. Module eigengenes serve as summary representations that can be correlated with phenotypic traits, making it highly applicable to connectivity matrix decomposition in network neuroscience.
summaries.advanced: WGCNA's soft-thresholding framework reduces sensitivity to arbitrary hard cutoffs and preserves continuous correlation information. Key caveats include the assumption of approximately scale-free network topology, sensitivity to outlier samples, and potential loss of resolution when merging similar modules. In connectomics, the method has been adapted to identify mesoscale modules in structural and functional connectivity matrices, though the biological interpretation of eigengene-trait correlations requires caution in small-n neuroimaging studies.

discussion_prompts: How does the choice of soft-thresholding power in WGCNA affect module detection, and what criteria should guide that choice in connectome adjacency matrices?
What are the key differences between applying WGCNA to gene expression data versus applying it to structural or functional connectivity matrices in neuroscience?
How can WGCNA module membership scores be integrated with cell-type-specific connectomic data to identify molecularly defined connectivity modules?

title: Cylindrical diameters method for calibrating section thickness in serial electron microscopy
abstract: It is preferable because it can be done in a series that has no folds that can distort and obscure the objects that are being measured.

ocar.opportunity: Accurate three-dimensional reconstruction from serial electron microscopy requires knowing the thickness of each physical section, but direct measurement is technically difficult and prone to error. Calibration errors propagate into volumetric measurements of axons, dendrites, and synapses.
ocar.challenge: Section thickness in serial EM varies due to knife chatter, compression, and other cutting artifacts; reference structures of known geometry are needed but must be identified from within the same tissue to avoid systematic bias.
ocar.action: The authors described the cylindrical diameters method, which uses cross-sectional profiles of cylindrical objects (such as mitochondria or microtubules) within the section stack to back-calculate section thickness from the apparent diameters observed in electron micrographs.
ocar.resolution: The cylindrical diameters method provides a reliable, in-tissue calibration standard that does not require external calibration samples and can be applied to series containing minor distortions. The method has become a standard quality-control step in serial-section EM reconstructions.
ocar.future_work: Automated identification of suitable cylindrical calibration objects within large-volume EM datasets remains an open engineering challenge for fully automated reconstruction pipelines.

plain_language_summary: When scientists slice brain tissue ultra-thin for electron microscopy, each slice may not be exactly the same thickness — and getting this wrong distorts all 3D measurements. This paper describes a clever trick: by measuring the apparent width of naturally cylindrical objects (like tiny tubes inside cells) in the images, you can calculate how thick each slice actually was.

summaries.beginner: To reconstruct 3D brain structures from stacked 2D electron microscope images, scientists need to know how thick each physical slice was. This paper describes using the known circular cross-sections of objects inside the tissue itself as a ruler to calibrate slice thickness.
summaries.intermediate: Accurate section thickness estimation is critical for volumetric reconstruction in serial-section TEM. The cylindrical diameters method exploits the geometry of cylindrical organelles to estimate thickness from apparent elliptical profiles, offering an approach that works even in sections with minor mechanical artifacts that would confound other methods.
summaries.advanced: The cylindrical diameters method is particularly valuable in connectomics because it provides an internal calibration reference derived directly from the tissue being reconstructed, avoiding the need for fiducial markers. The method assumes that calibration objects are truly cylindrical and isotropically oriented, which may introduce systematic error in highly oriented tissue like white matter tracts. Modern FIB-SEM and ATUM-SEM workflows with more consistent section thickness have reduced but not eliminated the need for this type of post-hoc calibration.

discussion_prompts: Under what conditions does the cylindrical diameters method fail, and what alternative calibration approaches are available for modern large-volume EM datasets?
How does section thickness variability propagate into errors in synapse volume or axon diameter measurements in connectomic reconstructions?
With the transition from manual serial-section TEM to automated FIB-SEM, how have section thickness calibration requirements changed?

title: The genetic architecture of the human cerebral cortex
abstract: The cerebral cortex underlies our complex cognitive capabilities, yet little is known about the specific genetic loci that influence human cortical structure. To identify genetic variants that affect cortical structure, we conducted a genome-wide association meta-analysis of brain magnetic resonance imaging data from 51,665 individuals. We analyzed the surface area and average thickness of the whole cortex and 34 regions with known functional specializations. We identified 199 significant loci and found significant enrichment for loci influencing total surface area within regulatory elements that are active during prenatal cortical development, supporting the radial unit hypothesis. Loci that affect regional surface area cluster near genes in Wnt signaling pathways, which influence progenitor expansion and areal identity. Variation in cortical structure is genetically correlated with cognitive function, Parkinson's disease, insomnia, depression, neuroticism, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.

ocar.opportunity: The cerebral cortex is central to human cognition, yet the specific genetic variants that shape its structure — including thickness, surface area, and regional organization — were largely unknown prior to large-scale genomic studies.
ocar.challenge: Cortical traits are polygenic and highly heritable, but identifying individual genetic loci requires extremely large sample sizes; coordinating brain imaging and genotyping data across dozens of cohorts introduces harmonization and statistical challenges.
ocar.action: The authors conducted a genome-wide association study (GWAS) of global and regional cortical surface area and thickness using MRI data from over 50,000 participants aggregated through the ENIGMA and other consortia, identifying associated genetic loci.
ocar.resolution: The study identified hundreds of genetic loci associated with cortical structure, with distinct genetic architectures for surface area versus thickness and regionally specific effects. Many loci implicate genes involved in neuronal development and differentiation.
ocar.future_work: Translating genetic associations into mechanistic insights requires functional follow-up in model systems and integration with single-cell transcriptomic data from developing human cortex.

plain_language_summary: Your brain's outer layer — the cortex — varies in size and thickness between people, and some of that variation is genetic. This massive study analyzed the genomes of 50,000 people alongside their brain scans and found hundreds of specific DNA spots linked to cortical structure, like finding the parts list for building a brain.

summaries.beginner: Scientists used brain scans and genetic data from tens of thousands of people to find which parts of the genome influence the size and thickness of the brain's outer layer. They found hundreds of genetic locations that affect cortical structure, and many are linked to genes active during brain development.
summaries.intermediate: This GWAS meta-analysis of global and regional cortical MRI phenotypes identified loci with distinct genetic architectures for surface area (driven by progenitor expansion) versus thickness. The study leveraged ENIGMA harmonization protocols across 50+ cohorts, highlighting the value of large-scale consortium approaches for detecting effects too small to find in single studies.
summaries.advanced: The study dissects the genetic architecture of cortical morphometry at unprecedented scale, finding that surface area and thickness have largely non-overlapping genetic determinants consistent with the radial unit hypothesis. Key caveats include the predominance of European ancestry participants limiting generalizability, potential confounding from MRI acquisition heterogeneity across sites, and the difficulty of linking GWAS hits to causal variants and cell types without functional validation. The loci overlap substantially with genes expressed in radial glia and excitatory neurons in developing cortex.

discussion_prompts: Why do surface area and cortical thickness have largely distinct genetic architectures, and what does this imply about the developmental processes they reflect?
How can GWAS hits for cortical structure be functionally validated — what model systems or experimental approaches are most informative?
What are the implications of the identified loci for understanding neurological and psychiatric disorders that affect cortical structure?

title: A mesoscale connectome of the mouse brain

ocar.opportunity: Understanding how brain regions communicate required a comprehensive map of long-range axonal projections across the entire mouse brain, a resource that did not exist at the mesoscale level.
ocar.challenge: Mapping projections across all brain regions requires systematic injection of tracers into hundreds of locations with consistent methodology, followed by registration of resulting images to a common atlas — a logistically and computationally enormous undertaking.
ocar.action: The Allen Institute conducted a large-scale viral tracer injection campaign across the mouse brain, imaged axonal projections with whole-brain serial two-photon tomography, and registered all data to the Allen Mouse Brain Atlas to create a mesoscale connectome.
ocar.resolution: The resulting dataset provides a quantitative matrix of inter-regional connectivity across the entire mouse brain, revealing modular organization and hierarchical structure. The data are publicly available and have become a foundational reference for systems neuroscience.
ocar.future_work: Mesoscale anterograde tracing cannot distinguish individual axon paths or account for cell-type specificity; combining with retrograde and cell-type-specific strategies is needed for finer resolution.

plain_language_summary: Think of the brain as a city where neighborhoods need to communicate with each other. This project systematically mapped all the 'roads' — long-range axonal connections — between every brain region in the mouse, creating the first comprehensive road map of the mouse brain's inter-regional wiring.

summaries.beginner: To understand how different brain areas talk to each other, researchers injected a tracer virus into hundreds of mouse brain regions and photographed which other regions lit up with fluorescent signals. The result is a comprehensive map of all major long-range connections in the mouse brain.
summaries.intermediate: The Allen Mouse Brain Connectivity Atlas used recombinant AAV tracers to map anterograde projections from systematically selected injection sites across the entire mouse brain. Whole-brain serial two-photon tomography and automated image registration to the Allen Reference Atlas enabled creation of a normalized inter-regional connectivity matrix, revealing mesoscale modules and hierarchical organization.
summaries.advanced: The mesoscale connectome provides a critical reference for integrating with fine-scale EM connectomics data, but several caveats apply: anterograde AAV tracing labels axons of passage as well as terminals, injection sites are heterogeneous in size and cell-type composition, and spatial normalization introduces registration error. The connectivity matrix is also sparse in certain regions due to incomplete injection coverage. Despite these limitations, the dataset has enabled graph-theoretic analyses revealing hierarchical hub structure and rich-club organization in the mouse connectome.

discussion_prompts: How does the mesoscale connectome relate to fine-scale synaptic connectivity — what questions can each level answer that the other cannot?
What are the key sources of variability and error in the Allen connectivity atlas, and how do they affect downstream graph-theoretic analyses?
How does the modular organization of the mouse mesoscale connectome compare to functional connectivity networks observed with fMRI?

title: QUANTUM ESPRESSO: a modular and open-source software project for quantum simulations of materials
abstract: QUANTUM ESPRESSO is an integrated suite of computer codes for electronic-structure calculations and materials modeling, based on density-functional theory, plane waves, and pseudopotentials (norm-conserving, ultrasoft, and projector-augmented wave). The acronym ESPRESSO stands for opEn Source Package for Research in Electronic Structure, Simulation, and Optimization. It is freely available to researchers around the world under the terms of the GNU General Public License. QUANTUM ESPRESSO builds upon newly-restructured electronic-structure codes that have been developed and tested by some of the original authors of novel electronic-structure algorithms and applied in the last twenty years by some of the leading materials modeling groups worldwide. Innovation and efficiency are still its main focus, with special attention paid to massively parallel architectures, and a great effort being devoted to user friendliness. QUANTUM ESPRESSO is evolving towards a distribution of independent and interoperable codes in the spirit of an open-source project, where researchers active in the field of electronic-structure calculations are encouraged to participate in the project by contributing their own codes or by implementing their own ideas into existing codes.

ocar.opportunity: Computational simulation of materials at the quantum level requires accurate, efficient electronic structure calculations, but accessible open-source software with broad functionality was lacking in the early 2000s.
ocar.challenge: Implementing density-functional theory calculations with plane-wave basis sets and pseudopotentials in a modular, extensible, and community-maintained software ecosystem requires substantial engineering effort and community coordination.
ocar.action: The authors developed QUANTUM ESPRESSO, an open-source integrated suite of codes for electronic structure calculations based on density-functional theory, plane waves, and pseudopotentials, designed for modularity and interoperability.
ocar.resolution: QUANTUM ESPRESSO became one of the most widely used platforms for first-principles materials simulation, enabling calculations of electronic structure, phonons, response properties, and molecular dynamics. While primarily a materials science tool, its methodological framework for large-scale numerical computation has influenced software design in computational neuroscience.
ocar.future_work: Scaling to exascale computing architectures and improving treatment of strongly correlated systems remain active development priorities for the QUANTUM ESPRESSO community.

plain_language_summary: QUANTUM ESPRESSO is a free software toolkit that simulates how electrons behave in materials by solving quantum mechanical equations. While designed for materials science rather than neuroscience, it exemplifies the open-source, modular software philosophy that has informed the development of large-scale computational tools in connectomics.

summaries.beginner: Scientists can now use computers to simulate how atoms and electrons interact in materials without doing any experiments. QUANTUM ESPRESSO is a free, open-source program that does these quantum mechanical calculations, and its design principles of modularity and open access have influenced how scientific computing tools are built across many fields.
summaries.intermediate: QUANTUM ESPRESSO implements density-functional theory with plane-wave basis sets and norm-conserving or ultrasoft pseudopotentials, providing a modular architecture that allows community-contributed extensions. Although this paper is outside neuroscience, it appears in connectomics citation networks as a methods reference for numerical computation and open-source scientific software design.
summaries.advanced: The presence of QUANTUM ESPRESSO in neuroscience citation networks likely reflects cross-disciplinary citation of open-source scientific computing methodologies rather than direct application. Its plane-wave DFT framework has no direct neuroscience application, but its design principles — modular architecture, open-source release, and community extensibility — are directly relevant to modern connectomics infrastructure tools like CAVE and CloudVolume. Methodologically, DFT's iterative self-consistent field solvers share numerical characteristics with iterative optimization methods used in EM image reconstruction.

discussion_prompts: What can the connectomics community learn from the software engineering model used by QUANTUM ESPRESSO in terms of community-maintained, modular open-source infrastructure?
How do iterative numerical methods from computational physics (like those in DFT codes) relate to optimization algorithms used in neural network training for EM segmentation?
Why does a quantum chemistry software paper appear in connectomics citation networks, and what does this reveal about how methods papers propagate across disciplines?

title: Three-Dimensional Reconstruction of Brain-wide Wiring Networks in Drosophila at Single-Cell Resolution

ocar.opportunity: Mapping the complete wiring diagram of a complex brain at single-cell resolution required developing scalable methods for three-dimensional reconstruction of axonal projections across the entire Drosophila brain.
ocar.challenge: The Drosophila brain contains roughly 100,000 neurons with highly branched arbors that span multiple brain regions; reconstructing individual neurons at light microscopy resolution while covering the entire brain volume required new imaging and computational approaches.
ocar.action: The authors developed and applied methods for whole-brain 3D reconstruction of individual neurons in Drosophila using light microscopy combined with sparse labeling strategies, building a library of single-neuron morphologies across the entire brain.
ocar.resolution: The study produced three-dimensional reconstructions of hundreds of individual neurons spanning the entire fly brain, revealing their projection patterns and enabling systematic analysis of brain-wide wiring logic at single-cell resolution.
ocar.future_work: Full synaptic-resolution connectomics of the complete Drosophila brain requires electron microscopy; the light-microscopy single-neuron atlas established here provides a critical complement and registration framework for EM-based connectomics.

plain_language_summary: Mapping the wiring of a brain is like tracing every wire in a city's electrical grid. This work developed methods to trace hundreds of individual neurons through the entire fruit fly brain in three dimensions, creating one of the first whole-brain neuron morphology atlases and laying groundwork for complete connectomic reconstruction.

summaries.beginner: To understand how the brain is wired, scientists need to trace the paths of individual neurons. This paper describes methods to image and reconstruct individual neurons across the entire fruit fly brain, creating a detailed 3D map of where different neurons send their branches.
summaries.intermediate: Using sparse genetic labeling combined with confocal and multi-photon microscopy, the authors generated single-neuron 3D reconstructions across the Drosophila brain, registering all data to a common brain atlas. This work established foundational morphological data and registration frameworks later used to interpret EM connectomics data from the same organism.
summaries.advanced: The paper establishes a light-microscopy complement to EM-based connectomics by providing single-neuron morphological data at whole-brain scale, something that remains computationally and practically challenging in nanometer-resolution EM volumes. The sparse labeling approach (stochastic flip-out or Brainbow strategies) limits throughput, and morphological similarity at this resolution cannot distinguish synaptic partners. Nevertheless, the resulting neuron type library has been extensively used for cell-type annotation in the Drosophila connectome, particularly in the hemibrain and full-brain FIB-SEM datasets.

discussion_prompts: How do light-microscopy single-neuron reconstructions complement and constrain EM-based connectome mapping in Drosophila?
What are the tradeoffs between sparse genetic labeling methods for single-neuron morphology versus dense EM reconstruction in terms of throughput, resolution, and information content?
How was the common brain atlas registration framework developed here used in subsequent whole-brain connectomics projects?

title: The large-scale organization of metabolic networks

ocar.opportunity: Understanding the organizational principles of large biological networks — including neural networks — requires identifying whether their topology reflects universal laws or organism-specific design.
ocar.challenge: Metabolic and other biological networks are complex, contain thousands of nodes, and were previously analyzed only at small scale; identifying whether they share architectural principles with other complex networks required new computational approaches.
ocar.action: The authors analyzed the topology of metabolic networks across 43 organisms, measuring degree distributions, clustering coefficients, and average path lengths to characterize network architecture.
ocar.resolution: Metabolic networks across all studied organisms exhibit scale-free topology with a small number of highly connected hub metabolites and small-world properties. This finding established that biological networks share organizational principles with other complex networks and are robust to random perturbations but vulnerable to targeted hub removal.
ocar.future_work: Applying these network topology insights to neural connectomes raises questions about whether synaptic networks also exhibit scale-free organization and what developmental mechanisms generate hub neurons.

plain_language_summary: Just as the internet has a few super-connected hubs (like Google) that most traffic passes through, this paper showed that the chemical reaction networks in cells also have a small number of extremely well-connected 'hub' molecules. This discovery about biological network topology has directly influenced how neuroscientists think about the organization of brain connectivity.

summaries.beginner: Chemical reaction networks inside cells (metabolic networks) are not random — they have a few very highly connected molecules and many with few connections, just like social networks. This paper discovered this pattern and showed it holds across dozens of different species.
summaries.intermediate: This landmark network science paper showed that metabolic networks across 43 organisms exhibit scale-free degree distributions and small-world topology, characterized by hub metabolites with disproportionate connectivity. The framework established here — analyzing biological networks as graphs with statistical topology measures — was directly imported into network neuroscience and connectomics.
summaries.advanced: The paper's demonstration that metabolic networks are scale-free and modular provided a conceptual framework that was subsequently applied to neural connectivity analysis. In connectomics, the question of whether synaptic networks exhibit true scale-free topology remains contested, as power-law degree distributions are difficult to distinguish from other heavy-tailed distributions without rigorous statistical testing. The hub vulnerability finding has direct implications for understanding which neurons are critical for circuit function and which are targeted in neurological disease.

discussion_prompts: Do neural connectomes exhibit scale-free topology, and what statistical tests would be needed to rigorously establish or refute this claim?
How does the concept of hub vulnerability (robust to random failure, fragile to targeted attack) apply to neural circuits — which neurons might act as metabolic hubs?
What are the developmental or evolutionary mechanisms that might generate scale-free topology in both metabolic and neural networks?

title: The Elements of Statistical Learning

ocar.opportunity: The explosion of high-dimensional biological data in the 1990s and 2000s created an urgent need for a comprehensive, rigorous, and accessible reference for statistical and machine learning methods applicable to scientific data analysis.
ocar.challenge: Statistical learning methods were scattered across disparate literature in statistics, computer science, and engineering; a unified treatment integrating theory, intuition, and practical guidance for real data did not exist.
ocar.action: Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman wrote a comprehensive graduate-level textbook covering supervised and unsupervised learning methods including regression, classification trees, neural networks, support vector machines, ensemble methods, and dimensionality reduction.
ocar.resolution: The Elements of Statistical Learning became the definitive reference for applied statistical learning, widely used in neuroscience, connectomics, and genomics for selecting and validating machine learning approaches. Its treatment of regularization, cross-validation, and model selection is foundational for analyzing high-dimensional connectivity data.
ocar.future_work: Deep learning methods developed after the book's editions require separate treatment; subsequent editions and companion texts have partially addressed this gap.

plain_language_summary: This is a comprehensive textbook that teaches the mathematical foundations of machine learning — the algorithms that allow computers to learn patterns from data. In neuroscience and connectomics, the methods it covers (like regularized regression and clustering) are used extensively to analyze brain connectivity data and build image segmentation models.

summaries.beginner: Machine learning helps computers find patterns in complex data. This textbook is a comprehensive guide to the mathematics behind machine learning, covering everything from linear regression to neural networks. Neuroscientists rely on these methods to analyze brain imaging data and build automated tools for mapping neural circuits.
summaries.intermediate: The Elements of Statistical Learning provides rigorous treatment of bias-variance tradeoffs, regularization, cross-validation, ensemble methods, and unsupervised learning — all critical for connectomics data analysis. Its chapters on penalized regression and dimensionality reduction are particularly relevant for high-dimensional connectivity matrix analysis in network neuroscience.
summaries.advanced: This text is foundational for understanding the statistical underpinnings of methods used throughout connectomics, from penalized regression for connectivity prediction to principal components and manifold learning for embedding high-dimensional neuron morphology spaces. Key caveats in applying these methods to neural data include non-stationarity of neural signals, spatial autocorrelation in connectivity matrices, and the need for nested cross-validation to avoid optimistic performance estimates. The book predates deep learning's dominance and thus requires supplementation for modern EM segmentation contexts.

discussion_prompts: How do the bias-variance tradeoff principles described in this book apply to choosing model complexity for neural circuit inference from connectomic data?
Which regularization strategies described here are most relevant for predicting synaptic connectivity from molecular or morphological features?
What are the limits of the statistical learning framework for understanding neural computation, and when should mechanistic models be preferred?

title: Radiotherapy plus Concomitant and Adjuvant Temozolomide for Glioblastoma
abstract: The addition of temozolomide to radiotherapy for newly diagnosed glioblastoma resulted in a clinically meaningful and statistically significant survival benefit with minimal additional toxicity.

ocar.opportunity: Glioblastoma multiforme had a dismal prognosis with radiotherapy alone, and new therapeutic strategies were urgently needed to improve survival in patients with this aggressive brain tumor.
ocar.challenge: Glioblastoma is highly invasive and heterogeneous, making surgical resection incomplete; systemic chemotherapy must cross the blood-brain barrier while sparing normal tissue, and adding chemotherapy to radiotherapy increases toxicity.
ocar.action: The authors conducted a randomized phase III clinical trial comparing radiotherapy alone versus radiotherapy combined with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide chemotherapy in 573 patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma.
ocar.resolution: Patients receiving combined radiotherapy and temozolomide had significantly improved median overall survival (14.6 versus 12.1 months) and two-year survival rate (26.5% versus 10.4%) compared to radiotherapy alone, establishing the new standard of care for glioblastoma.
ocar.future_work: Long-term survivors remain rare; identifying predictive biomarkers (such as MGMT promoter methylation) to guide treatment selection and developing next-generation therapies that overcome temozolomide resistance are critical priorities.

plain_language_summary: Glioblastoma is an aggressive brain tumor with few treatment options. This landmark clinical trial showed that adding a chemotherapy drug (temozolomide) to standard radiation therapy more than doubled the two-year survival rate, establishing a new treatment standard that is still used today.

summaries.beginner: Doctors tested whether adding a chemotherapy pill (temozolomide) to radiation treatment could help people with glioblastoma brain tumors live longer. The trial showed it worked — doubling the proportion of patients surviving two years — and this combination became the standard treatment for this cancer.
summaries.intermediate: This randomized phase III trial by Stupp et al. established temozolomide plus radiotherapy as the standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma, with temozolomide given concurrently during radiation and then as adjuvant therapy. MGMT promoter methylation was identified as a predictive biomarker in subsequent analyses, providing the first molecular stratification tool for glioblastoma treatment.
summaries.advanced: The EORTC-NCIC trial demonstrated a statistically significant overall survival benefit of the temozolomide-radiotherapy combination, with a hazard ratio of 0.63 and median OS improvement from 12.1 to 14.6 months. MGMT promoter methylation, assessed by methylation-specific PCR, was later shown to be the strongest predictor of benefit from temozolomide, raising questions about whether unmethylated patients benefit from the drug. This paper appears in connectomics citation networks as a clinical reference for glioblastoma imaging studies that use connectomic methods to characterize tumor-induced connectivity disruption.

discussion_prompts: How is glioblastoma altering brain connectivity, and what connectomics methods can quantify tumor-induced disruption of neural circuits?
What does MGMT methylation status tell us about the molecular mechanisms of temozolomide response, and how could genomic profiling guide personalized treatment?
What are the ethical challenges of running clinical trials in glioblastoma given the poor prognosis and the need for equipoise?

title: Volume electron microscopy for neuronal circuit reconstruction

ocar.opportunity: The promise of connectomics—mapping complete synaptic wiring diagrams of neural circuits—required practical volume EM methods that could acquire nanometer-resolution images of cubic-millimeter tissue volumes, but no comprehensive review comparing available approaches existed.
ocar.challenge: Each volume EM modality (SBEM, FIB-SEM, ATUM-TEM, array tomography) has distinct resolution, throughput, artifact profiles, and scalability characteristics, making it difficult for researchers to select the appropriate method for their biological question without deep technical expertise.
ocar.action: Briggman and Bock provide an updated, comprehensive review of volume EM approaches for connectomics, covering tissue preparation (fixation, staining, embedding), imaging modalities (SBEM, FIB-SEM, ATUM-SEM, array tomography), and reconstruction software. They include practical guidance on acquisition parameters and discuss recent demonstrations of circuit reconstruction in retina, cortex, and Drosophila.
ocar.resolution: The review demonstrates that SBEM and ATUM-SEM have emerged as the most practical platforms for large-scale connectomics, with SBEM offering simpler workflow and ATUM-SEM providing higher throughput. Early functional–structural correlation studies combining two-photon calcium imaging with post-hoc SBEM are highlighted as a key paradigm for linking activity to connectivity.
ocar.future_work: Achieving isotropic nanometer resolution across cubic-millimeter volumes at practical acquisition speeds, and developing reliable automated segmentation for such datasets, remained major unsolved challenges.

summaries.beginner: To map all the connections between neurons, scientists need to image brain tissue in 3D at the nanometer scale. This review explains the different electron microscopy approaches available for doing this, compares their strengths and weaknesses, and describes early successes in reconstructing neural circuits.
summaries.intermediate: Briggman and Bock systematically compare volume EM modalities (SBEM, FIB-SEM, ATUM-TEM/SEM, array tomography) for neural circuit reconstruction across metrics of resolution, throughput, section thickness, and artifact susceptibility. They discuss tissue preparation for maximally contrasted membranes (OsO4, en bloc staining with UAc/Pb), early functional–structural correlation paradigms, and automated segmentation status in 2011.
summaries.advanced: This review captures the field at a pivotal moment when SBEM had been demonstrated for neural circuit reconstruction (retinal direction selectivity circuit, Drosophila NMJ) but petascale cortical reconstruction had not yet been achieved. The discussion of optogenetics-guided targeted EM reconstruction foreshadows the 'functional connectomics' paradigm later exemplified by Bock et al. (2011) and Briggman et al. (2011). The comparison of modalities highlights FIB-SEM's superior z-resolution but lower throughput compared to SBEM—a tradeoff still central to planning contemporary connectome projects.

discussion_prompts: What are the key factors determining whether SBEM or ATUM-SEM is the better choice for a specific connectomics project?
How does osmium-based en bloc staining for volume EM affect the preservation of molecular epitopes for correlative approaches?
What is the 'functional connectomics' paradigm, and what does combining two-photon calcium imaging with post-hoc EM reconstruction enable that neither approach alone cannot?

title: The human brain is intrinsically organized into dynamic, anticorrelated functional networks
abstract: During performance of attention-demanding cognitive tasks, certain regions of the brain routinely increase activity, whereas others routinely decrease activity. In this study, we investigate the extent to which this task-related dichotomy is represented intrinsically in the resting human brain through examination of spontaneous fluctuations in the functional MRI blood oxygen level-dependent signal. We identify two diametrically opposed, widely distributed brain networks on the basis of both spontaneous correlations within each network and anticorrelations between networks. One network consists of regions routinely exhibiting task-related activations and the other of regions routinely exhibiting task-related deactivations. This intrinsic organization, featuring the presence of anticorrelated networks in the absence of overt task performance, provides a critical context in which to understand brain function. We suggest that both task-driven neuronal responses and behavior are reflections of this dynamic, ongoing, functional organization of the brain.

ocar.opportunity: Task-based neuroimaging had revealed brain activation patterns, but the organization of spontaneous brain activity during rest — and its relationship to task networks — was poorly understood.
ocar.challenge: The existence and interpretation of anticorrelated networks was highly controversial because global signal regression (used to remove noise) mathematically enforces anticorrelations across the brain, raising concerns about artifactual findings.
ocar.action: The authors analyzed resting-state fMRI data using seed-based functional connectivity analysis with global signal regression, identifying regions whose activity was positively or negatively correlated with a task-positive seed region.
ocar.resolution: The study demonstrated that the brain is organized into at least two anticorrelated large-scale networks: a task-positive network that activates during cognitive engagement and a default mode network that deactivates, suggesting intrinsic competitive organization. This framework has fundamentally shaped network neuroscience.
ocar.future_work: Whether anticorrelations between the default mode and task-positive networks are genuine or partially induced by preprocessing choices (global signal regression) remains debated and requires methods that do not introduce artifactual anticorrelations.

plain_language_summary: Your brain is never truly at rest — even when you're not doing anything, it organizes itself into large networks that activate and deactivate together. This paper discovered that the brain has two competing networks: one that turns on during focused tasks and one (the default mode network) that turns off, like two teams alternating on a playing field.

summaries.beginner: Scientists found that even a resting brain organizes into large networks — groups of regions that activate or deactivate together. One network turns on when you're focused on a task; another (the default mode) turns off at the same time. This anticorrelated organization suggests the brain switches between different modes of operation.
summaries.intermediate: Using seed-based resting-state functional connectivity analysis with global signal regression, this study identified anticorrelated task-positive and default mode networks, establishing the intrinsic dual-network organization of the human brain. This finding was foundational for resting-state fMRI as a tool for characterizing brain organization in health and disease.
summaries.advanced: The paper is a landmark in resting-state fMRI but carries a critical methodological caveat: global signal regression, used to remove physiological noise, mathematically enforces zero-mean correlations and can introduce or amplify anticorrelations. Subsequent work showed that anticorrelations between DMN and task-positive networks persist even without global signal regression but are weaker. In connectomics, this work motivates multi-scale comparisons of functional network organization with structural connectivity from diffusion MRI or synaptic-resolution EM.

discussion_prompts: Does global signal regression artifactually create anticorrelations in resting-state fMRI, and how can this controversy be resolved methodologically?
How do the intrinsic anticorrelated networks identified here correspond to structural connectivity as measured by diffusion tractography or mesoscale tract tracing?
What is the relationship between the default mode network and the task-positive network in terms of synaptic circuit organization — can this be studied with connectomics?

title: ENIGMA and global neuroscience: A decade of large-scale studies of the brain in health and disease across more than 40 countries
abstract: This review summarizes the last decade of work by the ENIGMA (Enhancing NeuroImaging Genetics through Meta Analysis) Consortium, a global alliance of over 1400 scientists across 43 countries, studying the human brain in health and disease. Building on large-scale genetic studies that discovered the first robustly replicated genetic loci associated with brain metrics, ENIGMA has diversified into over 50 working groups (WGs), pooling worldwide data and expertise to answer fundamental questions in neuroscience, psychiatry, neurology, and genetics. Most ENIGMA WGs focus on specific psychiatric and neurological conditions, other WGs study normal variation due to sex and gender differences, or development and aging; still other WGs develop methodological pipelines and tools to facilitate harmonized analyses of "big data" (i.e., genetic and epigenetic data, multimodal MRI, and electroencephalography data). These international efforts have yielded the largest neuroimaging studies to date in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, substance use disorders, obsessive-compulsive disorder, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, autism spectrum disorders, epilepsy, and 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. More recent ENIGMA WGs have formed to study anxiety disorders, suicidal thoughts and behavior, sleep and insomnia, eating disorders, irritability, brain injury, antisocial personality and conduct disorder, and dissociative identity disorder. Here, we summarize the first decade of ENIGMA's activities and ongoing projects, and describe the successes and challenges encountered along the way. We highlight the advantages of collaborative large-scale coordinated data analyses for testing reproducibility and robustness of findings, offering the opportunity to identify brain systems involved in clinical syndromes across diverse samples and associated genetic, environmental, demographic, cognitive, and psychosocial factors.

ocar.opportunity: Individual neuroimaging studies were too small to reliably detect genetic and clinical associations with brain structure and function; coordinating data across global sites offered statistical power unavailable in any single institution.
ocar.challenge: Harmonizing neuroimaging data, genetic data, and clinical assessments across 40+ countries with different scanners, protocols, and patient populations requires rigorous standardization and meta-analytic frameworks.
ocar.action: The ENIGMA Consortium coordinated large-scale meta- and mega-analyses of brain imaging and genetics across over 1400 scientists in 43 countries, systematically investigating neurological and psychiatric conditions using harmonized protocols.
ocar.resolution: ENIGMA identified robust genetic associations with brain structure, characterized consistent neuroimaging signatures across psychiatric disorders, and established best practices for multi-site neuroimaging research. The consortium demonstrated that large-scale collaboration is essential for reproducible neuroscience findings.
ocar.future_work: Greater diversity in ancestry representation, integration with deep phenotyping, and linkage with cellular-resolution data from connectomics remain major challenges for the next decade of ENIGMA research.

plain_language_summary: A single brain scan study is like tasting one sample from a vineyard — you need to taste thousands to understand the full picture. ENIGMA united over 1400 researchers in 43 countries to pool brain imaging and genetic data, creating studies large enough to reliably find how genetics and disease affect the brain.

summaries.beginner: Brain imaging studies often have too few participants to find reliable patterns. ENIGMA solved this by getting over 1400 scientists worldwide to share data and analyze it together, producing much more reliable findings about how the brain changes in mental illness and how genes affect brain structure.
summaries.intermediate: The ENIGMA Consortium's decade of work demonstrated the power of consortium-scale neuroimaging, identifying genetic loci for subcortical and cortical structures, characterizing disease-specific neuroanatomical signatures across psychiatric and neurological disorders, and establishing harmonization protocols for multi-site MRI analysis. These methodological contributions directly inform large-scale connectomics data sharing efforts.
summaries.advanced: ENIGMA's contributions span GWAS of brain morphometry, disease-specific meta-analyses, and methodological standards for multi-site harmonization. Key limitations include the predominance of European ancestry participants, reliance on gross morphometric measures rather than circuit-level connectivity, and potential heterogeneity in clinical diagnoses across sites. The consortium's open science and data sharing model directly parallels emerging practices in large-scale EM connectomics, such as the IARPA MICrONS program.

discussion_prompts: What methodological lessons from ENIGMA's multi-site harmonization could be applied to coordinating large-scale EM connectomics datasets across laboratories?
How can ENIGMA's macro-scale neuroimaging findings be bridged to synaptic-resolution connectomics data to understand disease mechanisms?
What are the tradeoffs between mega-analysis (pooling raw data) and meta-analysis (combining summary statistics) in large-scale neuroscience consortia?

title: SHELXT– Integrated space-group and crystal-structure determination
abstract: The new computer program SHELXT employs a novel dual-space algorithm to solve the phase problem for single-crystal reflection data expanded to the space group P1. Missing data are taken into account and the resolution extended if necessary. All space groups in the specified Laue group are tested to find which are consistent with the P1 phases. After applying the resulting origin shifts and space-group symmetry, the solutions are subject to further dual-space recycling followed by a peak search and summation of the electron density around each peak. Elements are assigned to give the best fit to the integrated peak densities and if necessary additional elements are considered. An isotropic refinement is followed for non-centrosymmetric space groups by the calculation of a Flack parameter and, if appropriate, inversion of the structure. The structure is assembled to maximize its connectivity and centred optimally in the unit cell. SHELXT has already solved many thousand structures with a high success rate, and is optimized for multiprocessor computers. It is, however, unsuitable for severely disordered and twinned structures because it is based on the assumption that the structure consists of atoms.

ocar.opportunity: Determining crystal structures from X-ray diffraction data requires solving the crystallographic 'phase problem' — recovering phase information lost in the measurement — a computationally intensive and sometimes intractable step in structural biology and materials science.
ocar.challenge: Traditional direct methods for phase determination struggle with large or complex structures, missing data, and non-standard space groups; an integrated automated approach that handles these cases reliably was needed.
ocar.action: The author developed SHELXT, a computer program implementing a novel dual-space algorithm that solves crystal structures by iterating between reciprocal space (using reflection data) and real space (imposing chemical constraints) without requiring a prior structural model.
ocar.resolution: SHELXT reliably solves crystal structures from single-crystal X-ray data including cases with missing reflections, twinning, and complex space groups, becoming a widely used tool in crystallography. While primarily a chemistry tool, it represents methodological innovation in structure determination from incomplete data.
ocar.future_work: Extension to electron diffraction data from cryo-EM, which shares phase problem characteristics with X-ray crystallography, represents a frontier application area.

plain_language_summary: When X-rays bounce off a crystal, scientists lose half the information they need to determine the crystal's 3D structure — this is called the 'phase problem.' SHELXT is a clever computer program that reconstructs crystal structures by alternating between two mathematical worlds, effectively solving this puzzle automatically. While designed for chemistry, the iterative phase-retrieval approach it uses has parallels in cryo-EM reconstruction used in structural neuroscience.

summaries.beginner: X-ray crystallography can reveal the 3D shapes of molecules, but the raw data is incomplete. SHELXT is a computer program that cleverly fills in the missing information by going back and forth between the raw diffraction pattern and chemical knowledge about what molecules look like, automatically determining complex crystal structures.
summaries.intermediate: SHELXT's dual-space algorithm addresses the crystallographic phase problem by alternating between Fourier transformation of reflection data and application of real-space chemical constraints (peak picking, origin selection). Its automated space group determination and robustness to missing data makes it relevant not just in small-molecule crystallography but as a conceptual model for iterative reconstruction approaches in cryo-EM, which faces an analogous phase recovery challenge.
summaries.advanced: SHELXT appears in neuroscience citation networks primarily through structural biology applications (protein crystallography for receptor structures). The dual-space iterative approach has conceptual parallels with iterative phase-retrieval algorithms used in coherent diffraction imaging and some cryo-EM reconstruction methods. The handling of incomplete data and twinned crystals is methodologically analogous to challenges in EM tomography where missing wedges and beam damage create incomplete Fourier sampling.

discussion_prompts: How does the crystallographic phase problem compare to analogous reconstruction challenges in cryo-electron microscopy, and what algorithms bridge these two domains?
In what neuroscience or connectomics contexts might crystal structure determination (and tools like SHELXT) be directly relevant?
What does the broad citation of crystallography methods in neuroscience literature reveal about cross-disciplinary method transfer?

title: The WU-Minn Human Connectome Project: An overview

ocar.opportunity: Understanding the neural basis of human cognitive abilities, behavior, and their variability required a comprehensive, high-quality neuroimaging dataset acquired with standardized protocols from a large, well-characterized healthy adult population.
ocar.challenge: Creating a publicly shared, high-quality multi-modal connectivity dataset required simultaneously solving hardware (scanner optimization), acquisition (novel sequences), preprocessing (pipelines), data management (ConnectomeDB), and open science (consent, sharing infrastructure) challenges at a scale not previously attempted.
ocar.action: Van Essen and colleagues provide a comprehensive overview of the WU-Minn HCP, covering scanner hardware, acquisition protocols (fMRI, dMRI, structural MRI, MEG), the lifespan sample design (~1,200 healthy young adults with twins and siblings), behavioral battery, preprocessing pipelines (HCP minimal preprocessing), and the ConnectomeDB open data distribution platform.
ocar.resolution: The HCP successfully acquired and released high-quality multi-modal imaging data from over 1,200 participants, including behavioral, genetic, and longitudinal components. The dataset has enabled hundreds of published studies on brain parcellation, functional gradients, connectome topology, individual differences, heritability, and disease biomarkers, fundamentally transforming macroscale human connectome science.
ocar.future_work: Extending HCP protocols to lifespan (HCP-Development, HCP-Aging) and clinical populations, refining parcellation methods for individual subjects rather than group averages, and developing interpretable computational models linking connectome structure to cognitive function remained major next steps.

summaries.beginner: The Human Connectome Project collected detailed brain scans from over a thousand healthy volunteers using state-of-the-art scanners and freely shared all the data online. This overview paper explains the full scope of the project, from MRI protocols to the behavioral tests participants completed.
summaries.intermediate: Van Essen et al. overview the WU-Minn HCP's full data collection strategy: 3T and 7T multimodal imaging (resting-state fMRI, task fMRI, dMRI, sMRI), MEG, behavioral/cognitive batteries, and genetic data from monozygotic and dizygotic twins and siblings. They describe the HCP minimal preprocessing pipeline and ConnectomeDB, and summarize early scientific applications of the openly shared data.
summaries.advanced: This overview paper describes the data resource that has become the de facto standard for human connectome science. The twin and sibling design enables heritability analysis of connectivity phenotypes, while the large unrelated sample supports genome-wide connectivity analyses. The HCP MMP1.0 parcellation (Glasser et al. 2016), functional gradients analysis (Margulies et al. 2016), and individual-level fingerprinting (Finn et al. 2015) all built directly on HCP data.
